posts.summary: Stancil argues that the January 6th attack and the GameStop saga reveal the same structural danger: social media enables fringe groups to reach critical mass quickly, turning online narratives into real-world destabilization. Because scale and speed are the product, even well-intentioned movements can spiral, leaving society on a runaway train with only two options—submit to the ride or dismantle the track.
money_quotes: All it takes is social media, at a global scale, operating as intended.
It turns out, to make the world wobble, a lot fewer people have to jump than we thought.
Off we’ll go, careening down an uncertain track on a runaway train captained by a rotating cast of hijackers—some who mean well, some who don’t, and most of whom just think it’s funny.
At this point, we have two choices: Give in to the ride, or dismantle the entire track.
Perhaps, just because we can build it, doesn’t mean we should.
posts.key_insight: The core risk isn’t misinformation or bad actors alone, but the scale of social media itself, which reliably converts fringe energy into real-world disruption.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the Clubhouse “slur” controversy shouldn’t be dismissed as mere performative tech-media drama, even if it looks like it. By comparing it to past journalism blowups, he suggests that Lorenz’s mistake can be read as a desperate outburst rooted in real, long-ignored harassment rather than a cynical hit job. The piece presses readers to examine the system that creates this rage instead of only condemning the person who erupts.
money_quotes: Maybe that easy cynicism is exactly the problem.
Maybe Lorenz’s tweet wasn’t a pantomimed punch that accidentally landed, but was a real outburst, born of furious despair.
I hate the dirty river I’m standing in, not the journalist and the college student who capsized in it.
Her lie wasn’t revenge or false narrative; it was desperate, dying rage.
If you’re going to question Lorenz’s motivation for tweeting the fiction that she did, you should also question your own motivation for ignoring the truth she’s been trying to tell you for so long.
posts.key_insight: The real story isn’t a single mistaken tweet but the long-ignored harassment that can drive people to desperate, attention-forcing outbursts.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that Paul Graham’s widely cited math on a startup wealth tax is not wrong so much as misleading, because it relies on extreme, compounding assumptions about growth rates, time horizons, and equity outcomes. Small tweaks to those assumptions swing the results wildly, revealing that spreadsheet models in this context mostly reflect prior policy beliefs rather than hard truths.
money_quotes: Graham’s number isn’t wrong—it’s irrelevant.
Exponential growth is extraordinarily sensitive to seemingly insignificant changes.
With such a range of possibilities available, anyone with an Excel spreadsheet can easily craft a story to fit their narrative.
These simple models are just a Rorschach test for pre-existing policy preferences.
By Graham’s own math, even with Warren’s wealth tax, a founder who puts in six years of work—the six years they earn above-market growth rates—will be worth more than $100 million for 230 years.
posts.key_insight: Wealth-tax impact estimates for startup founders hinge on extreme exponential-growth assumptions, so the headline numbers say more about the modeler’s priors than about the policy itself.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the missing wave of public self-flagellation after the Atlanta murders isn’t a sign of progress but evidence of how racism adapts to preserve itself. He suggests that when anti-Asian racism isn’t widely recognized as structural, there’s no safe “systemic” shield for apologies, so blame shifts and the caste system stays intact. The core point is that racism in America is flexible and self-protecting, and its contradictions make it stronger, not weaker.
money_quotes: The only constant is the outcome.
Racism in America isn’t defined by an ordered set of principles, but is shapeshifting and infinitely flexible, able to take whatever form is necessary to perpetuate it.
Its apparent inconsistencies don’t undermine it, but make it stronger.
You cannot take it apart with reason. You cannot pound the shape out of water.
Race and caste are not logic. They're an ugly, violent lie.
posts.key_insight: The absence of performative apologies after Atlanta reveals how racism’s power lies in its adaptability, not in consistent logic.

posts.summary: Stancil uses a simulation of Barry Bonds's 2004 pitches to show that Bonds could have nearly matched his historic on-base percentage without swinging, because pitchers avoided the strike zone. Extending the method league-wide suggests that, as zone rates have fallen, many hitters would actually improve their OBP by swinging less, implying modern pitching has made patience unusually valuable.
money_quotes: In Bois’ world, Bonds finishes with an on-base percentage of .608.
Bonds in isolation is breathtaking: He could’ve had the greatest OBP of all time without ever taking a single swing.
In other words, if we played the entire 2004 season without bats, the total number of baserunners would fall by about 10 percent, or two to three baserunners a game.
Swinging, it turns out, not only makes the average hitter worse.
If your swing looks like mine—am I taking this pitch or swinging? Why is my bat so far behind my hands? And what on earth is happening with my front leg?—try doing less.
posts.key_insight: As pitchers increasingly avoid the strike zone, the expected value of simply watching pitches rises so much that the average hitter can improve by swinging less.

posts.summary: Stancil recounts how, as a data analyst cofounding a startup with no product or data, he found himself without a clear role and started a blog to explore quirky data stories. Years later, he’s relaunching that impulse on Substack, committing to regular posts that mix data culture and tech commentary with his eclectic interests.
money_quotes: On August 27, I realized why data analysts like myself aren’t typically among the first employees at tech startups—you don’t have much to do.
A day in, I was a founder with a company, but without a job.
So, as I think the saying goes, those who can, do; those who can’t, start a blog.
Because Mode’s now well-manicured, professional company blog wasn’t the right place to rant about how social media is dooming society, this Substack was born.
Expect predictions about data technologies and observations about corporate data culture alongside the regularly scheduled commentary on Pitbull.
posts.key_insight: The Substack is a renewed, more regular outlet for Stancil’s mix of data-driven analysis and broader tech-cultural commentary, shaped by his experience building a data-focused company.

posts.summary: Benn Stancil argues that self-serve BI keeps failing because teams define it as a code-free version of analyst work, which is the wrong goal. Analysts create new measures to test hypotheses, while non-analysts mostly want to extract known metrics, so self-serve should be built around opinionated “metric extraction” rather than open-ended analysis.
money_quotes: Self-serve initiatives try to solve the wrong problem.
Analysts and non-analysts use data in structurally different ways.
Non-analysts work like journalists, collating existing metrics and drawing conclusions by considering them in their totality.
The more questions people can theoretically self-serve, the fewer they can practically self-serve.
Opinionated simplicity is better than indifferent optionality.
posts.key_insight: Self-serve BI should focus on constrained metric extraction, not on replicating analysts’ open-ended analytical work.

posts.summary: Benn argues that the data tooling industry is shifting from vertically integrated, all-in-one products to a horizontally integrated “stack” where each layer (ingestion, storage, transformation, consumption) is owned by specialized tools. This change is driven by the explosion of vendors and the need to blend data across functions, but it creates new challenges around integration and boundary discipline.
money_quotes: In response to these changes, companies are turning their stacks sideways.
One tool is responsible for each layer—each stage of the data supply chain—across the entire business.
Data teams are better off not trying to cut against the grain of this emerging architectural outline, and vendors are better off not encouraging them to do so.
Horizontally integrated companies, however, can’t operate in a vacuum; for this orientation to work, these companies have to work well with the rest of the supply chain.
When they do, however, it won’t be a single product.
posts.key_insight: The modern data stack is evolving toward horizontal specialization, which improves flexibility but makes interoperability and clear architectural boundaries the central challenges.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the modern data stack is missing a crucial component: a centralized metrics layer that defines and serves consistent metric logic across all tools. Without it, self-serve analytics devolves into fragmented, inconsistent calculations, and BI tools end up as isolated silos rather than shared infrastructure. A proper metrics layer would make metrics universally accessible via SQL, unify reporting with deeper analysis, and complete the stack’s foundation.
money_quotes: Except for one: a metrics layer.
Today’s current stack makes it easy to answer this question, but really hard to answer it consistently.
The core problem is that there’s no central repository for defining a metric.
A better architecture would do for metrics what dbt did for transformed data—make them globally accessible to every other tool in the data stack.

That, ultimately, is the biggest benefit of a metrics layer: It completes the foundation on which operational BI and exploratory data science both live, bringing these two functions together in ways they can’t be combined today.
posts.key_insight: A centralized, SQL-addressable metrics layer is the missing foundation needed to make self-serve analytics consistent across the modern data stack.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that if you want a Twitter profile picture that truly stands out, you should treat it as a color-contrast optimization problem rather than a flattering headshot. By sampling thousands of avatars and comparing dominant hues, he shows that extreme, edge-of-the-wheel colors maximize visual distinctiveness against both other profiles and Twitter’s UI.
money_quotes: In the middle of a sea of brown and beige heads, yellow stood out like a data scientist on a TV panel of seasoned political anchors.

Color science is complicated, and I don’t understand most of it. Figuring out which single color stands out the most in this rowdy mosaic requires a level of sophistication well beyond what my TV brain is capable of.
In other words, of all the colors on the visible spectrum, the best choice for my profile has to be among these eight.
While these colors are uncommon, they don’t necessarily stand out.
So green it is—scorching, unapologetic, and crackling new. Time to go get some likes.
posts.key_insight: A purely attention-optimizing Twitter avatar is best achieved by choosing a highly contrasting, extreme color rather than a conventional photo.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that Airbnb’s Minerva addresses the core problem in analytics: inconsistent metric definitions across teams and tools. He praises Minerva’s metrics layer and its integration with the data catalog for shifting discovery toward metric-centric, consumer-friendly views. His main critique is the API-first access, which makes adoption and SQL-centric analyst workflows harder than a warehouse-native approach.
money_quotes: We have the tools for governing tables, and we have tools for governing dashboards and analysis, but we’re still pretty sloppy about governing the space between the two.
Because we don’t manage metrics globally, people define and calculate them in different and often bespoke ways.
But most people aren’t analysts. They don’t think of data as a set of tables, columns, and relationships; they think in terms of metrics and charts.
Rather than the usual data exploration, it’s pure metric extraction.
In the end, more than any feature or technical detail, Minerva’s adoption is its headline.
posts.key_insight: A metrics layer solves a real governance gap, but it should integrate seamlessly with SQL-centric workflows and existing tools to be broadly adoptable.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that proximity doesn’t just humanize people—it biases us in their favor, often excusing real faults. He uses Paul Graham’s defense of Antonio García Martínez to show how personal closeness clouds judgment and leads to asymmetric scrutiny. The piece concludes that public accountability matters because elites tend to protect their own.
money_quotes: Closeness to someone doesn't make us know them better—it makes us take their side, for better and for worse.
For Martínez, an entire autobiography isn’t enough to know who he really is. For the Apple employees, a single signature is.
That isn’t logic; it’s just defending a friend.
If they’re left to judge one another, they’ll judge themselves as Graham did Martínez, or as Dianne Feinstein did Lindsay Graham, or as Sarah Silverman did Louis C.K.
And when that happens, we already know the verdict: I know them too well to hate.
posts.key_insight: Personal closeness often distorts judgment in favor of the powerful, making public accountability essential to counter elite self-protection.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that analytics is inherently creative and messy, not an objective, linear path to truth. Metrics are subjective constructs shaped by business context, so early analytical work should embrace exploration before converging on stable definitions. Attempts to prematurely impose structure or swap tools often mistake the natural mess of discovery for failure.
money_quotes: Data isn’t objective, and analysis isn’t structured. It’s just as creative—and just as messy—as the papers I ran away from in college.
There is no correct win rate waiting to be unearthed; one version isn’t true while another is false.

Our job as analysts isn’t to do the math right so that we can figure out which answer is in the back of the book; it’s to determine which version, out of a subjective set of options, helps us best run a business.
Often, the most useful things we find are the things we weren't looking for.

If you force too much structure on a creative exercise, you’ll end up like me when I did the same thing in my English literature class—with a disappointed professor, and the worst grade on your transcript.
posts.key_insight: Analytics work succeeds when teams accept that defining metrics is a creative, messy process and plan to refine it later rather than forcing premature structure.

posts.summary: The post argues that closing airplane window shades is a small but corrosive act of selfishness that traps everyone in a sterile, joyless cabin. Stancil frames the window as a hard-won, almost sacred portal to wonder, and implores the window-seat passenger to share that view rather than retreat into screens.
money_quotes: These are the unholy creatures who, upon finding their seat, take a couple glances at the bustling tarmac outside, and push the window shade shut, like a pedestrian waving away a Greenpeace canvasser.

To them, the tiny perforations in our barrel of steel and precious life aren't an engineering marvel, or a portal to the divine horizon through which were hurtling, or even a way to get a little bit of pleasing natural light in humming, sterile tube; no, they are irritants to be extinguished, distracting from the more important business on their screens.

Nonsense. Turn away from the unnecessary Powerpoint you're creating to excuse your unnecessary trip to Dallas, to help an unnecessary pharmaceutical company sell an unnecessary drug to make an unnecessary profit.

And now, at this privileged edge of our long anthropological timeline, at this culmination of millennia of patience and tragic trial and error, against the odds and the natural laws governing our universe, we can!
We beg of you: A magnificent world is out there, but in here, the darkness is devouring us, and we are transforming into monsters.
posts.key_insight: Closing the window shade symbolizes modern complacency with screens and convenience, squandering a rare, communal chance to experience awe.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that “self‑serve analytics is a mirage” has become a fashionable shibboleth, but the real issue is that analysts project their own needs onto business users. When you define self‑serve narrowly—mostly as consistent metric extraction—it becomes solvable, while deeper analysis still requires hiring more analytical thinkers.
money_quotes: Self-serve analytics, which promises to open up vast troves of “insight” by making data accessible to everyone at a company, doesn’t actually work.
The problem self-serve is meant to solve—that there are more questions than analysts, and we don’t have time to answer them all—is still a problem, even if self-serve is a lousy solution.
He showed me that my reflexive dismissal of self-serve was wrong because my definition of self-serve was wrong.

They want to choose from a list of understood KPIs, apply it to a filtered set of records, and aggregate it by a particular dimension. It's analytical Mad Libs—show me average order size for orders that used gift cards by month.
If you want to make great decisions with data, you...buy great self-serve tools? No, you hire great analytical thinkers.
posts.key_insight: Self‑serve fails when treated as analyst‑grade exploration, but works when scoped to standardized metric extraction, while truly complex questions still demand more analysts.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that despite increasing standardization in data stacks, reusable dashboard templates haven’t taken off because the most meaningful business data differences are intentional and central to how each company operates. Even when tooling and schemas align, companies define success differently, making one-size-fits-all reporting impractical; at best, templates might work in narrow, rigid domains if they ask for meaning-first inputs.
money_quotes: The bespoke elements of our Salesforce data that distinguish it from that of other B2B SaaS businesses aren’t analytical blemishes to be polished off of a template that just sums the “amount” field on every Salesforce opportunity.
In other words, the shared foundation that templates are supposed to be built on is often only name deep.
A daily active user template is useless if everyone defines both activity and users differently.
Despite being nearly indistinguishable services, Teams and Slack almost certainly measure their success in very different ways.
My templates, like every effort before them, didn’t take off.
posts.key_insight: Standardized tools don’t yield standardized meaning, so templates fail because the differences that matter most are the ones businesses intentionally encode in their data and metrics.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that strict, uniform SQL formatting rules are less important than making a query’s logic legible to humans. Formatting should adapt to the structure and emphasis of the logic, even if that breaks conventional style guides or what a formatter can automate.
money_quotes: What’s the best way to format these parts of a query? The answer—an answer every analyst should love, if not many priests—is that it depends.
Queries—which, above all, transcribe vague business concepts, like revenue and win rate, into precise formulae—should also make their logic legible.
Formatting is our medium, and the medium is the message.
As query authors, consistency shouldn’t be our goal, no more than we should make it our goal to consistently use bar charts.
Rules, even the best documented and most hallowed ones, are sometimes best ignored.
posts.key_insight: Effective SQL formatting is not about rigid rules but about highlighting the logic that matters, even if that means breaking conventions.

posts.summary: Benn argues that analogies aren’t obfuscation but a clarifying tool because they force us to simplify a complex idea into its essential structure before comparing it to something else. That simplification exposes the logical core and prevents us from hiding behind jargon or unnecessary complexity. He links this directly to analytics work, where the hardest task is distilling signal from noise and telling a clear, memorable story.
money_quotes: Though analogies may seem like misdirection, I believe they’re actually clarifying—even when they don’t seem to make sense.
To create an analogy, you have to pull out the essential bones of an idea, and map them onto the bones of another idea.
When we use simple models, we can’t hide behind complexity or unnecessary details, either intentionally or inadvertently, to obscure our argument's logical core.

Phrases like "solutioning" and "value-add" are an argumentative sleight of hand: They create artificial complexity, and let us appear to make a point without ever having to actually say what that point is.
To think this way—to create limited models to uncover larger truths—is to think like both an analyst and an analogist.
posts.key_insight: Analogies work because the act of simplifying a complex idea into its essential structure is what clarifies both analysis and communication.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that analytics teams overinvest in dashboards and BI systems that produce fleeting value while neglecting the ad hoc analyses that drive the most important decisions. The real problem is that these analyses are ungoverned, hard to find later, and treated as disposable. He proposes reframing and organizing this work—starting by renaming it—to make it central, durable, and systematized.
money_quotes: We invest in the ephemeral, and throw away the enduring.
No amount of “reimagining the deck chair” will keep our Titanic afloat.
By naming it something temporary, we make it temporary.
A system that organizes some results and loses others isn’t organized; ungoverned work anywhere undermines governance everywhere.
So, by all means, let’s stop building dashboards. But first, to convince everyone—including ourselves—of the value of what we should be building instead, let’s come up with some better technobabble.
posts.key_insight: Dashboards are a seductive but low‑durability investment; the real value comes from ad hoc analysis, which needs to be renamed, systematized, and preserved to build lasting organizational knowledge.

posts.summary: Stancil analyzes 12 years of NBA games to answer how fans should feel in close games at halftime. The data shows that when the score is tied or within one point, teams that played poorly in the first half are actually more likely to win, indicating mean reversion beats momentum.
money_quotes: In close games, a poorly performing team wins 57 percent of the time; a well performing team wins only 43 percent of the time.
In other words, mean reversion overpowers momentum (if momentum exists at all).
Teams that perform great in the first half are just as likely to perform badly in the second half as they are to perform great again.
If your team is in a tight game, the worse they’ve played, the better you should feel.
posts.key_insight: In tight games, poor first-half performance is a good sign because teams tend to revert toward their average in the second half.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that “self-serve analytics” isn’t defined by features or who/what answers questions, but by how the experience feels to the people using and providing it. Because trust, comfort, and skill differ by user and context, the same tool can feel self-serve to some and not to others, so teams should design for the experience that makes their users feel at home rather than chase a canonical feature checklist.
money_quotes: Defining self-serve isn’t about knowing when we see it, but knowing when we feel it.
The experience is what matters, not the functionality.
If they don’t like the experience or don’t trust the answer it gives them—regardless of how much the tool meets some abstract definition of self-serve—they’ll just ask someone for help.
That’s why it’s dangerous to define business users as a vague, homogenous unit.
So often, as data teams, we chase the self-serve experience that we think we’re supposed to build.
posts.key_insight: Self-serve analytics succeeds when users feel comfortable and trust the experience, not when a tool checks a feature list.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that analytics is being miscast as a primarily technical, engineering-adjacent discipline, which both misidentifies what makes great analysts and excludes strong candidates who lack conventional technical credentials. He says the real differentiator is curiosity, judgment, and communication, and that the emerging analytics engineer role could either enable this distinction or blur it further.
money_quotes: Analytics isn’t primarily technical.
While technical skills are useful, they’re not what separate average analysts from great ones.
Given the choice, I’d hire Sherlock Holmes—the antiquated Sherlock Holmes, from the 19th century, a man for whom “technology” means the light bulb—over any present-day MIT engineering PhD.
By describing analysts as engineers, we’re telling people we want them to solve Jamie’s problems, and then expect them to solve Erik’s—because Erik’s are the problems we really have.
Rather than fighting this claim, we should demand that others judge us against a different yardstick.
posts.key_insight: Analytics should be judged by curiosity, reasoning, and communication rather than by engineering credentials, or the field will keep excluding many of its best potential practitioners.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that analytics is often seen as undervalued not just because of mislabeling or bad tooling, but because the work itself is genuinely hard and average results can be underwhelming. The field’s value depends on exceptional analysts, yet most teams can’t consistently produce that level of impact, so organizations may be reacting rationally. He suggests improving selection and cultivation—hiring for the right mix of skills and training the squishier parts of the job—to close the gap.
money_quotes: We analysts, I fear, are honeydews.
The average engineer, by contrast, can regularly deliver more valuable results—the consistent cantaloupe to our erratic honeydew.
But better frameworks may not be enough.
Perhaps they are seeing it, and we’re the ones who are confused.
posts.key_insight: Analytics is hard to do well at scale, so boosting its perceived value requires better hiring and training, not just better tools or organizational sympathy.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the modern data stack is carved up by an implicit technical vs. non-technical divide, which is a false boundary imposed on a continuous spectrum of work. This gerrymandering distorts product design—especially in analytics consumption—by forcing tools into narrow buckets instead of enabling a unified experience across users and use cases.
money_quotes: The artificial boundaries we define eventually come to define us.
The line between BI reporting and analytical research isn’t a hard line; it’s a fluid, shifting continuum.

Like a city divided by a poorly drawn political boundary, our method for consuming data is split by an arbitrary technical line that makes it extraordinarily difficult to cross a border that we frequently need to cross.
The modern data stack doesn’t need a BI bucket and a data science bucket; it needs a unified consumption layer.
The sooner we see the space as a spectrum, and not a thousand discrete colors, the sooner we can build a better experience for everyone.
posts.key_insight: Treating data work as a technical/non-technical dichotomy gerrymanders the stack and blocks the unified, spectrum-based tools teams actually need.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that while the modern data stack has delivered dramatic technical advances, it has failed to produce a coherent, reliable experience for everyday analysts and business users. The core challenge is not more tools or abstract “culture,” but designing a modern data experience that integrates workflows, trust, and collaboration across a decentralized ecosystem.
money_quotes: Until we can serve them that, our technology, no matter how revolutionary, is academic.
Fault lines between products become fault lines in how it feels to use the modern data stack—and for most people, that’s what matters most.
Data should help people do their jobs, rather than add a new job for them to do.
Indicators of trust need to be explicit: Every data asset should show if upstream processes are operating abnormally, out-of-date, or in some state of development or disrepair.
Without it—without thinking about the experience that pairs with the stack—we can build the tools, and the things we’ve promised... won’t come.
posts.key_insight: The modern data stack’s technical progress won’t deliver its promised value until the ecosystem aligns around a cohesive, user-centered data experience.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that creative work has a generative phase that cannot be scheduled the way editorial work can, and meetings are especially destructive to that fragile state. By treating meetings as fixed and creativity as the flexible block, organizations stretch creative tasks into slow, demoralizing slogs. He proposes rebalancing calendars to allow people to decline meetings when in generative flow, even in data roles.
money_quotes: Inspiration will visit you when she chooses.
Meetings are catastrophic when you’re searching for this spark.
By making meetings the fixtures in our days, we have to schedule time to be creative.
Nothing lowers your standards more than trying to wring results from a dry stone.
Surely we can do better than crucifying creativity on the calendar's cross.
posts.key_insight: Treating meetings as immovable and creativity as schedulable turns generative work into a slow, demoralizing grind, so calendars should flex to protect creative flow.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the analytics ecosystem’s core problem isn’t just “too many tools” but the lack of an operating system that standardizes how tools integrate and behave. He critiques extreme centralization and decentralization (including the data mesh as commonly defined) and proposes a delegated, OS-like layer—potentially embodied by dbt—that abstracts heterogenous data sources and provides shared utilities for the stack.
money_quotes: The data ecosystem functions quite differently. Buy a new tool, and you’ve got to shove others aside, jerry-rig some tenuous connections to the rest of the stack, and hope that the whole haphazard contraption survives long enough for you to band-aid it with the next product you stuff into it.
In many ways, this is the price of success.
This isn’t a decentralized architecture; it’s a delegated one.
What if, rather than acting as a simple switchboard, the mesh provided common utilities for interacting with the data underneath it?
In my view, this is the most pressing problem the analytics industry faces today: How do we create, in a disparate ecosystem, a functional modern data experience?
posts.key_insight: The analytics stack needs an OS-like layer that standardizes integrations and utilities across tools, and a delegated model like an expanded dbt could provide that foundation better than pure centralization or decentralization.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that just as the Supreme Court’s rulings are shaped by human preferences and political context, data-driven arguments are similarly tilted by subjective choices and prior beliefs. Data feels like neutral ground, but the metrics and analyses we treat as objective are built from arbitrary definitions, incentives, and power—so numbers often mask opinions rather than replace them. The remedy isn’t more data, but more humility about our limits and more skepticism toward quantitative authority.
money_quotes: Data is not the level ground that we act like it is.
In short, everything can be tilted: the data, the analysis, and the people analyzing it.
Data is both a sword and a shield: It is a weapon for prosecuting your point, and a defense for protecting yourself as reasonable and impartial.

The best quantitative illusionists don’t trim y-axes or talk in absolutes rather than ratios; they use data to hide their opinions behind sober analyses, pointing to their inside voices as proof of their neutrality.
We aren’t inherently better or more unbiased than those who argue from emotion and experience; we simply take a different tact.
posts.key_insight: Data-driven argumentation is often treated as objective, but it is built on subjective choices and can be wielded to launder opinions as neutrality.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that BI, like early software, has been unbundled and stripped of legacy connotations, leaving today’s BI as a diminished fragment focused on visualization and reporting. He contends the next generation of BI should be a universal consumption layer that integrates self-serve and deep analysis, sits atop shared governance, and centers collaboration between analysts and non-analysts.
money_quotes: It’s as though we took our definition of BI from twenty years ago and started crossing off clauses, until we’re left with “visualization and reporting.”
BI tools should aspire to do one thing, and do it completely: They should be the universal tool for people to consume and make sense of data.
Eventually, a semantic layer will be a bug, not a feature.
By defining BI as just self-serve, we shortcut what it could be, no matter how good that self-serve is.
The old version of BI—a complete end-to-end stack, responsible for nearly all of a company’s analytical needs—is already dead.
posts.key_insight: BI should evolve from a fragmented, report-centric tool into a universal data-consumption platform that unifies self-serve and deep analysis while relying on shared governance layers.

posts.summary: The post argues that the analytics community has built a reputation for being welcoming, but that welcome is not shared equally, with Black professionals profoundly underrepresented in conferences and the field. The author contends that diversity should be pursued as a moral imperative, not a business case, and that the community’s blind spots risk embedding and amplifying racism through data-driven systems.
money_quotes: This shows the limits of self-perceived kindness and progressivism.
They turn systemic racism into systematic racism, encoded and executed automatically, relentlessly, at scale.
A culture of congeniality can be just as ruthlessly biased as a toxic one, while also being better disguising its closed doors.

Unless the community becomes more visibly diverse, “data literacy” could become one of racism’s most powerful professional variants—and the analytics community could, even inadvertently, be one of its most forceful accelerants.
The data industry has the money to do more. It remains to be seen if it has the commitment.
posts.key_insight: A community can be outwardly kind yet structurally exclusionary, and in data this exclusion risks hard-coding racial bias into systems and careers.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that BI and visualization are far more complex than feature backlogs suggest, so “thin” consumption apps coordinated by a data OS are unlikely to deliver a coherent user experience. He proposes a middle path: a strong, general-purpose BI “planet” for exploration, surrounded by specialized satellites for specific use cases.
money_quotes: Visualizations are the ultimate cookie.
We should ship an experience that mirrors what people want to do, not one that mirrors the org charts of the teams helping them do it.
For this reason, I don’t think it’s feasible to make BI thin.
Visualization on its own is a very heavy piece of technology, and that’s only a fraction of what BI tools need to do.
We need a global exploration tool that works for most general uses, surrounded by satellites that solve specific problems really well.
posts.key_insight: Because visualization and BI are inherently complex experience layers, a single general-purpose “planet” tool with specialized satellites is more realistic than a fully decentralized ecosystem of thin apps.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the public Facebook leak revealed something rare: not just debate about social media, but about the quality of analysis itself. Because most analysis is proprietary, the field obsesses over tools and structures while the actual work stays invisible, making it hard to define or improve what “good analysis” looks like. He concludes that quality is subjective and multi-dimensional, and the way forward is to expose more real analysis so standards can evolve.
money_quotes: In the middle of the analytical field, in the eye of our swirling hurricane about titles and team structures and tooling, is analysis itself.
The analysis we produce is still only in our peripheral vision, at the center a black hole: unknowable on its own, only detectable by its effects.

To extend Randy Au’s woodworking analogy, the veil around analytical work forces us to talk about the saws we prefer, the types of wood we like, and the paths we can take through our apprentice program without talking about the actual chairs we build.
It feels to me, then, that our actual answer is an analytical classic: It depends.

Unless we see their work—unless we figure out how to talk about what’s currently hidden behind NDAs instead of talking about teams and languages and tools and tools and tools and tools and tools—we’ll never know how high the bar could be set.
posts.key_insight: Good analysis can’t be reduced to a single yardstick, and without public examples of real work, the field can’t reliably raise its standards.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the Looker–Tableau integration signals a structural split in BI: governance/modeling (LookML) is separating from visualization and exploration. This shift redefines BI away from all-in-one suites toward a metrics/governance layer paired with best-in-class exploration tools, with big implications for vendor consolidation and the economics of the modern data stack.
money_quotes: Looker’s crown jewel is LookML; Tableau’s is visualization.
Major BI tools are now also recognizing the combination of governance and consumption can’t hold.
In other words, BI as we know it is dead. BI as global exploration is ascendent.
The list of what we need in a modern data stack... is getting long.
By choosing Tableau, Google’s conceding, at least for the time being, that their data stack ends with LookML’s metrics layer.
posts.key_insight: The Looker–Tableau integration marks a decisive unbundling of BI, with governance/metrics becoming a separate layer and exploration tools defining the new BI landscape.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that consumer products like Yelp made data-driven decisions feel natural by embedding data inside the experience, while corporate analytics isolates data in dashboards divorced from work. The future of operational analytics is to integrate focused, disciplined data experiences directly into workflows and build a data “operating system” that enables those experiences rather than just more BI tooling.
money_quotes: We just did it, all of us, on our own.

These services don’t attempt to make you “data-driven.” Instead, they focus on a bigger problem—choosing a restaurant, a means of transit, and a reservation booking—and integrate data alongside other features, like phone numbers, pictures of food, and links to menu, that help you address it.
Dashboards are the opposite. They’re often data, looking for a problem.
Our ambition shouldn’t be to make decisions automatically.
The future is an experience, with data integrated into and inseparable from how we do our jobs.
posts.key_insight: Operational analytics will only become ubiquitous when data is embedded seamlessly into the tools and decisions people already use, not isolated in standalone dashboards.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the modern data stack’s fragmentation hides compounding costs: each new tool and higher-fidelity use case triggers cascading usage fees across vendors. Because data tooling is mostly services-oriented and priced inconsistently, the ecosystem is economically unstable unless it consolidates or builds better coordinating layers. The likely outcome is fewer dominant platforms with satellite products and stronger cost management.
money_quotes: In its normal operation, costs are intertwined and opaque.
Each category makes the data stack more technically robust—and more economically tenuous.
But the analogy is fatally flawed.
The demand for these tools is there, and more is coming.
To make this whole thing work, we don’t need less mass in the solar system; we just need more organization.
posts.key_insight: The modern data stack’s economic problem isn’t demand but the fractured, opaque, usage-amplifying supply that makes costs balloon unless the market consolidates or coordinates.

posts.summary: The post argues that analytics lacks a reliable way to measure itself, leading to circular judgments, bias, and poor incentives. Benn proposes a concrete metric: evaluate analysts by how quickly their work helps others make decisions, because that directly reflects the purpose of analysis and improves communication, focus, and reputation over time.
money_quotes: Analysts should judge their work by how quickly people make decisions with it.
The moment an analyst is asked a question, a timer starts.
That's the only metric we should measure ourselves on--straight up, without qualification or caveat.
This paradox--that analysis can only be measured in circular ways--has real effects that go beyond stressing out armchair philosophers with Substacks.
And that journey starts with a simple step: Ignore all the ambiguity around measuring analytical quality and ROI, and do whatever it takes to make others more decisive.
posts.key_insight: The best way to evaluate analytical work is the speed with which it enables sound decisions.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that data and analysis are often used not to clarify decisions but to escape them, letting leaders off the hook when choices are hard and evidence is inconclusive. He contends that real leadership requires courage to decide and own outcomes when the data cannot provide certainty, and analysts must recognize when their role is to inform rather than to defer responsibility.
money_quotes: Decision makers, from CEOs to the person on the couch with the remote, also use data to serve a more subtle purpose: As a substitute for courage.
Analysis like this, that’s chasing a conclusive result for its own sake, is a cover for surreptitiously handing decisions over to chance.
Leaders aren’t promoted or elected because they’re better at running cost-benefit analyses than everyone else.
Not every problem is quantifiable; not every choice will have a clear consensus; not every decision can be data-driven.
Without that courage, we’re just clever puppets, dancing to whatever tune our data sings to us, hoping nobody sees the wires we’re submitting to.
posts.key_insight: Data should inform decisions, but when evidence is inconclusive, leaders must choose with conviction rather than hide behind analysis.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that analytics leaders get stuck on a career treadmill just below the C-suite because the chief data/analytics role is defined as an operational, second-class position. He proposes splitting the function like engineering does: keep a VP of data for management and create a chief analytics officer who sits with executives to do strategic, high-leverage analysis. This would improve executive decision-making while giving strong analysts a real top-level career path.
money_quotes: The terminal point of an analyst’s career, it seems, is the data leader treadmill, hopping between different VP roles, always running but never moving forward.
There is no role committed to this work, and no title that acknowledges its value.
Data departments should follow the same pattern.
Adding a quiet observer to the executive team is a small price to pay for consistently putting the full force of the company’s best analysts behind its most pressing problems.
What I’ve been looking for, it turns out, may have been here the whole time. I just needed to rewrite it to make it my own.
posts.key_insight: The analytics function needs a true executive-level analyst role—peer to the CTO—to unlock strategic impact and provide a viable top-tier career path for analysts.

posts.summary: Stancil argues, half-seriously and with mock rigor, that the name “coffee cake” is a linguistic fraud because the cake contains no coffee. He marshals examples and a pseudo-statistical survey of cake names to claim the exception is absurd and should be corrected by renaming it “coffree cake.”
money_quotes: My argument is simple: It’s called coffee cake.

Coffee cake, I’m told, is a reasonable exception because you eat coffee cake with coffee—suggesting, I suppose, that potato salad is a salad eaten with potatoes, or espresso martinis are regular martinis for chasing your morning coffee, or maple syrup is a sugary topping for trees.
Of these 62, only 8 are missing the ingredient in their name. One is coffee cake.
Coffee and walnut cake, which is made with walnuts and coffee. But coffee cake, without the walnuts? Only a fool would believe it has coffee.
It's time to call it what it is: coffree cake.
posts.key_insight: The post uses mock data analysis and linguistic pedantry to argue that “coffee cake” is a misleading exception that should be renamed.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that dashboards and reporting aren’t distractions from “real” analytics work; they are how companies construct a shared reality in an abstract, non-physical world. Metrics don’t just measure the business—they define it—so consistent dashboards are foundational for trust and decision-making.
money_quotes: Data and metrics—revenues, retention rates, product usage patterns—don’t measure a company’s world; they are its world.
The code is the concept, and the concept is the code.
Every dashboard is a window into a Pixar scene, and every metric is a rendering of it.
Looking at two dashboards that don’t match is like looking out two adjacent windows and not seeing the same thing.
Building consistent dashboards is hard.
posts.key_insight: Dashboards are essential because they create the consistent, shared reality that companies need to operate in an abstract world.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the early-2010s tech culture he experienced was not a temporary excess but a symptom of deeper structural incentives that breed toxicity. In contrast, the analytics community—exemplified by dbt’s Slack and Coalesce—has built a healthier, more inclusive culture, proving that tech’s ambition and impact can be separated from its worst behaviors. He concludes that software should learn from analytics by exporting this cultural model outward into the broader industry.
money_quotes: The analytics community’s success is proof that, even if software eats the world, software culture doesn’t have to.
It’s proof that the good things about the tech industry—the ambition, the impact, the financial opportunity—aren’t the causes of the bad.

Among all the characters in the community—the jesters, the cheerleaders, the philosophers and deep thinkers, the educators, the entertainers, the eager learners—one is conspicuously missing: the arrogant jerks.
Given how much the data industry tries to learn from software engineering, maybe it’s time to go the other direction.
There are a lot of dark spots in tech, and what a light, my lord, is needed to conquer so mighty a darkness.
posts.key_insight: The analytics community demonstrates that tech’s cultural toxicity is not inevitable, and its healthier norms should be exported back into the broader software industry.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that modern data observability catches structural and statistical issues but misses the most dangerous failures: semantic anomalies where data is technically valid yet conceptually wrong. These errors evade alerts, erode trust, and can drive major business mistakes, so teams need tools and practices that directly encode business meaning and keep humans in the loop.
money_quotes: Semantically anomalous data is structured correctly—it has the right schema, strings are indeed strings—and is statistically uninteresting—it’s an acceptable number of records, and doesn’t include a row for a transaction of negative eighty trillion dollars—but is still wrong.
It’s data that, on the trip from measurement to meaning, got lost in translation.

For people who want to know if they can trust the data they’re seeing—for people who want to know their data is reliable—the distinction between a broken pipeline and a misreported metric is irrelevant.
No observability tool, from the nascent ones in the market today to some future sentient AI, is a substitute for the data team knowing what the marketing team is doing.
Semantic anomalies are mistakes. They’re human errors, the things we should’ve caught and didn’t.
posts.key_insight: The biggest risks in analytics come from semantic errors—data that looks fine but means the wrong thing—so observability must evolve to encode business meaning and reinforce human context, not just monitor pipelines.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that blogging should be treated less like Twitter’s contrarian take economy and more like TikTok’s remixable, story-driven culture, where familiar ideas can be retold with fresh perspective. He shares a candid, iterative writing process—freeform conversation first, structure later—to help hesitant writers start and keep momentum, emphasizing that authenticity and enjoyment matter more than polish.
money_quotes: The magic of TikTok’s medium is that it’s built around stories, not takes.
Rather than chase new ideas, or feel the pressure to make everything original, I try to accept that everything’s already been said.
Posts begat conversations, conversations begat ideas, and ideas begat posts.
Critically, I write this in full paragraphs and not bullets. No bullets; never bullets.
Edit, but don’t sterilize.
posts.key_insight: Sustainable, enjoyable writing comes from conversational drafting and remixing familiar ideas rather than chasing originality or rigid outlines.

posts.summary: Benn argues that today’s warehouse-centered modern data stack can’t really support a thriving ecosystem of data apps, and reverse ETL is a brittle stopgap rather than a true platform. He proposes an “entity layer”—a generalized, interoperable semantic layer for business objects—as the missing bridge that could make data apps reliable and eventually turn the warehouse into an operational brain.
money_quotes: The modern data stack—and more specifically, the centralized data warehouse—is a good foundation; data apps are a cool idea.
But as platforms for backing data apps, they’re a fudge.
Entities are semantic objects first, and tables second.
A better semantic layer would be universally accessible, interoperable with other means of querying the underlying data, and optional.
And the data warehouse finally evolves from being a dumping ground for organizational data into being its centralized operational brain, the production database for a company.
posts.key_insight: An entity layer that standardizes business objects—beyond metrics—could be the missing abstraction that turns the warehouse into a true platform for data apps.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that data startups overhype “open standards” as the modern equivalent of network effects, but most so‑called standards are fragile and transient. Durable success usually comes from building useful products and brands, not from winning imaginary winner‑take‑all markets. He proposes a practical, paid “switchboard” for metadata plumbing rather than another aspirational standard.
money_quotes: Standards that are only standards until a better idea comes along aren’t really standards at all.
Network effects often make for better funding pitches than actual flywheels.
The rest is fable and folklore, the conflation of social proof and a high NPS with something much more mythical.
Open standards could solve this problem, if everyone consolidated around that standard. A snowball’s chance, as they say.
Just cold, hard product, sold for cold, hard cash.
posts.key_insight: In the data stack, chasing open standards is mostly a pitch narrative; real value and money come from pragmatic products that solve integration plumbing without relying on network effects.

posts.summary: Stancil uses a dataset of Wordle scores shared on Twitter to show that most players win easily and that "hard mode"—reusing known greens—actually performs better than more exploratory strategies. He then pivots to the bigger lesson: data analysis is shaped by the questions we choose and the paths we ignore, so truth-telling requires acknowledging those biases.
money_quotes: The overwhelming conclusion that they tell us is this: You shouldn’t lose.
Evidently, playing strategically is foolish, and playing on hard mode is easier.
Wordle is Wordle, not Five-random-letters-dle.
The final product was a child of these choices.
Nobody goes digging looking for dirt.
posts.key_insight: Data can illuminate optimal play in Wordle, but the deeper takeaway is that analysis is never fully objective because the questions we ask—and skip—shape the truths we tell.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that data teams aren’t service organizations, but they face similar pressures and can learn from support teams’ habits. By treating stakeholders’ perceptions as the reality to understand, prioritizing empathy over cleverness, and valuing maintenance, data teams can be more effective and resilient. The core shift is from consultant-as-expert to detective-and-therapist who helps people feel heard and move forward.
money_quotes: Production is what’s in people’s heads.
We should instead say, “that’s interesting; tell me more.”
On a support queue, however, nobody cares how smart you are.
CSAT scores, imperfect as they are, measured what we cared about most—if people were actually happy.
Our backlogs are inevitable, and a service mindset can turn those requests into routines; they can become weekly work that isn’t an ante to do something better, but a healthy habit to be celebrated.
posts.key_insight: Data teams do their best work when they adopt a service mindset: listen deeply, prioritize stakeholder reality, and take pride in ongoing maintenance rather than only big projects.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that while the back of the modern data stack has converged on clear architectural norms, the consumption layer (analytics/BI) is fragmented and unsettled. This diversity reflects real differences in people and business use cases, yet the industry still lacks shared standards and is muddied by vendor incentives. He expects eventual consolidation, driven by community consensus or economic gravity, even if innovation thrives in today’s chaos.
money_quotes: But the front quarter of the ship—analytics and BI tools for data consumption—the front is a very different story.
There’s barely a shared understanding of what companies should be doing with the data they have, much less how they should do it.
Helping people use data effectively is the hard problem.
This makes the conversation about the consumption layer a campaign, and proxies opinions about architectures through specific products.
Technology trends are often cyclical, swinging back and forth from centralized and decentralized, from bundled and unbundled.
posts.key_insight: The analytics consumption layer is fragmented because human and business needs vary, but it will likely consolidate toward shared standards as economic and community forces reassert order.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that Excel is software’s greatest achievement because it turned hundreds of millions of non-programmers into programmers by making simple automation accessible. He proposes a more general “Excel for everything” that lowers the prerequisites of scripting, letting people automate any task through a block-based, spreadsheet-like interface, and reframing programming as a universal tool rather than a career identity.
money_quotes: In my view, Excel is an unqualified triumph, for one very particular reason: It’s given more than a billion people direct access to the profound power of computers.
This efficiency disguises what Excel really is: An interface for writing programs.
Which raises an obvious question: Could there be an Excel for generic scripting?
The goal, in other words, should be to make each block small enough that it could be copied from Stack Overflow.
Programming is simply a tool, applicable to everything from planning a party to blogging, and to every career from recruiter to lawyer.
posts.key_insight: Excel’s genius is its interface for simple code, and a similarly accessible, block-based scripting tool could democratize automation far beyond spreadsheets.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that undisclosed angel investing creates hidden conflicts of interest in the data community, distorting discourse and reinforcing insider cliques. The same transparency norms applied to vendors should apply to investors, because credibility in a tight-knit ecosystem depends on openly disclosing financial ties.
money_quotes: Without more transparency into who’s financially aligned with whom, this risks something that should be bigger than any individual’s portfolio: the health of the community.
We’re invisible thumbs on scale, gently tiling honest conversations in our financial favor.
As soon as these same groups start to invest in one another, it becomes impossible to untangle partnerships from friendships, customers from investors, and spectators from shareholders.
As the data ecosystem grows, kind words and thoughtful guidelines alone won’t keep it healthy or make it inclusive.
Disclose your investments.
posts.key_insight: The data community’s trust depends on treating angel investing like any other conflict of interest and disclosing it openly.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that in moments of clear moral crisis, the pressure to add commentary or analysis is misplaced; sometimes the right response is to acknowledge the obvious and refrain from performative insight. He emphasizes that numbers, clever takes, or detached cynicism aren’t required, and it’s acceptable to feel helpless, speak plainly, or stay quiet.
money_quotes: For data people, it's tempting to treat numbers like a universal cipher that grants you the privilege to weigh in on any issue, if only you show your cute little proof.

The lesson I’m trying to learn, while watching a confusing war on a distant shore, is that it’s ok to be at a loss for clever words (a lesson that, given the paradoxical existence of this post, I’ve yet to fully embrace).

It’s ok to simply reaffirm the obvious—that, while, most conflicts are complex and nuanced, this one is not; there is nothing to add up; there are good guys and bad guys, and this is a war of unhinged aggression.
It’s ok to drop the facade of cool detachment and jaded cynicism, and share how you feel—anxious, stunned, helpless, and indiscriminately angry.
And beyond that, it’s ok to watch, to listen, and to have no other numbers or opinions to add.
posts.key_insight: In the face of clear-cut moral horror, the most honest response may be to admit there’s nothing clever to add and to speak (or stay silent) plainly.

posts.summary: Stancil argues the real shift in the data world isn’t bundling vs. unbundling of tools, but the blurring of roles between analysts and everyone else. Based on Mode’s usage, analytical behavior spreads across functions and deepens over time, making analysis a team sport. The implication is that “modern data experience” should serve a future where many more people want to work like analysts, not just unify tooling.
money_quotes: Analysis, even SQL-based analysis, isn’t like design, where a handful of people create stuff and everyone else is a consumer, with clear lines between them.
Users weren’t bimodal like we expected, but continuous.
Analysis is becoming a team sport.

As far as paradigm altering shifts go, this one—analysis becoming multi-functional; the democratization of analytical reasoning and technical talent, not just data—is far more foundational than whatever reshuffling happens among the data stack’s middleware.
The reason to build a “modern data experience” isn’t to unify the disjointed products of a bunch of startups; it’s to serve a world in which far more people want to work like analysts.
posts.key_insight: The crucial change in data work is the fading divide between analysts and everyone else, so tools should be built for broadly shared analytical behavior rather than narrow specialist roles.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that Snowflake’s acquisition of Streamlit is the start of a long-term shift toward a true data app platform, not just another top-of-stack SaaS play. He contends that a Streamlit framework plus a Snowflake-backed app marketplace could make data apps cheaper to build and sell, catalyzing a broad ecosystem of small, specialized tools built directly on the data layer.
money_quotes: Acquisitions don’t create revolutions; they capture those that have already happened, and repackage them into a limited enterprise offering.
Streamlit will never step foot in Gartner's data apps magic quadrant, or, I suspect, ever even attempt to bring a traditional product to market.

Without these tools, building data apps today is akin to building web applications before frameworks like Rails—every application has to solve low-level problems like, “How do I interact with a database?”
The combination of these two things—a framework for data app development, and a marketplace for apps that Snowflake stands behind—closes nearly all the gaps in Martin’s vision.
It won’t completely reshape the data landscape—avalanches level trees, not mountains—because we’ll still need core categories like centralized warehouses, ETL pipelines, and robust analytics tools.
posts.key_insight: Snowflake’s Streamlit deal is less about a product and more about seeding a platform-plus-marketplace that could make data apps scalable to build, distribute, and monetize.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that while revenue eventually matters, early-stage startups should resist making it their defining KPI, especially during market downturns. Using revenue as the main yardstick distorts incentives and alignment; better, durable metrics help teams focus on long-term value creation rather than short-term extraction. Data teams, in particular, should champion alternative north stars that are singular, reachable, and enduring.
money_quotes: Revenue is a one-way door—once you use it as the scale with which you weigh yourself, you never step off.
Any chart that’s sufficiently up and to the right can tell a compelling narrative, and fund a business to keep building.
In practice, however, they’re often lousy at both.
A simple metric presented with conviction is far better than a detailed one presented with precision.
For as long as you can, make your answer something other than revenue.
posts.key_insight: Revenue is inevitable, but making it the primary KPI too early warps incentives and focus, so startups—especially data teams—should elevate clearer, longer-term metrics as their north star.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that OLAP cubes persist as a conceptual “ghost” in modern BI tools: even when the infrastructure no longer requires cubes, interfaces still expose confusing, cube-like abstractions. He contends that BI should hide internal data structure and instead present data in the simple forms users actually think in—metrics and flat tables—so tools are understandable without implicit knowledge.
money_quotes: OLAP cubes are just tables, but tables structured in a very particular way.
But there’s another, much less discussed downside to OLAP cubes: They’re very difficult to understand.
Rather than displaying data as we model it, we should display data as we use it.
Most BI tools look like the Census site.
After three decades of trying with OLAP cubes, it’s time to give up the ghost.
posts.key_insight: Even as OLAP cubes fade technically, BI tools still mirror their confusing abstractions, so we should redesign interfaces around how users ask questions—metrics and flat tables—rather than how data is modeled.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that writers and data professionals inevitably tailor what they say to the audience they want to impress, often defaulting to an insular circle of industry peers and influencers. This inward focus distorts priorities in the data community—conference talk, tool debates, and discourse drift toward self-referential validation rather than serving end users. The remedy is to deliberately broaden the audience we write and speak for, seeking respect from people outside the data bubble.
money_quotes: We have to bring other people back into our conversations.
The point I’d hoped to make was that it’s not the content of what we talk about that matters, but who we make that content for.
We can build companies, careers, and reputations on each other alone.

For people who talk on the internet, our religion is the opinions of other people who talk on the internet—particularly those who are more successful, who make more money, who are more widely followed, or who simply talk and write better than we do.
The solution, then, is both easy and incredibly difficult: We have to bring other people back into our conversations.
posts.key_insight: The real problem isn’t what the data community talks about but the narrow audience it talks to, and widening that audience is the only way to regain perspective on what actually matters.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the Big Data era is ending not because data got less important, but because the industry finally shifted from grand, hype-driven promises to boring, practical tools like Snowflake and dbt. The real breakthrough is lowering friction and cost so “ordinary” teams can do more with familiar workflows, which will quietly unlock new use cases over time. He contends Databricks and similar tools should stop selling visionary narratives and instead sell themselves as simple, better databases.
money_quotes: Databricks is a $38 billion dollar mistake.
But we never connected these dots during our evaluation because the story Databricks told was buzzwords.
Snowflake’s was boring, and all we wanted—at least at first—was boring.
By following the same path, Big Data is finally starting to live up to its potential.
With Snowflake—and hopefully, with Databricks—we don’t get flying cars or predicting the future; we just get a little less headache.
posts.key_insight: Big Data’s promise is being realized not through revolutionary new paradigms, but through boring, reliable infrastructure that makes familiar analytics cheaper and easier.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that SQL’s dominance has encouraged data teams to model business logic directly in queries, but many real business processes aren’t relational and get poorly approximated as a result. He suggests that semantic layers should move beyond pure SQL, via richer logic languages (like Jinja-heavy dbt or Malloy) or even non-SQL databases, to capture complex business models more faithfully.
money_quotes: Perhaps, to nail a disputation on the church door, one of the core responsibilities of a data team—modeling a business, and defining a semantic layer—is best done in another language.
This type of semantic layer has its problems, though. Most notably, there is notrue model.
In other words, defining a semantic layer in pure SQL is like animating a movie by hand.

Which leads us to the heretical conclusion: Just as SQL can, but shouldn’t, be used to define complex statistical analysis, perhaps SQL can, but probably shouldn’t, be used to model the complex operations of a business.
Every once in a while, though, that which is supposed to be certain, isn't.
posts.key_insight: SQL-centric semantic layers make business modeling accessible but brittle, and richer logic-driven layers may be necessary to represent complex operations accurately.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the more common way statistics mislead isn’t through doctored charts but through the words that frame them. Adjectives like “narrowly,” “nearly perfect,” or “spiked” interpret data for us, so we accept a narrative we might not reach on the numbers alone.
money_quotes: People can spin stories simply by telling us what to think.
It’s not the data that gets us, but the adjectives that describe it.
The claim decodes the data, and the data proves the claim.
With data, you’re still just another person with an opinion.

As often as not, we wouldn’t—not because the conclusion is wrong, but because, when presented with data on some domain we don’t deeply understand, we have no choice but to look for clues and shortcuts to help us make sense of those numbers.
posts.key_insight: Most statistical deception happens in the narrative framing, where words interpret numbers for us and steer our conclusions.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that Elon Musk’s $265B fortune is so large that normal comparisons fail, so he offers vivid, often absurd benchmarks to make the scale legible. He then pivots to the real concern: a single private citizen controlling a major channel of public discourse is dangerous regardless of who that person is, and supporting the deal often boils down to personal affinity for Musk rather than principle.
money_quotes: There is a plain and obvious problem with Elon Musk owning Twitter: One man will have taken complete control of the major artery of mass political communication and elite discourse.
Some things—nuclear weapons, access to the pictures on every iPhone on the planet, the entire world’s Chrome browsing history, Gmail’s servers—shouldn’t be for sale.

There are only two answers. You can either support people’s right to spend money how they please—you know, free speech—and accept the anonymous purchase, or you can object to it, on general principle, because no one person should be entrusted with that power, no matter how many lotteries they win.
The only other option is to just admit that you like Elon Musk, that you like the idea of him being in charge, and that nearly everything else is a rationalization for rooting for your guy.

Most efforts to explain it are absurd “comparisons”—Elon Musk is worth more than all but 25 companies on the S&P 500! If you took 265 billion one dollar bills and laid them end-to-end, they’d stretch from here to Venus!—that are no easier to grasp than the original figure.
posts.key_insight: Musk’s fortune is nearly incomprehensible, but the more important issue is that such wealth enables one person to control a core public communications platform, a power that should be off-limits regardless of who holds it.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the modern data stack’s ideals—modularity, open source, and avoiding lock-in—are less important to most buyers than consolidated, tightly integrated suites that simply work. Microsoft’s dominance in BI and databases shows that customers often prefer convenience, bundling, and sales reach over craft and composability. The likely future, he suggests, is consolidation, not a sprawling best‑of‑breed ecosystem.
money_quotes: Microsoft is a dominant player in the data industry.
This, combined with Microsoft’s dominant position in the market, suggests that customers actually don’t care about modularity.
We don’t want to be locked in, but more than that, we don’t want to have to migrate at all.

Most people “don’t care about the aesthetics of software in the same way non-plumbers don’t care about the aesthetics of plumbing—they just want their information in the right place or their hot water to work.”
Consolidation, it turns out, may be less of economic necessity, and more of a product opportunity.
posts.key_insight: For most data buyers, the practical benefits of a consolidated, integrated suite outweigh the ideals of modularity and openness, making consolidation the more realistic winning strategy.

posts.summary: Benn argues that for startups and big strategic choices, committing fully to a path matters more than picking the “perfect” option; optionality and constant iteration often dilute focus and stall progress. He extends this to data work, claiming analytics can undermine commitment by highlighting uncertainty, so data teams should prioritize helping organizations stick to decisions to improve outcomes.
money_quotes: To propose an analytically heretical axiom about big choices like these, making the decision is more important than the decision you make.
Often, we don’t fail because we choose the wrong thing; we fail because we refuse to choose anything at all.
But eventually, the company will find itself stretched across multiple visions, unsure of where it’s supposed to go next and who it’s building for.
As data folk, we’re not here to help organizations make better decisions; we’re here to create better results.
We also have to help people stick to them.
posts.key_insight: Commitment to a chosen path drives results more than continued analysis or optionality, and data teams should support that commitment rather than perpetuate doubt.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that Silicon Valley (like DC) has built a status hierarchy around external scoreboards—valuations, funding rounds, and stock prices—that distort how people measure their work and worth. The market crash reveals how fickle those metrics are, and he suggests using the downturn to detach from hype and refocus ambition on controllable, intrinsically meaningful outcomes.
money_quotes: In this context, fundraising rounds and startup valuations are more than financing mechanics; they’re yardsticks for our careers, and an unspoken sorting algorithm for social interactions.
On reflection, though, there is wisdom in these points. They’re just aimed at the wrong problem. For it’s not the technical dynamics of the venture market that need a reset—it’s us.
In other words, if Zoom’s stock price is its scoreboard, it’s a terrible one—driven by a mix of randomness, animal spirits, the murderous whims of an egomaniacal dictator, and worldwide Acts of God.
Rather than despair during bad storms such as these, we should use them as an opportunity to let go.
This isn’t a call for nihilism or to reduce our ambition; it’s a reminder to pay attention to what we’re ambitious about.
posts.key_insight: Market crashes expose how arbitrary Silicon Valley’s status metrics are, offering a chance to shift ambition from hype and valuation to meaningful, controllable work.

posts.summary: The post argues that confusing data job titles create unstable pay bands and inequitable compensation across the industry. Stancil contends that analysts’ work—translating messy business problems into persuasive, high‑impact decisions—is harder to develop and more valuable than analytics engineering, yet Silicon Valley’s bias toward technical skills inflates engineer pay. He calls for intentionally paying analysts more to counter this bias and to elevate “soft” skills often gendered and undervalued.
money_quotes: If we don’t talk about pay—because it’s complicated, because it’s taboo, or because we assume it’ll just sort itself out—pay bands and industry habits will settle along the same biased lines that they always have.

These sets of responsibilities require different skills, some of which are much harder to acquire and hone than others. We should pay people accordingly—and, therefore, analysts should make more than analytics engineers.
It’s telling, I think, that this feels like a radical proposal.
To me, the most plausible explanation is a simpler one: Silicon Valley overvalues technical skills.
By paying analysts more, we can also break down some of the historic rot that has denigrated “feminine” soft skills.
posts.key_insight: The industry’s default bias toward technical work distorts pay, and correcting it means intentionally valuing analysts’ interpersonal, problem-framing, and influence skills more highly.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that current AI/automation tools in analytics mostly disappoint because they’re built to mimic analysts’ existing tasks, which often require human judgment and context. He suggests a more promising future is AI as a creative partner—posing novel questions, reframing problems, or generating alternative structures—changing what analytics work even looks like.
money_quotes: Augmented analytics tools, which market themselves as needle detectors for our giant haystacks of data, find mostly useless correlations, some spurious ones, and the occasional meaningful one.
This is why analysts (should) get paid. They have to account for unaccountable factors.
In dismissing the potential of AI in analytics, I’m falling into the same trap that I did with Alexa.

The broader point is that this approach—use AI as a creative partner rather than a calculator for trudging through a bunch of repetitive math problems—is almost entirely different from how the industry has traditionally thought of applying it to analytics.
But, for better or for worse, I now believe it’s inevitable that we’ll get something.
posts.key_insight: AI is unlikely to replace analysts by doing their current work, but it may transform analytics by acting as a creative partner that changes the questions and problems themselves.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that Google has amassed an impressive but incoherent set of data products, like a kid with powerful Pokemon cards but no cohesive deck. The opportunity is to define a clear buyer (the "purple people" in data orgs) and sell an integrated, easy-to-buy stack rather than overpowered but disconnected tools, a miss driven by tech-first culture and compute-driven sales.
money_quotes: In the data industry, Google is this kid.
As far as data technologies go, it’s an embarrassment of riches. But like that of the kid with the Pokémon deck of death, Google’s strategy is much harder to discern.
Modularity, in other words, often isn’t as valuable as convenience.
In the multifaceted, multimodal, multiplayer world of enterprise data, it’s the glue that matters the most.
Google has the technology to build it, and the capital to buy it. What’s less clear is if they have the vision to imagine it.
posts.key_insight: Google’s data stack has the pieces to dominate, but without a coherent buyer focus and integrated experience, its overpowered tools fail to translate into market leadership.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that Snowflake’s new application framework signals a shift from “data apps” as dashboards on top of the warehouse to apps that run inside the warehouse itself. If Snowflake executes this vision, it becomes more like an iPhone platform than an app store, enabling deeper functionality, easier distribution, and a new wave of data products. He concludes that this could reignite the data tooling ecosystem rather than mark its decline.
