money_quotes: Data apps are actually much more than blinged-out dashboards that sit on top of a warehouse.
They’re also programs that run in a data warehouse.
Snowflake isn’t just an app store for buying widgets; it’s the entire iPhone.
Installing a data app would be like downloading a mobile app: Put in a credit card, check a few permission boxes, and you’re ready to go.
Running apps inside of a database could redefine what a database is as much as iPhones redefined what cell phones were.
posts.key_insight: Snowflake’s app framework points toward a future where apps run inside the data warehouse, potentially turning Snowflake into a platform layer that reshapes data infrastructure and product ecosystems.

posts.summary: Stancil pauses his usual light commentary to mark the Supreme Court’s decision as a grave moral and democratic failure. He argues that six unelected justices are imposing their will, stripping rights, and signaling further harm, making the day too serious for his normal industry jokes.
money_quotes: Today, all of the sudden,isn't the dayto tell those jokes.

In what is fast becoming itsnew tradition, the Supreme Court decided again to impose its deranged will on anunapproving populace, stripping people ofthe rights they want(to say nothing of what the rights they’re owed), transparentlyinventing legal rationalesalong the way, all while its most vile member—a man with such a feeble commitment to his oath of office law thathe clearly entertainednot only rejecting the apparent public opinion of the people, but the voted, counted, and certifiedwill of the electorate—teased thatmore is to come.
The arc of the moral universe is long, and it bends toward whatever loathsome destination six unelected and unaccountablepoliticianswant it to.

But on this Friday, I don't have much to say, other than it's a disastrous day, created by six disastrous people, confirmed by adisastrous idiot, and empowered by what has become adisastrous institution.
But today,read something else.
posts.key_insight: The author sets aside his usual industry commentary to condemn the Supreme Court’s decision as a disastrous assertion of unaccountable power.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the rise of analytics engineering isn’t just hype or better tooling; it reflects analysts’ deeper preference for structured, engineering-flavored work over the messy social reality of analysis. The role offers clear boundaries and tangible outputs, making it an attractive escape from organizational politics and ambiguity. This shift suggests the industry may need to rethink what it means to be an analyst and be more honest about what actually satisfies people in data roles.
money_quotes: So naturally, ten years later, the new sexiest job of the twenty-first century, the job that analysts want to graduate into, is…the analytics engineer, whose primary responsibility is cleaning and modeling messy data.
The problem, in other words, was never the task; it was how we did it.
Analytics engineering emerged as our escape.
For an analytics engineer, success is a humming system, a clean codebase, and the satisfying tick of dbt jobs completing in your terminal.

To me, that reveals the real reason behind the industry’s hard tilt towards analytics engineering: Many of us have had a latent interest in engineering, and, more cynically, a lurking dissatisfaction with the messier side of analytical roles.
posts.key_insight: Analytics engineering’s popularity says less about the job itself and more about analysts’ desire for structured, engineering-like work over the messy, political reality of analysis.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that as more data people work at data vendors, community interactions are increasingly shaped by corporate affiliation, turning peers into prospects and conversations into subtle marketing. The result is a loss of genuine, human connection in the data community, which he thinks can be restored only by centering shared personal experiences over tools and company agendas.
money_quotes: More than finding a human connection, I’m protecting a brand.
The problem isn’t that vendors are present in our space; it’s that vendors are present in us.

No culture of congeniality changes the fact that we’re trying to take money from each others’ pockets and cause genuine harm to each others’ careers; no amount of professional appreciation changes the fact that our community is one in an active and sincere war with itself.
We can still build a thriving community in this world; it just means it needs to be built on a different foundation—one that’s a little more human, and a little less vendor.
We may play for the name on the front of our jersey, but we keep the name on the back.
posts.key_insight: The data community can only stay healthy if people deliberately separate their identities from their employers and prioritize human connection over vendor promotion.

posts.summary: Benn argues that data teams hardcode business logic all over their stacks, leading to inconsistent definitions and opaque concepts for anyone outside the data team. He proposes centralizing these logical variables (and eventually logic itself) in a shared configuration file so definitions stay consistent, discoverable, and editable. This could shift workflows toward more composable, collaborative analytics engineering, much like dbt did for transformations.
money_quotes: Nearly every asset that a data team creates—a report, a dashboard, a data model, a predictive forecast, you name it—is riddled with hardcoded bits of business logic.
Because it’s so simple to encode logic like this in queries…it’s impossible to keep the values of these variables consistent.
Even if we manage to keep everything consistent, nobody outside of the data team can ever know for sure how these concepts are defined.
A config file offers a third alternative. We can simultaneously centralize variables without needing to rethink the architecture of the lineage graph.
A better data configuration file, and the change in workflow that it nudges us towards, might echo the same pattern, even if it, too, initially sounds like a toy you could build on a weekend.
posts.key_insight: Centralizing business logic in a shared config file could fix definition drift and make analytics workflows more consistent, discoverable, and collaborative.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that while data helps, it is not a decisive competitive advantage against stronger talent, intuition, or speed—especially in early-stage companies. Data’s real value is a small, compounding edge across many decisions, not dramatic breakthroughs on a few big bets.
money_quotes: Great data teams likely make somewhat better guesses, but at the end of the day, we’re all still gambling.
Data offers the same promise. Its constant presence in an organization is like knowing the count of the deck.
Though it makes us a bit more informed in each decision, the effect is only felt in the aggregate, as the small edge compounds over time.
Being data-driven is a long game. It takes time for an advantage to accumulate.
Data is a competitive edge, but it’s earned, from months and years of grinding at the table, counting cards, playing hand after hand, slowly bringing down the house.
posts.key_insight: Data-driven companies can win, but only because steady, modest advantages compound over many decisions rather than because data guarantees better outcomes on big strategic bets.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the modern data stack lacks a true “single pane of glass,” and that multiple tool categories are converging to claim that central control role. This competitive rush, driven by venture funding expectations, makes fragmentation worse until the market forces a single winner.
money_quotes: But to get there, we also have to agree on who should provide that pane of glass—and right now, there’s nothing close to a consensus on that question.
The biggest looming battle, however, will be over a different territory: The brain—or operating system—of the data stack.
Generally speaking, if we can’t get something right in that many tries, the issue probably isn’t some tweak that we’ll fix on the next one.
The issue is systemic—and each new effort to make it better actually makes the fragmentation worse.
Because by 2032, someone will own it. But there can only be one unifier—there can only be one single source of truth, one single pane of glass—and not everyone can be king.
posts.key_insight: The data stack’s chaos is less a tooling problem than a market structure problem, and VC-driven competition will force a single “control tower” winner even as it intensifies fragmentation in the meantime.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that analysts are screened on poor signals like resumes and brand names because their work is hard to showcase, which shuts out strong talent. He proposes replacing boot-camp style credentialing and take-home tests with concise analytical portfolios that emphasize thinking, storytelling, and real project context. A short, structured slide deck could surface skill and potential more fairly while improving interviews.
money_quotes: I think we can solve this though—analysts should create portfolios.
Portfolios keep the baby and throw out the bathwater.

As an interviewer, I want to understand how candidates translate a vague business problem into a quantitative one, and how they navigate the inevitable frustrations of their first efforts not working out.

Whether we like it or not, job candidates are going to teach themselves to the test: So long as we use take-home SQL assignments and whiteboard coding exercises in our interview processes, that’s what people will try to learn.
Analysts aren't just actuaries who take in and spit out numbers; they're storytellers who need to be engaging and persuasive.
posts.key_insight: Analytical portfolios would surface real capability and storytelling skill more fairly than resumes or tests, reshaping hiring toward how analysts actually work.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that data orchestration is backwards: we schedule pipelines from upstream triggers instead of from the freshness guarantees we actually care about at the end of the lineage. He proposes a reverse-ETL-style model where each table declares latency requirements and the system derives the DAG and run timing to meet those guarantees, improving maintainability, cost control, and alert relevance.
money_quotes: We want the updated datasets or fresh dashboards at the end of our lineage graph, but define our schedules around what’s at the beginning of it.

As analysts, we committed parameterized SQL statements and YAML configuration files to Integritie, which would use the queries, and the dependencies implied in them, to regularly create and update tables in our warehouse.
Instead, for each model, analysts set a latency requirement—this table should never have data that’s more than four hours out of date, a day out of date, a week out of date.
When adding a new table that we needed to update once a day, we didn’t have to define a procedure for making it so... We just told the tool what we wanted.
I just want my data to be fresh—where I can declare what fresh means—and to know when it’s not.
posts.key_insight: Define data freshness guarantees first and let orchestration derive the DAG and schedules, rather than chaining tasks from upstream triggers.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that while analytics teams obsess over preventing errors, they barely grapple with what to do when mistakes inevitably slip into production. Because analytical errors shape decisions and persist, he questions whether engineering-style blameless retros are sufficient and suggests borrowing from journalism’s accountability. He also proposes rounding metrics to signal uncertainty and reduce the blast radius of inevitable corrections.
money_quotes: For as much as we talk about how to avoid getting things wrong—which is an implicit acknowledgement that we get things wrong a lot—we spend hardly any time talking about what happens when we do.
Despite its sheen of scientific rigor, data is a confidence game; the only currency we have is trust.
Analytical bugs, from bad data to miscalculated metrics to unsound analysis, are different. They stick.
If data teams want the same kind of influence, to be in the room where decisions get made, and to “be heard and have an impact,” we probably have to bear the burden of our mistakes.
No matter how vetted our queries and precise our math, most things that we produce as data teams are estimates.
posts.key_insight: Because analytical errors are inevitable and have lasting consequences, data teams should treat metrics as uncertain estimates and rethink correction practices, potentially emphasizing accountability and rounded communication over engineering-style blamelessness.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that habits and identities often calcify from chance events, and we mistake their persistence for virtue. He suggests we are less bound by the past than we believe, and should prioritize future possibilities over inherited routines or traditions. The right question is whether a habit still serves who we want to become, not how long we have maintained it.
money_quotes: The future is precious, not the past.
We mortgage the promise of our future for the familiarity of our past.
We should live life like there's no yesterday.
Inertia, however, is not strength.
The most hallowed ground is not the trail behind us, but the open field in front of us.
posts.key_insight: Endurance and tradition aren’t inherently virtuous; we should keep only the habits and identities that actively serve the future we want.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that Snowflake’s apparent inevitability is less certain than the industry consensus suggests, and runs a “pre‑mortem” on how a dominant data warehouse could lose its edge. He outlines multiple plausible failure paths—cost dynamics, architectural fit, market overreach, and margin pressure—while still acknowledging Snowflake’s current strength.
money_quotes: Sure things, in other words, aren’t always dominant.
More succinctly, demand for Snowflake won’t go down because demand for Snowflake went up.
In ten years, that could prove to be outdated, and Snowflake’s new features could be—like an on-premise provider stumbling their way into a clumsy cloud offering—a facade on an aging foundation.
Just because we can build a database with lots of technical bells and flashy whistles doesn’t mean we should.
Under these conditions, in a war of attrition against a bigger opponent, even the invincible become vulnerable.
posts.tone: analytical and critical with a wry, satirical edge
posts.key_insight: Even if Snowflake looks like an inevitable winner today, multiple structural and strategic pressures could still erode its dominance over time.

posts.summary: Stancil argues the “data as a product” idea is incomplete without a clear definition of what counts as production. Because data work often blurs one-off analysis and durable assets, teams end up maintaining a sprawling, accidental production surface. He proposes explicitly declaring production assets and letting everything else be temporary, with real support and deprecation for the chosen few.
money_quotes: If data is a product, we should have a better definition of production.
Production is a fuzzy concept, with a wide and blurry boundary between it and development.
When I send a report like this to someone, there’s an implicit contract attached to it: It works now, for the thing I said it would work for. It is, at that moment, in production.
We should keep production narrow and exclusive, and be content going days and weeks at a time without ever shipping anything to it.
To paraphrase a favorite data cliché, that which is defined is maintained.
posts.key_insight: Data teams need an explicit, narrow definition of production so they can support what matters and let the rest expire instead of growing an unmanageable, accidental production surface.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that Fivetran’s dominance is not inevitable despite riding two waves: SaaS proliferation and centralized data warehouses. He lays out three ways Fivetran could fail—if the need to move data fades, if SaaS vendors and warehouses change how data is moved, or if a better/cheaper tool displaces it—while ultimately betting on inertia but acknowledging how quickly the “impractical” can become the new normal.
money_quotes: Fivetran is, if nothing else, a product of its time.
We’d all be better off—well, all of us except ELT vendors—if more apps did what Segment does, and wrote data straight to the warehouse themselves.
This, to me, is the biggest structural risk to Fivetran. Apps build outlet stores, and go DTC. SaaS vendors giveth Fivetran its market; SaaS vendors could taketh it away.
I’m skeptical of this too. The data market is moving aggressively towards hosted services.
Sometimes, the future that looks unreasonable and impractical today ends up looking right and natural tomorrow.
posts.key_insight: Fivetran’s position is strong but structurally vulnerable if SaaS vendors and warehouses shift data movement upstream, eroding the economics that make ELT a standalone business.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that data contracts address a real problem—brittle, implicit expectations between data producers and consumers—but negotiated agreements between teams are impractical and misdirected. Instead of people-heavy contracts, he favors technical safeguards built by data teams (e.g., schema/staging tests) that validate data before it hits production without forcing providers into ongoing negotiations.
money_quotes: Data contracts make exactly that trade. They replace a brittle technical system with a negotiating table.
If those structures need to change to make a product better or to smooth over a kink in the sales cycle, they shouldn’t have to consult an analytics engineer first.
In other words, data teams can’t expect to stop changes to products or Salesforce; we can only hope to contain them.
They should instead be a simple defense—built by data teams, for data teams—against communication failures.
This structure satisfies our primary problem: It tests data before it reaches production, not after.
posts.key_insight: The best “data contract” isn’t a negotiated promise between teams but a technical pre-production testing layer that data teams control to prevent bad data from propagating.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the iPhone is remarkable for delivering a fundamentally egalitarian product experience across social strata, unlike Twitter, where each user inhabits a materially different version shaped by identity and prominence. Because these experiences are unmeasurable and often inaccessible, leaders and data practitioners must approach product decisions with humility and seek lived experience beyond dashboards.
money_quotes: Of all of the iPhone’s accomplishments, one of the most remarkable is that it put the same exact piece of technology into the pockets of people in every strata of society.
On any social media platform—and on Twitter in particular—none of us use the same product.
In contrast to the iPhone, Twitter is remarkable in its experiential inequality.
We don’t have to use numbers to be rational.
Our data won’t save us from having to endure those of others.
posts.key_insight: Some products demand humility because their most important user experiences are unequal, unquantifiable, and only knowable through empathy rather than data alone.

posts.summary: Stancil imagines a plausible failure case for dbt Labs, arguing that the company’s success could be undermined by a mismatch between its SQL-first core and the added complexity of semantic layers, pricing shifts, and community fatigue. He contends that these pressures would weaken dbt’s moat and invite both direct and indirect competitors. He ultimately concludes the failure narrative is unlikely because dbt’s product value is real and enduring.
money_quotes: Given such a golden run, the only data industry story that would be more captivating than one about dbt Labs’ success would be one about its failure.
When dbt was first released, its promise was simple and elegant: It’s SQL, all the way down.
Just like every other prior effort to build a semantic model, dbt Labs ended up building a complex semantic language of their own.
This decay of the community was a slow but fatal disease.
It’s a damned good product, and it’s not just pretty to think so.
posts.key_insight: dbt’s biggest risk isn’t demand but the tension between its SQL-first simplicity and the complexity of expanding into semantics, pricing, and new markets, which could erode its community moat.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that internal tools often look like startup gold because they solve a real problem inside a company, but that success is a mirage driven by a captive customer, edge-case needs, and effortless internal distribution. Once outside the company, those tools face messy, varied workflows, politics, and integration realities that make them poor fits for broader markets. He suggests investors should be more skeptical and look to consultancies for better-validated product ideas, noting his own experience at Mode as a cautionary example.
money_quotes: There are three fallacies that make internal tools look more promising than they actually are.
It’s easy to find product market-fit when your market is your coworker sitting right next to you, telling you exactly why the other products they’ve tried don’t work.
Selling into a market like this requires selling a lot more than a single product; it also requires selling a philosophy, a process, and a bunch of counseling sessions to get everyone to agree.
Internal tools are often bespoke bits of tile that fit exactly within the mosaic of each company—a perfect shape for the picture they’re in, but not useful anywhere else.

Had Mode failed years ago, this would’ve been its cause of death: We misunderstood the market we were building for, made a thing that wasn’t useful to enough people, and ran out of money before we realized that we needed to step out of the shadow of the internal tool we were trying to emulate.
posts.key_insight: Internal success is a misleading signal because the very factors that make a tool thrive inside one company—captive users, bespoke needs, and frictionless distribution—rarely translate to a viable external market.

posts.summary: Stancil contrasts ambition-driven careerism with work chosen for its intrinsic value, arguing that communities thrive when people show up for the work rather than the payoff. He uses dbt Labs’ Coalesce as a rare, genuine data community that feels like college, while acknowledging that scale and business pressures will eventually erode that culture. The piece ends with a call to appreciate the community now, even knowing it won’t last forever.
money_quotes: By separating the work from the reward, we can be more honest with ourselves about why we do the things we do.
When we become fixated on a destination, the journey is an interchangeable means to an end, a disposable rocket booster that’s only value is to put us into space.
The combination of the two has turned Coalesce into one of the last honest gatherings in the data community.
This isn’t a community, but a broadcast, with talking heads and commercials, and an audience wondering if they’re allowed to participate.
No community lasts forever, and the business success that makes Coalesce possible today is the same force that will one day compel it to become something different tomorrow.
posts.tone: reflective and analytical, with a note of wistful criticism
posts.key_insight: Even knowing a community’s golden era is temporary, its value comes from people showing up for the work and relationships themselves, not just the eventual rewards.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that data teams inside startups operate like collectivist service orgs, which leads to fuzzy prioritization and hidden politics. He proposes an internal market with credits and auctions to price data work, claiming it could allocate effort more efficiently and transparently while still letting teams steer strategy. He ends cautiously, noting the model could work with safeguards but needs regulation to avoid a cutthroat culture.
money_quotes: Nobody is a better judge of a data asset’s worth than its consumer.
Credits level the playing field.
An open auction doesn’t just create a market for the work itself, however; it also prices in a lot of other intangible tradeoffs that data teams often have to make.
A transparent bidding system in which everyone can see what bids the team accepts and at what cost they accept them may actually create a healthier dynamic than one built on argument and influence.
As promising as a free market for analysis might be, on today of all days, I’m still voting for a bit more regulation.
posts.key_insight: A credit-and-auction market for data work could replace political prioritization with transparent price signals, but it needs guardrails to avoid perverse incentives.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that elite institutions and Silicon Valley share a performative culture that rewards confident, untested ideas rather than rigorous scrutiny, producing a class of leaders who are affirmed rather than challenged. He sees the VC pitch dynamic and Twitter’s Musk-era chaos as parallel examples of the “very smart boy” myth and its consequences. Twitter becomes the live-fire test of whether tech’s celebrated first‑principles bravado is wisdom or just noise.
money_quotes: They were never told that they were wrong, only that they were interesting.
Most of these suggestions are naked copies of industry fads or the VC's latest successes.

But if it tears itself down—if Twitter ferments into another toxic backwater, overrun with misinformation, frothing violence, and scams and scammy ads, as so many are warning it will—Silicon Valley’s reputation should go with it.
The ugly experiment at Twitter is becoming the ultimate test of tech industry dogma, and of entrepreneurs’ supposed golden touch.
Our emperors have no shortage of confidence; now we’ll find out if they have any clothes.
posts.key_insight: The tech elite’s confidence and folklore about visionary genius are being stress‑tested in public, and Twitter may expose how shallow those claims really are.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the data industry’s shift from tools to people and process misses a deeper constraint: most companies don’t have data with the leverage of Silicon Valley giants. The modern data stack looks powerful, but for many businesses the data is more like peat than oil—limited value means copying big-tech practices often yields disappointing returns.
money_quotes: Tools are out; people and processes are in.
In other words, rather than being exemplars of good data practices because they were so good, it’s possible that they’re exemplars because they didn’t have to be that good.
The data of a mid-sized B2B SaaS product simply doesn’t have the potential energy of Google’s search histories, or of an Amazon’s browsing logs.

Peat suppliers can’t copy the refinement methods and cost structures of oil producers and still expect to come out ahead; small SaaS businesses can’t operate as though their data has anywhere near the same value as Uber’s or Airbnb’s.
If there’s a reckoning in the data industry, it’ll come from our expectations of data coming back to earth.
posts.key_insight: Most companies overestimate the value of their data, so emulating big-tech data practices and tooling often leads to waste rather than outsized gains.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the internet has entered a new, hotter phase of chaos where platforms collapse fast, frauds flourish, and attention cycles overwhelm any stable order. He portrays a culture trapped in a runaway machine, where even serious news is instantly drowned by the next manic catastrophe. The only viable stance, he suggests, is to accept the turmoil and write for the void rather than for a stable audience.
money_quotes: The doors are coming off, and the wheels are rattling loose.

Twitter is being called from inside the house; it is being murdered; organs are failing; it’s losing blood; it could practically die overnight, at the height of its cultural power (and popularly, evidently).
We can’t have the internet anymore, because it is down and someone lost the keys.
Surrender into the chaos; widen the gyre; post your most manic ideas; free the falcon from the falconer.
Do not write for a quaint audience on the internet; write for the howling void.
posts.key_insight: The contemporary internet is less a stable platform than a runaway spectacle, so the only honest response is to acknowledge and write into its chaos.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that despite the rough edges and hype, the modern data stack represents a huge leap forward from the brittle, expensive, and manual systems of the early 2010s. Yet he highlights a paradox: data teams delivered substantial business impact even with inferior tooling, so the key question is why today’s far better tech hasn’t produced proportional value gains.
money_quotes: The tools we have today—built and supported by thousands of people across dozens of companies—represent a profound leap forward from what we had then.
Yammer’s data team was as impactful as any that I’ve ever worked with.
Why has data technology advanced so much further than value a data team provides?
Does all of this new tooling actually hurt, by causing us to lose focus on the most important problems... in favor of the shiny new things that don’t actually matter?
But if our 2032 selves want to be as grateful for 2020s as we should be for the 2010s, those are the next questions we need to answer.
posts.key_insight: Modern data tooling is dramatically better, but the industry still hasn’t closed the gap between technological progress and real business impact.

posts.summary: Stancil riffs on watching Lou Gehrig’s retirement speech and realizing he is the same age, which sends him into compiling a sprawling, unnecessary spreadsheet of famous people’s ages at notable moments. The post uses that absurd catalog to poke at age-based comparison, mortality anxiety, and the futility of measuring life against milestones.
money_quotes: A spreadsheet nobody needs
Wrenching stuff, indeed.
Until then, do your own calculus, and join me in making 101 comparisons that never needed to be made.
Are you here to read blog posts about data tooling and analytical best practices—like these bangers—not be reminded of your own looming mortality?
Ok, fair enough—though there is data in this post, I’m guessing that the data that most people are after isn’t the ages of the actors in Home Alone.
posts.key_insight: Age comparisons across famous milestones are both irresistible and ultimately meaningless, revealing more about personal anxiety than about achievement.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that analysis relies on the difficulty of constructing convincing arguments, which gives it a kind of legitimacy even when people are biased and imperfect reasoners. Large language models erase that friction by generating plausible narratives on demand, making it harder to distinguish truth from motivated reasoning and undermining decision‑making.
money_quotes: We’re not smart enough for AI.

There is, however, one useful protection against us constantly being fooled by questionable reasoning: It's a lot harder to come up with decent arguments for something that's wrong than something that's right.
None of these are real, in any sense. Even in examples when the underlying facts are true, there is no logical thread that ties one point to the next.
If we can’t unsee patterns that we know are illusionary, what hope do we have in evaluating the “seemingly-feasible” arguments that can now be generated for anything?

We are lazy; we are illogical; we are biased. We will not collectively better ourselves in response to being slowly boiled by distortions of reason; we will sit in the soup until our brains melt out of our heads.
posts.key_insight: LLMs make it trivially easy to generate convincing but ungrounded arguments, eroding the friction that once helped analysis stay tethered to reality.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that despite sweeping technological advances, productivity hasn’t improved because the tools we use shape behavior more than any process or best-practice talk. Slack exemplifies this by incentivizing frantic, low-quality communication, so the real path to better work is to build products that enforce better processes, especially for data teams. In data, he claims only purpose-built tools will shift norms around requests, alerting, and decision-making.
money_quotes: Slack is a kanban board with two stages: Gone forever, and do it now.

But if we score Slack against communication tools’ more meaningful measures—does it help us make good decisions, and keep everyone informed of and aligned around those decisions?—Slack is a spectacular step backwards.
If an environment makes a particular behavior convenient, that’s what we’ll do.
No matter how much we bang on process, we won’t solve our own productivity paradox until we get the products right.
To butcher every self-styled internet intellectual’s favorite quote, “the product is the process.”
posts.key_insight: Lasting improvements in how data teams work will come from purpose-built products that reshape behavior, not from exhortations about process.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that penalty shootouts are a different skill than the 120 minutes of soccer that precede them, creating a strategic arbitrage opportunity. Because shootouts are common and roster spots are underutilized, teams could meaningfully raise their World Cup odds by carrying dedicated penalty kick specialists. The idea is a low-cost, “Moneyball” style edge that conventional roster construction ignores.
money_quotes: The problem with penalty kicks isn't their randomness; it's that they have next to nothing to do with the sport that everyone just played for 120 minutes.
What if they were players who didn’t do anything else other than practice pounding the upper nineties from twelve yards out?
The answer to that question—what if teams put specialists on their roster?—is that they’d be about two percent more likely to win the World Cup.
Teams could carry two penalty kick specialists, at essentially no cost to how they manage their teams.
Don’t be cowards. Don’t look for traditional talent, and fill out your rosters with safe picks of “world-class” players that’ll get six minutes of garbage time in your eventual quarterfinal collapse.
posts.key_insight: Because shootouts are common and hinge on a distinct, trainable skill, dedicating roster spots to penalty-kick specialists is a small, quantifiable change that can materially improve a team’s World Cup chances.

posts.summary: Stancil reviews a decade of data-industry predictions and finds that fewer than half were mostly correct, with many being vague, trend‑extrapolation filler. He argues that forecasts repeatedly promise imminent breakthroughs (AI, predictive analytics, streaming, data marketplaces) that arrive later or not at all, while genuinely important developments often go unpredicted. The upshot: expect 2023 to miss in familiar ways, with surprises filling the gaps.
money_quotes: In total, I’d estimate that less than half of the nearly 600 predictions issued by various experts over the last ten years could be reasonably scored as correct.
We stubbornly predict, year after year, that this is finally the moment some sweeping change bears fruit.
To adapt Rudi Dornbusch’s famous quote about economics, “things take longer to happen than you think they will, and then they happen faster than you thought they could.”
Outside of a few fuzzy nods towards the emergence of a new analytics stack, we didn’t anticipate the explosion of the modern data stack.
If past performance is any indicator of future results, 2023 won’t look like we expect.
posts.key_insight: A decade of data forecasts shows a pattern of over‑eager, vague predictions that miss timing, while the biggest shifts often emerge unexpectedly.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that math is professionally overrated in data work: most roles rely on basic arithmetic and judgment, not advanced theory. The real value of “mathiness” is cultural signaling—using quantitative language and artifacts to claim authority and shut down qualitative debate—so we should prize communication and domain understanding more than we do.
money_quotes: In other words, it’s not our ability to do fancy math that makes us effective, but our ability to apply basic math.
Our deference to math, and particularly its more advanced strains, isn’t about its practical business value.
It’s about setting the terms of our internal debates, and ensuring that those who appear to “bring data” maintain their power in the professional order.
Like the business jargon it’s usually wrapped around, these phrases only appear precise.
If we valued persuasion and storytelling as much as math, this wouldn’t happen.
posts.key_insight: Math’s prestige in data work functions more as a signaling and power tool than as a reflection of the actual skills most analysts need.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the data tooling boom favored neutral, modular ecosystems, but the market’s fragmentation and tighter budgets are pushing vendors into direct competition. Instead of simple consolidation into big-platform bundles, he proposes a “conglomerate” model where adjacent data products live under one umbrella, staying independent yet more seamlessly integrated. This could preserve product character while delivering the cohesion customers increasingly demand.
money_quotes: They found a hole in the market that was overwhelmingly complementary with the rest of the industry, and quickly built a popular product that discouraged head-on competition.
Without that, the data stack, assembled from a dozen tools produced by the same number of startups, will remain frustratingly disjointed and incongruous.
But that was then. Today, time may be a luxury the industry no longer has.
As the data industry settles, I believe there could be—and probably should be—a couple similar conglomerates that focus on data services.
If it’s the path that helps companies keep their character while also building more cohesive experiences for their customers, the modern data stack might be better as the modern data conglomerate.
posts.key_insight: The data stack’s future may be best served not by pure modularity or full consolidation, but by conglomerates that align adjacent tools under one roof to deliver cohesion without forcing total lock-in.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that many data teams may lack true product-market fit: they are often sold rather than eagerly bought, and persistent struggles to gain influence suggest a deeper mismatch with stakeholder demand. He contends that the field may be extrapolating from a few early “customers” and should rethink what services are actually wanted, even if that means reinventing the role.
money_quotes: Products that have product-market fit are bought, not sold.
In these cases, user education and a few more features won’t fit the product into the market.
What if we’re not prophets in the wilderness, but salespeople selling a lemon?
We may be extrapolating too much from our first few customers, and are trying to force their story onto a broader market that never wanted it.
The problem might not be our sales pitch, but what we’ve been taught to sell.
posts.key_insight: The persistent need for data teams to sell their value suggests a product-market mismatch, and the discipline may need to redefine what it offers based on real customer demand.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that while LLM demos look impressive, they fail in real-world data environments because messy schemas, ambiguous definitions, and complex queries overwhelm current models. The real revolution will come not from smarter models alone but from rebuilding data structures to be AI-friendly—standardized, machine-readable schemas that shift analytics work from bespoke design to factory-like production.
money_quotes: The distance between this and companies’ actual data ecosystems is staggering.
The progress bar is a mirage.
Rather than asking self-driving cars to navigate hopelessly complex terrain, we could bulldoze the terrain into something more manageable.
What if instead of building new patterns for people, we built them for our AI gods?
If that changes, if we design our worlds for AIs, if we give our power and strength unto the beast, we might be able to go much further than we can today.
posts.key_insight: AI won’t transform analytics by replacing analysts outright; it will force a redesign of data schemas themselves so machines—not people—can reliably interpret and query them.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the data industry has inflated the word “insight” into a vague marketing promise, even though most users and businesses mainly want reliable, basic reporting about what’s happening. He contends that data teams chase flashy analysis because it is socially and commercially rewarded, while the harder, more valuable work of accurate reporting is undervalued.
money_quotes: Insights are grandiose enough to sound valuable, and amorphous enough to avoid actually saying what that value is.
The real insights, the ones that people are paying for and are consistently valuable, are simple descriptions of fact.
More often than not, this isn’t insight; it’s distortion.
Basic reporting isn’t actually easy.
Until we celebrate and reward the “simple” work of creating basic reports and of working with experts around the business, data teams and data vendors will keep chasing the false idol of insight.
posts.key_insight: The industry’s obsession with “insight” obscures the real value of data work, which is delivering clear, accurate reporting about what’s happening.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that data teams often overvalue objective truth-telling, when their most useful role in business may be to align people around a chosen strategy. Because execution and belief shape outcomes, selective, narrative-driven use of data can be more valuable than neutral analysis, even if it feels uncomfortable.
money_quotes: But, crass as it may sound, truth is just a means to an end—to make a company successful.
The question shouldn’t be if this is a useful role for a data team; the question should be, is it the most useful role for a data team?
The success of business decisions and strategies, by contrast, depend heavily on what happens after a decision is made.
In this light, it seems at least possible that the 76ers’ data team—and by extension, every data team—would be more valuable as periodic propagandists than they would be as truth seekers.
The point of the dashboard isn’t to make decisions. It’s to keep the mission at the front of everyone’s mind.
posts.key_insight: In business, data’s highest value often lies not in objective truth-finding but in building conviction and alignment that make strategies succeed.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that startups can fall into “customer capture,” where divergent early customers pull a company into incompatible directions, eroding product coherence and market identity. This is especially acute in the fragmented data tooling landscape, where many adjacent expansion paths look like optionality but become unsustainable variability. The antidote is a disciplined, caring core identity and the willingness to say no to customers and investors until the company’s center of gravity is strong enough to expand.
money_quotes: Eventually, the customers fully capture the company, trapping it not in a single identity, but in many.
The product and its positioning become a labyrinth, a maze without a floor plan, full of doors that don’t open and staircases that lead into the ceiling.
Almost overnight, a narrow tool that was too small to fund can become too big to build.
But in most cases, it’s better to keep a humble soul than to scatter a sold one across a half-dozen horcruxes.
In a world of captives, the free company is king.
posts.key_insight: Customer-driven iteration can quietly splinter a startup’s identity, so sustained success requires disciplined focus on a coherent core before expanding.

posts.summary: Stancil uses his anxiety about flying to argue that emotions often overpower facts, making rational self-talk an unreliable defense against fear. He warns that modern AI, especially chatbots, is uniquely adept at shaping feelings, so the larger risk is not misinformation but engineered emotional manipulation at scale.
money_quotes: I can’t bend my feelings around facts; I can’t forge them between a hammer of reason and the anvil of reality.
Those were stilted and sterile, like an uncomfortably formal support agent who’s required to read from an unnatural script.
But even in these early days, ChatGPT and its siblings are fluid and anthropomorphic; they can, as Thompson says, “make the human it is interacting with feel something.”
In other words, the entire point of Tome, and dozens of other apps like it, is emotional capture.
If this is where we arrive, it’s hard to see who’s controlling who—are we in charge of the AI, or is the AI in charge of us?
posts.key_insight: AI’s greatest near-term power is its ability to manipulate human emotion, and our weakness against that manipulation makes collective harm more likely than mere confusion about facts.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the concept of a “data warehouse” has become fuzzy and skeuomorphic: we recognize examples by vibe, not by a stable definition. As architectures shift toward storage-less, serverless, and file-based compute, he suggests warehouses are already breaking apart and may evolve into something like dbt running directly on object storage.
money_quotes: The alarming truth, I’m realizing, is that the three layers underneath our disorganized outer boundary aren’t actually so stable after all.
To define a data warehouse is to define a sandwich: Every attempt has weird and unacceptable exceptions.
It suggests that the data stacks don’t have to have a traditional warehouse at their center, because today’s warehouse is a skeuomorph.

Though his example is more of an experimental prototype than enterprise product, its general pitch could be similar to that of DuckDB itself—huge and complex data platforms are overpowered for most of what we need them to do.
The only thing better than being a category creator is being the pacesetter in a category that everyone already buys from.
posts.key_insight: The warehouse has become a legacy label for a shifting set of capabilities, and the next “warehouse” may look more like dbt-driven compute on object storage than a monolithic database.

posts.summary: Stancil contrasts JPMorgan’s massive data investment with a hypothetical NBA team’s analytics push to argue that data initiatives work when they’re aimed at clear, concrete problems, and fail when they’re driven by hype and vague promises. He’s skeptical of open-ended “digital transformations,” but sees value in the messy experimentation of the data boom, even if much of it proves wasteful.
money_quotes: Their investment is, as the cliche goes, a $12 billion solution in search of a problem, inspired, it seems, by fear and FOMO.
My skepticism about JPMorgan isn’t exactly about data; it’s about direction.
Open-ended data initiatives suffer from, as Gwen Windflower put it in her new blog, an intention deficit.
Ultimately, that will be the legacy of the modern data stack: the data industry’s creative phase.
Our inventions are real, even if the money that created them was artificial.
posts.key_insight: Data investments deliver value only when tied to clear hypotheses and problems, but even wasteful experiments can yield lasting innovations.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the SVB bailout was justified either to prevent systemic panic or to protect startups deemed uniquely vital, but that second rationale implicitly demands special treatment that Silicon Valley doesn’t want to admit. He suggests SVB may be a “Lehman moment” for tech, exposing a subprime-like financial scaffolding that could trigger regulatory reckoning and shows how risky innovation finance has become.
money_quotes: The acute danger of this crisis, then, wasn’t that some people would be fired, though that would indeed be bad for those people; it was who would be fired.
But Tan’s appeal has one clear but unsaid implication—that Silicon Valley deserves special treatment.
In this way, SVB’s collapse really could be Silicon Valley’s Lehman moment, not because it leads to some immediate catastrophe, but because it catalyzes a dramatic regulatory shift.
Squint, and Silicon Valley's financial scaffolding has a lot of similar contours.
If we want to keep the tech ecosystem’s innovation engine going—if it really is too innovative to fail—we also need to make sure its financial engine doesn't become too risky to exist.
posts.key_insight: The SVB episode reveals a tension between Silicon Valley’s claim to exceptional importance and its resistance to the regulation that such exceptional treatment would logically require.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that OpenAI’s real destiny isn’t to become a consumer app platform like the iPhone, but an AI infrastructure provider akin to AWS. Generative models will become a ubiquitous, mostly invisible backbone powering countless products, and the biggest prize is dominance as a foundational utility rather than direct user attention.
money_quotes: Because public AI providers like OpenAI aren’t destined to become the next iPhone, but the next—and maybe, much bigger—AWS.

The utilities are building materials, and cloud providers are the internet's hardware stores—they sell pre-cut lumber and boxes of nails and sandpaper of dozens of different grains, but don’t offer birdhouses or lawn furniture or two-story houses.
In ten years, a new type of cloud—a generative one, a commercial Skynet, a public imagination—will undergird nearly every piece of technology we use.
Public AI providers can do both.
The real war isn’t for users, but for the public imagination.
posts.key_insight: OpenAI’s highest-leverage path is to become the generative infrastructure layer of the internet, not a consumer-facing app platform, because control of the backend yields scale, lock-in, and enormous power.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that as LLMs proliferate, they will sort into “low-skill” and “high-skill” tiers much like human labor markets, with cost and capability gaps driving differentiation. This could create new forms of inequality and competitive advantage, as premium models compound small advantages over time, even if baseline tools become cheap and widespread.
money_quotes: The introduction of Dolly suggests that LLMs and other generative AI models are also “skilled,” and could be divided along the same crude lines that economists use to divide the labor force.
These high-skill models are likely to be more expensive to employ than their less-skilled counterparts.
Employing a marginally better AI “workforce” might not make a discernible difference in how a company operates from day to day, but those benefits could accrue into huge gaps over time.
The reality, however, seems much more complicated—and much weirder.
Whatever happens, it feels like one thing is pretty clear: We’re barreling towards a very strange future.
posts.key_insight: LLMs are likely to form stratified labor markets where marginal capability differences create compounding economic and social advantages.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the modern data stack, once the corrective to Hadoop-era pain, has grown into an overgrown ecosystem of tools, costs, and complexity. He suggests we are entering a liminal period where AI/LLMs will trigger a new discontinuity, making the modern data stack’s philosophy ill-suited for the next era. The core claim is that data teams and vendors must adapt to AI-native infrastructures or risk becoming yesterday’s stack.
money_quotes: The punchline is the entire modern data stack.
We had 99 problems, so we used the modern data stack—and now we have 100 problems.
I now believe we’re in the liminal space between two eras.
Artificial intelligence, it seems, will be the next discontinuity.
For the modern data stack, this is it.
posts.key_insight: The modern data stack’s tool-heavy philosophy is peaking and will likely be eclipsed by AI-driven “intelligent infrastructure” that reshapes how data systems and teams are built.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that dbt Labs’ success rests as much on its community and human-centered approach as its product, but the market tailwinds that protected it are fading. He lays out three futures—stay the course, go on the offensive into adjacent markets, or sell—while noting that the rise of LLM-driven data stacks could reshape what “dbt” needs to be.
money_quotes: Though dbt Labs' success is impressive, this bubbling appreciation from its users is what makes it truly unique.
Building a company at this pace is a marathon of sprints.
Everyone’s friends until we’re hungry, desperate, and there’s blood in the water.
I’m increasingly becoming convinced that the entire modern data stack is on the cusp of getting rebuilt for our AI overlords.
Wall Street doesn't care about how much goodwill dbt Labs has; it doesn’t care about Slack members, or Github stars, or meetups hosted.
posts.key_insight: dbt’s edge has been its human-centered community-driven model, but shifting market dynamics and AI-driven architectures may force it to either expand aggressively or find a strategic exit.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that analysts can resemble “nicer, kinder” Jared Kushners: parachuting into others’ problems with shallow context and overconfident analysis that adds little value. He suggests shifting data teams from decision-support consultants to newsroom-style publishers who build company-wide, ambient understanding through continual narrative updates. A better-informed organization, not better point decisions, is the real competitive edge.
money_quotes: A crash course, no matter how intense and thorough, would be a poor substitute for just reading the news for twenty years.
Our involvement just isn’t that useful, and we’re mostly saying things they already know.
Dashboards lack narratives, and those narratives are often more important than the metrics themselves.
Instead of being researchers and scientists who make the news, data teams could be journalists and editors who report on it.
Without curation, publication, and distribution, this could, counterintuitively, make a company less informed.
posts.tone: reflective, critical, and analytical with humor
posts.key_insight: Data teams should prioritize publishing continuous, narrative context that keeps everyone informed, rather than parachuting into discrete decisions with shallow analysis.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the “two-step startup” is a myth: companies rarely evolve into a fundamentally different second act, and their founding product definition tends to persist from seed to IPO. Because partial pivots mostly fail, founders and investors should be brutally honest about whether the original idea can scale or must be fully rebuilt. Data dashboards won’t surface this truth; the real signal is felt in conviction and customer pull.
money_quotes: In other words, the two-step startup is a myth.
Put differently, for a business to grow into something big, its initial product has to grow into something big.

The gut check for founders and early-stage investors, then, is this: Do you believe that the product description in the VentureBeat writeup about the seed round can also be the first sentence on an S-1?
Partial pivots, by contrast, are a prolonged death march.
Startups don’t die because leaders miss these feelings; they die because teams ignore them.
posts.key_insight: If the seed-stage product description can’t plausibly be the IPO description, a startup needs a full pivot—because partial pivots only prolong failure.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that today’s “universal semantic layers” focus on metric extraction and accessibility via APIs, but that makes them effectively BI tools without charts rather than truly universal layers. Because data consumption preferences are diverse and tools require different computational models, a semantic layer that only serves metric tables can’t support the broader ecosystem. A real universal layer would combine shared entities with flexible computation like SQL, and he’s skeptical current approaches can do that yet.
money_quotes: Presenting data in this way—in a tidy, consumable, and constrained format that’s accessible to everyone—is typically what BI tools are for.

In other words, today’s independent semantic layers aren’t universally accessible encodings of business logic that can be used by any downstream analytical application; they’re BI tools, without charts.
Our tooling preferences reflect fundamentally different models for interacting with data, and they can’t all be shoehorned into a framework of dimensions and measures.
So long as they operate as applications for governing metric extraction, they’ll only be able to serve products that primarily need to extract metrics.
If we’re going to build universal semantic layers, they have to be flexible enough—like SQL itself—to work with tools that present data in a range of different ways.
posts.key_insight: Metric-focused semantic layers solve BI-style reporting but fall short of being truly universal because they can’t support the diverse computational models that real data tooling demands.

posts.summary: Stancil uses the Kasparov–Deep Blue story and Sutton’s “bitter lesson” to argue that AI progress comes less from encoding human domain knowledge and more from scaling computation and search. He then applies that lens to data teams, warning that human-built semantic models may constrain impact and suggesting that more compute-centric approaches could uncover strategies we can’t easily see.
money_quotes: The bitter lesson is based on the historical observations that 1) AI researchers have often tried to build knowledge into their agents, 2) this always helps in the short term, and is personally satisfying to the researcher, but 3) in the long run it plateaus and even inhibits further progress, and 4) breakthrough progress eventually arrives by an opposing approach based on scaling computation by search and learning.
These programs are unnatural, but deadly superior.

Our business models and semantic layers are efforts to describe businesses as we see them; they are a “favored, human-centric approach,” and are our methods—as opposed to the methods—for reasoning about difficult and tangled problems.
Instead, perhaps we should be trying to remove ourselves and our semantics as much as possible, and let AI agents work on problems on their own terms.
The space between our decisions and the optimal decisions in business domains—in marketing operations, in corporate finance, in sales strategy—must be immense.
posts.key_insight: Data teams may be stalling by over-relying on human-built abstractions, and greater gains may come from compute-heavy approaches that search beyond our semantic assumptions.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the most disruptive impact of generative AI is not that content becomes free, but that it becomes immediate, which changes how we seek and use information. That shift will erode search-driven, click-based web behavior and favor direct answers, known destinations, and on-demand interfaces, reshaping products like data catalogs and the broader web.
money_quotes: In other words, price changes mostly affect how much of something we consume.
Speed changes how we consume it-and the effects of that can be much more profound.
If a computer can manufacture documentation on the fly, what's the point of writing it down at all?
But if content is free to create and can be created immediately, people stop searching.
Unless this AI circus is a giant bubble, my guess is that neither of those assumptions will be true for much longer.
posts.key_insight: The internet will change most because AI makes content instantaneous, collapsing the need for search-based discovery and static documentation in favor of direct, on-demand answers.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the modern data stack’s promise of modular, user-driven tools has been undermined by the absence of a true platform—there’s “no potato” to hold the pieces together. Microsoft Fabric responds by collapsing the stack into a single integrated product, which could appeal to enterprises and shift the market from “legacy vs. modern” to “all‑in‑one vs. best‑of‑breed.” He concludes that the real opportunity is a neutral platform layer that enables genuine modularity, ideally from a cloud provider building infrastructure rather than a monolithic suite.
money_quotes: But this plan has always had one big problem: There is no potato.
The modern data stack has never had a platform.

Ten years in, this the legacy of the modern data stack: Dozens of creative and delightful features, often buried inside of overlapping and overweight products, all clumsily bumping into each other in a soup of startups and data services.
Fabric isn’t a suite of loosely connected services; it’s a single application that has one login experience, one user interface, one storage layer, one permission model, and one monthly bill.
The choice is no longer legacy versus modern, but all-in-one versus best-of-breed.
posts.key_insight: Without a shared platform layer, the modern data stack collapses into bloated, overlapping products—and Fabric’s integrated bet forces the market to choose between seamless consolidation and true best‑of‑breed modularity.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that most talks are forgettable and audiences expect to be bored, which creates a low-risk environment for bold choices. Because cautious and daring talks are equally ignored, the best strategy is to gamble on entertainment, pace, and narrative cohesion. The goal is to keep attention and create a memorable experience rather than deliver didactic takeaways.
money_quotes: A daring talk that flops and a cautious talk that safely sticks to all the comfortable best practices are equally ignored.
They’re movies, not documentaries.
Give me a talk that keeps me on my toes.
Like great rap songs, they expect their listeners to keep up.
Because most talks are performative busywork: The speaker gives it for themselves, either for their resume, their ego, or to hear themselves talk.
posts.key_insight: Since audiences already expect talks to be boring, speakers should take creative risks that prioritize entertainment and momentum over safe, instructional delivery.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that data teams over-invest in validating inputs and lineage, yet trust in metrics actually comes from output consistency over time. Because users judge credibility by whether numbers stay stable across reports, he proposes tools and practices that surface changes and drift in outputs rather than just monitoring pipelines and tests.
money_quotes: No, everyone else uses Method 2: “Do I believe this number, given what I believed yesterday?”
In other words, trust isn’t built by confirming our sources; it’s built via mathematical induction.
To trust the output, test the output.
The point is to reframe the problem around validating outputs instead of inputs.
Because in data, there are only two possible constants: Consistent metrics or consistent questions.
posts.key_insight: Trust in metrics comes less from perfect pipelines and more from visibly consistent outputs over time, so data tooling should highlight change and drift in results.

posts.summary: Stancil tests whether polite prompts improve ChatGPT performance and finds the opposite: neutral phrasing yields the best accuracy, while nice or mean tones slightly degrade results and increase verbosity. Despite the data, he frames the choice to be polite as a Pascal’s Wager: a tiny performance cost might be worth it if kindness matters in a future where AI could punish us.
money_quotes: The verdict: Nice guys finish last.
When asked to do something in a neutral tone, ChatGPT correctly completed the task 73.4 percent of the time.
Being nice has other negative effects as well.
Anybody who uses ChatGPT has to place a similar wager—do we choose to treat it well, or not?
Yes, there are thirty basis points of accuracy to be had by being unkind to ChatGPT, but there’s eternal bliss to had by being nice to it.
posts.key_insight: Neutral prompts slightly outperform polite or harsh ones, but he jokes that politeness may still be rational if you treat it as a low-cost hedge against a sci‑fi future.

posts.summary: Stancil uses microwaves as a metaphor for data: ubiquitous, useful, but potentially capped in transformative value. He argues data tooling may have hit a plateau as a utilitarian appliance, and that only a shift to fast, natural‑language access (via LLMs) could unlock a true second act by empowering many more people to ask and answer questions.
money_quotes: What’s notable about this story is that it’s not declaring the microwave dead; to the contrary, the microwave is everywhere. It’s obituary for the microwave’s potential.
There’s a different future that’s also out there: One in which data is everywhere, but the revolution is nowhere.
In our 72 attempts to redefine BI, we still haven’t broken the dashboarding barrier.
The only interface that will let people get the answers fast enough and painlessly enough to break this cycle is natural language.
If there’s value in our data, it’s the people that will find it.
posts.key_insight: Data may be stuck as a commodity appliance unless LLM‑driven, natural‑language BI enables mass participation in analysis.

posts.summary: Stancil reflects on how a decade of startup ambition and workaholism strained his relationship with his parents, costing them time and attention he never gave back. With Mode’s sale, he pauses his usual arguments to offer a heartfelt apology and gratitude, acknowledging their sacrifices and affirming the personal meaning of what was built.
money_quotes: So I said it wouldn’t cost me anything. But I didn't say what it would cost you.
I lived my life one fiscal quarter at a time, convinced that things would calm down in three months.
There are only two people who always called, even when I didn’t answer.
Two hundred million dollars is not, here in Silicon Valley, all that much money; we obsess over billions here, not millions.
And it has made a very lasting impact on me.
posts.key_insight: The true cost of startup success is often paid in neglected relationships, making gratitude and repair as important as the outcome.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that we conflate “databases” with structured, analyzable data, while the far larger opportunity is an “informationbase” that indexes and retrieves useful facts, documents, and context without heavy modeling. Snowflake’s Document AI hints at a shift from data warehousing toward Google‑like information retrieval, even if the most likely explanation is simply customer demand. The broader point is that search and retrieval for operational questions may matter more to most organizations than analytics-grade data pipelines.
money_quotes: A database isn’t just a collection of information; it’s a tightly organized catalog of capital-D Data.
Google’s “database” is none of these things.

We usually want information more than we want data, and the appeal of a chatbot to tell us the answer we want, without having to do the complicated work of retrieving and interpreting the data ourselves.
Any technology that makes solving those problems a prerequisite to using it is a technology that’ll be out of reach for most people.
So the real conclusion from Summit may be that: Though they’re doing it more slowly than Microsoft, Snowflake is gradually building a bomb of their own.
posts.key_insight: The most valuable next step for data platforms may be indexing and retrieving information, not just storing analyzable data.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that most SQL chatbots are aiming at the wrong problem: LLMs can draft queries in toy schemas, but real-world analysis requires extensive context, governance, and BI-like infrastructure. He contends that LLMs are better at creative, hypothesis-generating work than at precise, deductive querying, which flips the traditional promise that tech automates the tedious parts so humans can do the fun parts.
money_quotes: For bots to be successful query writers—and even harder, for them to be proper analysts that can answer questions about a business—LLMs will likely only be a small part of the solution.
Put differently, the LLM isn’t an application, or even close to one.
More bluntly, LLMs and query writing are fundamentally mismatched.
Because so far, one of the most striking things about LLMs is that they’re much better at the creative parts of analysis than they are at the mechanical parts.
In other words, it inverted the workflow between human and computer.
posts.key_insight: LLMs are ill-suited to reliable query generation at scale but excel at creative analytical reasoning, so chatbots risk automating the fun parts while leaving humans with the drudgery.

posts.summary: Stancil uses the ThoughtSpot–Mode merger to explore how acquisitions in BI can fail, arguing that strategic fit is less important than protecting user experience, respecting differing product philosophies, and acknowledging market shifts. He warns that integration traps, mismatched customer needs, suite consolidation, and AI-driven paradigm shifts are the real risks, while the biggest failures often come from well-known, unaddressed weaknesses.
money_quotes: Data consumption tools aren’t infrastructure; they’re productivity tools.

Protecting that experience across the two products has to be the first priority of any acquisition like this; enhancing it has to be the second; and neatly sorting through the technical details of how to do it has to be a distant third.
As a company’s promise grows, its outcomes become more binary—IPO or bust; runaway success or cautionary flop; generational triumph or catastrophic collapse.
If any of these things happen, the deep moat of features that protects today’s BI tools’ would suddenly become a cage, trapping us all in the past as the world moved on.
When you’re in the arena, everyone already knows what’s wrong—and it’s courage that you need, not cleverness.
posts.key_insight: Acquisitions in BI succeed or fail less on headline strategy and more on preserving product experience, respecting customer philosophy, and confronting obvious operational risks amid looming platform shifts.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that clean data is less a solvable engineering problem than a tradeoff that teams often choose, because businesses evolve faster than their data systems can. Tools like SDF help mitigate issues, but they are edge improvements that don’t change the messy, exploratory nature of data work. He suggests shifting from chasing stability to designing for resiliency and easier refactoring.
money_quotes: Data quality is a tradeoff.

Companies’ data infrastructures—including those at many young startups—are often terminally messy, full of brittle scripts, manually updated CSVs, and “pipelines” that involve transferring an Excel file from one computer to another with a USB drive.
The mess has a purpose: the development process looks less like an organized construction site and more like an artist’s studio or a writer’s desk.
It’s not a data reliability tool; it’s a data resiliency tool.
The other approach—leaving it all up to Jeff—doesn’t seem to be working.
posts.key_insight: Clean data isn’t a permanent destination; the realistic goal is building systems that tolerate change and make messy realities easier to debug and refactor.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the business world has overcorrected against intuition by treating emotions as inadmissible evidence, even though feelings often act as leading indicators of what data will later confirm. He contends that companies should surface emotions alongside analytics to make more honest decisions, avoid self-deception, and unlock bolder moves. Emotions shouldn’t drive choices blindly, but they should inform them as real signals.
key_points: Correlation and emotion are imperfect but useful leading indicators that should not be dismissed as mere noise.
Analytical culture often masks emotion with quantitative jargon, which distorts decision-making rather than improving it.
Teams ignore gut signals at their peril; many failures stem from trusting spreadsheets over instincts.
Openly acknowledging emotions can make discussions more honest and reduce hidden bias in decision-making.
Emotions provide conviction and courage that data alone cannot supply.
money_quotes: Correlation may not imply causation, but it’s almost always its leading indicator.
There’s a lot of signal in our emotions, even if it’s not scientifically robust or “statistically significant.”
They die because they assume their numbers are right and their emotions are wrong.
The best companies and leaders aren’t driven by emotions, but they don’t ignore them either.
But the next great idea is in these feelings—and data alone will never give us the courage to move on them.
posts.key_insight: Treat emotions as informative signals alongside data, because honest, courageous decisions require both analysis and feeling.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the data industry’s core pain isn’t tooling or process but the need to work on layered caches; unlike software, data pipelines rarely run live end-to-end. If instant, on-demand processing were possible, reliability, debugging, and trust would improve dramatically, but until then, copying software engineering practices only helps at the margins.
money_quotes: In data development, people are working off of an infinitely splintering collection of caches.
Every number or chart we see is a cache on a cache on a cache.
The entire job is practically cache management.
If everything could be updated immediately, most of these problems would go away.
Until we have our superconductor, we can’t actually work like engineers.
posts.key_insight: The data stack’s single point of failure is its reliance on cached, stepwise processing, and only near-instant end-to-end computation would make data work as reliable as software engineering.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that most LLM analytics chatbots are misaligned with real analytical work because they answer requests without the context needed to produce useful or correct outputs. He proposes a “smol analyst” workflow—iterative, feedback-driven drafting like a junior analyst—which could make LLMs more practical, while noting that data’s need for transparent logic makes this harder than in software.
money_quotes: If LLMs do end up changing how we use computers, this type of approach—one that introduces new ways of accomplishing some task rather than accelerating old ways; the car rather than the faster horse—feels like a glimpse of the future.
If we have to ask a bunch of follow-up questions before we dig up some number for people, why are we excited about LLMs that mechanically do exactly that?
Instead of asking for answers, people describe the report or dashboard that they want to create.
In data, black boxes don’t work.
The only way to know for sure what a query or Python script does is by reading the query or Python script.
posts.tone: analytical and skeptical with a reflective, lightly humorous edge
posts.key_insight: LLM analytics will be most useful when treated like junior analysts in an iterative feedback loop, but their outputs still require transparency and review because data work cannot tolerate black boxes.

posts.summary: Stancil uses a bar-pricing parable to argue that pricing models are economically flexible but socially constrained by expectations. He contends that dbt’s usage-based pricing is theoretically sensible and incentive-aligned, yet the real conflict is with entrenched norms from its free/open-source roots, which will shape who it ultimately builds for.
money_quotes: Pricing is like that.
We mostly accept pricing as a matter of precedent.
A social one, about pricing expectations.
If a product is already useful but people are used to getting it for free, trying to claim that value is a very delicate line to walk.
If the id of a man is in his bar, the id of a company is in its customers.
posts.key_insight: The “right” pricing model is less about economics than expectations, and those expectations determine which customers a company will ultimately serve.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the stigma around down rounds is exaggerated and often serves VC incentives more than startup interests. While down rounds can hurt employees, the real damage is usually optics-driven: fear of reputational harm pushes founders into worse terms and riskier bets than they should take.
money_quotes: The greatest trick that VCs ever pulled is convincing startups that down rounds are a disaster.
The biggest problem with a down round is the optics of a down round.
What’s good for the ecosystem may not be good for individual companies within it.
Stigmatizing down rounds protects that. It’s the side-scrolling left wall of Silicon Valley, always pushing startups forward to chase something bigger.
If nothing else, I do think there’d be one advantage of buying over leasing: It encourages us to stop shopping, and invest in what we have.
posts.key_insight: Down rounds are often less financially disastrous than their stigma implies, and the fear around them mainly serves VC incentives rather than startup realities.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the modern data stack is consolidating not just in vendor count but in category count: buyers are compressing spending and pushing tools to become broader, more BI-like platforms. As a result, specialized tools either get absorbed, stagnate as niche players, or rebrand into wider enterprise suites, and $10M ARR or “category leader” status no longer signals inevitability.
money_quotes: People stopped looking for specialized tools to solve every problem, and instead started trying to stretch the ones they have across several categories at once.
Software categories aren’t defined by Gartner, or VCs, or marketing taglines; they’re defined by buyers.

My unrelenting belief is that every BI-adjacent product is likely bound for one of three destinations: Be subsumed into some bigger and unabashed BI platform; stall, and become a niche player serving a small market; or get acquired by a major SaaS business that wants to start selling better reporting.

In the data world, I don’t think that’s true anymore. The market is big enough, fragmented enough, and disparate enough that getting to $10 million simply doesn’t—or didn’t, at least—have that much signal anymore.
So what makes a data company inevitable? Honestly, it’s probably the same as it ever was: Repeatable enterprise sales, solving problems in proven categories.
posts.key_insight: Category boundaries in the data stack are collapsing under buyer-driven consolidation, making breadth and enterprise fit more important than niche differentiation or early revenue milestones.
