posts.summary: Stancil argues that the data industry’s “purple” era—where analytics people bridge business and engineering through bespoke insight-hunting—has not delivered reliable value, despite better tools and more talent. He suggests the real maturation path is toward standardized, accountant-like practices with shared metrics and rules (e.g., SOMA) that trade novelty for trust and institutional reliability.
money_quotes: Ok, look. It’s 2023. If our issue is a skill shortage, it’s not an issue. It’s an excuse.

But for a job to be mainstream, it has to be able to hire mainstream talent. So if we still think our problem is that the talent we have can’t do the job we want them to do, the problem isn’t them; the problem is the job.
To create true institutional trust around the work that data teams do, we might need to do what accountants do: Give our work some rules.
We make progress as a discipline not by reasoning our way through our problems individually ... but through social inquiry, in which we can all stumble forward together.

Counterintuitively, we’d go further if we didn’t think of ourselves as intrepid explorers searching for novelty, but embraced our role as accountants—reliable, pedantic, and, actually, less boring than us.
posts.key_insight: The data profession will deliver more durable value by standardizing metrics and practices like accountants do, rather than chasing bespoke insights and perpetuating the myth that talent or tools are the main bottleneck.

posts.summary: The post argues that the common startup plan to win SMBs first and then graduate to enterprise rarely works as imagined, because enterprise sales take far longer, demand different products and buying motions, and can derail a company’s model. Stancil says most startups either need a path to scale within their initial buyer segment or start as enterprise-first from day one, rather than assuming a smooth transition later.
money_quotes: But it takes much longer, happens much later, and is a much more fitful and distracting journey than almost every startup thinks that it is.
In the best cases, interminably flirting with the enterprise is psychological torture.
Whatever segment powers a startup to $10 million in revenue needs to be able to power it to $100 million.
The buyers are too different, and products and brands are too hard to change.
On a long enough timeline, any company can sell a product to both small companies and big ones. But for most startups, that timeline is well beyond a horizon that’s worth planning for.
posts.key_insight: Assuming you can seamlessly pivot from SMB traction to enterprise revenue is a strategic error; either your initial segment must scale to $100M or you must build for enterprise from the start.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that there is a persistent gap between what data tools can do and what teams actually use, much like glossy Office “vision” videos versus real messy workflows. The problem is less about missing features and more about adoption, skepticism, and the lack of belief that investing in a tool’s full potential will pay off.
money_quotes: The dance isn’t a ballet; it’s a mosh pit.
Though teams could solve lots of their problems with Y42, very few will.
It’s as though we’re hungry, go into a buffet, and never bother to walk past the appetizer station.
Unless a new product hits us square in the face with an idea that’s dead simple to test or obviously revolutionary, we won’t believe its potential.
There’s no sense in building more product that won’t get used. And people won’t use products unless they believe in them.
posts.key_insight: The hardest problem for modern data tools is not capability but belief-driven adoption—teams rarely invest enough to realize the tools’ full potential.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the popular story that venture capital “ruins” good products is incomplete and often misattributes cause. The real degradation comes not from the cash itself but from the growth imperative that VC financing imposes, which pushes companies to expand features and markets beyond a product’s natural fit.
money_quotes: Venture capital doesn’t ruin products—it ruins this myth.
The problem with the narrative that VC money ruins products is that it has to ruin them through some mechanism.
Because VCs don’t just invest in startups to make money; they invest in startups to make money by selling their equity for more than they paid for it.
There is no cruising altitude for a venture-backed startup. The options are to fly higher, or stall trying.
As Randy Au pointed out earlier this week, that’s what ruins products. It encourages companies to push them beyond what they are naturally suited to do.
posts.key_insight: VC funding doesn’t inherently worsen products; the need to sustain rapid growth does, by forcing expansion beyond a product’s natural scope.

posts.summary: Stancil pauses his planned data post to acknowledge the conflict affecting readers in Israel and Palestine and express hope for their safety. He offers the platform to those directly impacted, inviting them to share stories or perspectives.
money_quotes: I hope that all of you are safe, that your family and friends are safe, and that the people driving what is happening in both Israel and Gaza insist on mercy for the innocent as much as they do justice for the guilty.
That’s all I have to say today—the planned post can wait.
This week, if you’re one of the 151 people in the middle of this spiraling nightmare, the microphone is yours.

If you’re currently living in Israel or Palestine, and you have a story you want to share with the people who read this blog, or a perspective that everyone is missing… email me, and we’ll figure it out.
posts.key_insight: In a moment of crisis, he prioritizes empathy and amplifying affected voices over publishing a planned data post.

posts.summary: Stancil uses Coalesce in San Diego as a lens to argue that the data industry is maturing away from hype and toward process-heavy, often boring work, with AI notably absent. That shift strips away the “main-character” career narrative and forces practitioners to ask whether they’re in the field for the craft or for the promised spotlight. He hopes the community keeps making room for human celebration even as the industry professionalizes and attention moves on.
money_quotes: There were no AI-powered features.
The problems that data teams deal with aren’t likely to be solved by some fancy flourish of AI, or by a wrapper around ChatGPT.
Our main-character strut is turning into a slump: At Coalesce, several VCs told me they weren’t very excited about any of the early-stage data startups that they’d met recently.
Do I actually want to do any of those things?
What’s special is giving people who are increasingly working in the dark their moment in the light.
posts.key_insight: As data work shifts from hype to legwork, the industry must decide whether it values the craft itself or merely the status it once promised.

posts.summary: The post assembles firsthand accounts from people in Israel and Gaza to show how the war is devastating daily life, safety, and work across both communities. It argues that beyond the headlines, the human cost is vast and intimate, and that the least we can do is acknowledge the tragedy and offer practical support.
money_quotes: Both Israelis and Palestinians are crushed right now.
There are no winners in war.
What truly makes me feel desperate and hopeless is the amount of people around the world that are celebrating what happened to us.
It’s not complicated to not kill kids.
Just acknowledging that something horrible has happened (and is happening) is actually really, really important.
posts.key_insight: By centering personal testimonies and concrete ways to help, the post frames the war as a shared human tragedy that demands empathy and practical support, not silence.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that founders are consistently surprised by startup difficulty because the culture overemphasizes clever wedges and underestimates the years of unglamorous work required to build durable companies. The only enduring moat is sustained effort: you can hack attention or early growth, but lasting advantage comes from doing the boring, direct, head‑to‑head work better than incumbents.
money_quotes: The only true shortcuts are lots of steroids or lots of crimes.
We can’t outrun the work; not forever.
Big businesses aren’t built on gimmicks.
The strategy was to do the work.
We’ll make Mailchimp, but faster; but better designed; but with more dedicated and relentless customer support.
posts.tone: analytical and reflective, with a critical edge
posts.key_insight: Startups feel surprising hard because founders bet on clever hacks, but real, durable success comes only from sustained, unglamorous execution.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that most enterprise AI features are being rushed out for hype, but real usefulness demands messy, unglamorous infrastructure work that many companies won’t sustain, echoing the blockchain cycle. He then contrasts BI tools with spreadsheets, claiming Excel’s static, file-based nature is its moat, and closes with a satirical take on privatizing productivity gains inside companies.
money_quotes: It’s all handstands again—months of daily practice, and not a two-week sprint.
Unless the company is existentially bound making these features work, it’s easy to imagine the various chatbots and assistants slowly spiraling into decay.
What makes Excel (and Google Sheets) unique from BI is that it’s not just a tool for working with data; it is the data.
That makes replacing Excel a catch-22: If you build a tool that solves its biggest problems—being disconnected and static—you’re suddenly building a BI tool and not Excel.
But I love a fun scheme, and would be very excited if someone tried it.
posts.key_insight: Most AI add-ons will decay without sustained, non-glamorous engineering effort, just as past tech hypes did, while Excel’s very limitations are what keep it irreplaceable.

posts.summary: Benn argues that 2021-era data startup valuations were inflated by stacked multipliers—market exuberance, community hype, and category narratives—that have since collapsed as rates rose and sentiment cooled. He says many companies can’t “grow into” those valuations, so the most plausible path is opportunistic fundraising that preserves the signal of success even if the market fundamentals are weaker.
money_quotes: In 2021, investors were willing to pay premiums for looser, more atmospheric, more gameable, and less monetizable signs of progress.
Multiply it all together—the default 50x 2021 multiple, a 5x category multiplier, a 5x community multiplier—and Airbyte’s $1 million in revenue is worth $1.25 billion.
The compression math is brutal.
Private companies aren't valued by the aggregated opinion of the market, but by a single highest bidder.
Good VCs are humble advisors, bad VCs are overconfident micromanagers, and useful VCs tell the blunt stories that make sure startups fully face what’s probably coming next.
posts.key_insight: The post’s core insight is that 2021’s stacked valuation multipliers for data startups have unwound, so the only way to sustain lofty prices is to find a single bidder willing to buy a narrative.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the OpenAI boardroom drama crystallized existing AI disagreements into tribal identity politics, where siding with Altman became a proxy for accelerationist allegiance rather than reasoned debate. He then contends that Altman’s mythos is less about unique merit and more about whose stories get amplified, urging public recognition of underappreciated people.
money_quotes: No, the masses sided with Altman—and now, accelerationism more generally—because Silicon Valley is our team; OpenAI is “ours;” Sam Altman is one of us.
E/acc is a brand—a flag—not a philosophy that every reply guy researched before they add it to their Twitter handle.
Now, there is dissent, there is The Way, and there is a blind march into the unknown.

What is unique about Altman—what separates him from the other people in Silicon Valley who are also irrepressibly kind and unrelentingly helpful, who are also there when nobody is looking—is that Altman’s had his stories told.
Don’t tell your friends how great they are; tell the world how great your friends are.
posts.key_insight: The OpenAI saga turned AI debate into tribal loyalty, and Altman’s stature reflects story-amplified reputation more than singular distinction.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the real power of data is not its objectivity but its aggregatability, which makes structured numbers easy to summarize while rich qualitative information remains costly to synthesize. He contends that LLMs can act as an “aggregate function” for text, unlocking the value of unstructured data that traditional data stacks ignore. The future of data platforms, he suggests, is less about squeezing more out of structured data and more about making qualitative corpora computable.
money_quotes: In other words, data hasn’t been put on a corporate pedestal because its contents are more valuable than the contents of customer interviews, or support tickets, or online reviews.
It’s been put there because, unlike qualitative observations, which can only be seen one at a time, data can be summed into an elephant.
That, I think, is actually what makes data powerful. We can perform mathematical operations on it.
With LLMs, there are? In some rough sense, LLMs train themselves on libraries of text, and then regurgitate an approximate average of what they read.
It’ll come from pointing AI at a new well—the unstructured video interviews in Dropbox—and letting us do the basic things with it that we’ve never been able to do before.
posts.tone: analytical and critical, with a reflective edge
posts.key_insight: The real breakthrough in data platforms will come from using LLMs to aggregate and analyze unstructured text, not from further optimizing traditional structured data pipelines.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that Microsoft’s Fabric and Databricks’ MosaicML deal show the modern data stack is too small to be the real battleground; the bigger fight is for the next “higher-order bit” in AI infrastructure. Databases and MDS tools can’t win by eating smaller apps because cloud providers still sit above them, so vendors are repositioning to become AI model infrastructure providers where access to data is the key advantage.
money_quotes: In the hierarchy of things you want to sell, databases aren’t actually the cash cow, but the middle fish—bigger than the apps, but smaller than the cloud.
If database vendors want to build durable defenses against their larger predators, eating the modern data stack doesn’t help all that much.

The real money in AI, then, may not be in foundational model providers who sell inference via API, but in the services that actually look much more like today’s cloud providers—offering metered infrastructure that’s designed to help companies build custom models, fine-tune open source ones, and run both of them.

In other words, had Databricks acquired an ETL or BI tool, they’d be a database eating a smaller fish. In MosaicML, they bought what they hope becomes a bigger fish that will one day eat the database.
And if it doesn’t want to get passed by—or outright eaten—the modern data stack is gonna need a bigger boat.
posts.key_insight: The modern data stack’s consolidation is a sideshow; the real strategic move is building AI infrastructure where data-rich vendors can climb above the database layer.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that ChatGPT, like Google before it, rewards intuition and practice more than formal tricks, and that its real value today is speeding up tedious data work rather than generating novel analytical insight. Using Seth Stephens‑Davidowitz’s 30‑day AI‑assisted book as a case study, he says AI will flood us with faster, shakier analyses, making credibility and narrative framing even more decisive than the math itself.
money_quotes: People who are good at Googling aren’t good because they know that you search for keywords in URLs by using the “allinurl:” prefix; they’re good because they have an instinct for how Google works.
If we want to understand what our jobs might look like in several years, Who Makes the NBA? is the best answer we have yet.

In the end, most of the value that Stephens-Davidowitz got from ChatGPT was in accelerating the mundane and tedious tasks of cleaning and blending data, and in quickly cycling through dozens of regressions and pivots of those datasets.
Analysis isn’t an irrefutable science, but a kind of quantitative logical argument.
Just as Google made it possible to find an argument for anything, I suspect AI will eventually make it possible to create an argument for anything.
posts.key_insight: AI speeds analysis but doesn’t solve judgment, so credibility and narrative cues will matter even more as conclusions become cheaper to produce.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that Silicon Valley startups operate on a “scheme” where equity is valued by the next buyer rather than by steady profits, and that this dynamic shapes incentives from hiring to executive comp. The Alteryx sale illustrates the scheme faltering under higher rates and stricter markets, pushing companies toward a capital-allocator model that prioritizes cash flow over hype. He closes by skewering a viral Twitter “funding” lie as a quasi‑fraud enabled by social media’s incentive structure.
money_quotes: In Silicon Valley startups’ employees and investors, this is the whole scheme: Not to build a business that steadily makes a little bit of money for a long time, but to build a business that someone—another company, a private equity firm, public investors on the stock market—buys for a lot of money all at once.
The scheme is the whole reason most people are here, but the scheme doesn’t always work.
If I had to guess, that’s what this deal is all about: Insight and Clearlake saw a $900 million annual revenue stream that they can buy at a relative discount.

In this market—of higher interest rates, and governed by more aggressive anti-trust regulation—a company with those performance metrics is valuable because of the cash it can make, and not because of what it might eventually be worth.
The only question is whether they’ll be the master of their own game, or a pawn in someone else’s.
posts.key_insight: When the venture “scheme” of selling future potential gets harder to run, companies like Alteryx are revalued as cash-generating assets, shifting power to capital allocators and exposing the incentives that built the startup model.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the value of news to OpenAI depends on whether LLMs become search-like or agent-like: copyrighted content is essential for an “omniscient” Q&A product but merely convenient for task-performing agents. The New York Times lawsuit is less about stopping AI and more about setting a lucrative legal precedent while the industry’s economic regime is still being defined.
money_quotes: If ChatGPT ultimately occupies the first role—a bot that does stuff; an agent—OpenAI doesn’t need copyrighted material.
The frontier of ChatGPT potential isn’t replacing Google, but in using Google.
In other words, for the enhanced search engine that OpenAI is today, copyrighted content is necessary.

The New York Times’ lawsuit probably won’t bankrupt OpenAI, but its foundational objective seems the same: To establish an economic and legal precedent that protects publishers if chatbots and LLMs replace search engines as the lobby to the internet, and still enriches them if they become trillion-dollar generative AI agents that no longer need the news.
So if you’re the Times, why not sue?
posts.key_insight: The fight over news licensing is really a battle to set the economic rules for AI while content is still a bottleneck.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that Carta’s doomed exchange (CartaX) wasn’t fundamentally different from Google’s data-driven ad marketplace, but it violated privacy expectations by making the human, personal act of outreach visible. The real failure was optics: people accept algorithmic data brokering at scale, yet recoil when a person appears to rifle through private records. He then generalizes to startup strategy, warning that “step two” visions often collapse because success at step one hardens a company’s identity and constraints.
money_quotes: The bigger problem, however, was about trust.
The difference between Google and Carta may not be so much in how they manage their exchanges, but in how they manage how their appearances.
For better or for worse, privacy and trust are probably like that. It matters how they look as much as how they work.
For CartaX, the only thing worse than step one failing was step one succeeding.
If you want to build an exchange, build an exchange.
posts.key_insight: Carta’s exchange collapsed less because its model was unique or unethical in kind, and more because the human-facing optics of data use shattered trust that algorithmic marketplaces usually avoid.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the modern data stack boom was driven as much by hype and attention-seeking as by genuine technical conviction, and that the post-ChatGPT shift to AI has hollowed out that “club.” The slowdown creates a better moment for durable startups to rebuild proven ideas more deliberately, while he also critiques the industry’s near-religious faith that “data” is inherently valuable.
money_quotes: The party was the point.
Hype cycles are great for building, but not great for enduring.
They just have to rebuild what’s already there, but more deliberately and on more certain ground than the original pioneers could.
This sort of message has become so ubiquitous that it’s become our water.
Wherever there’s data, there’s power—is the very thing that makes people believe.
posts.key_insight: The data-stack hype cycle is fading, and the real opportunity now is in rebuilding established ideas thoughtfully while questioning the industry’s faith that data is inherently valuable.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that corporate values are inherently vague and often serve as flexible, quasi-legal tools that can be weaponized by management or disputed by employees. The real issue isn’t that companies want values, but that these slogans obscure tradeoffs between profit and principle, creating ambiguity and conflict. He proposes replacing abstract values with concrete precedents that show how a company actually behaves under pressure.
money_quotes: Most for-profit companies have two sets of values.
Values often being framed as constitutional beliefs, they are actually squishy gestures at unknown and unknowable tradeoffs.
Only the former give both executives and employees the ability to pick and choose when and how to apply a rule to their liking.
Rather than making people deduce specific behaviors from a set of generalized values, give them the precedents and let them induce the principles for themselves.
People can say they will do anything; but nobody can escape what they’ve done.
posts.key_insight: Corporate values are less useful as slogans than as documented precedents that reveal how a company actually balances profit and principle in real situations.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that most dashboard-driven analysis is closer to roulette than science: we see patterns and invent narratives in essentially noisy data. He praises Cedric Chin’s simple, iterative method for reading operational data because it gives permission to ignore false complexity and focus on actionable variation. The post then pivots to influencer culture, framing brand value as a social association game where the “wrong” endorsers can erode or even perversely strengthen a brand.
money_quotes: Roulette is one giant dice roll.
It’s barely even science.
The better answer, I think, is that Chin’s post gives us permission to make this simple.
When you investigate everything, you find nothing; when you find nothing, you doubt your methods; and when you doubt your methods, you flail.
Much better to get paid for the things you don’t do.
posts.key_insight: Good analysis comes less from sophisticated techniques than from disciplined, simple observation of meaningful variation—and social perception games in branding operate by similarly irrational but powerful pattern‑seeking dynamics.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that data rarely transforms big, high-leverage decisions; it mostly nudges outcomes at the margins. The real advantage comes from finding small edges that can be repeated frequently, or better yet, using data to discover what you’re already slightly better at and then doing that more often so the edge compounds.
money_quotes: Data helps on the edges.

Don’t be intuition-driven or data-driven; be quantity-driven, and look exclusively for places where you can create a durable advantage through frequent, small—and in the aggregate, predictable—bets that you’ve tilted in your favor.
Even small improvements are hard to find.
Don’t use data to get good at something we do a lot; use data to figure out what we’re good at, and then do it more often.
Those advantages come from the boring compounding of small biases.
posts.key_insight: Data’s real power is not in heroic, one-off decisions but in identifying small, repeatable edges and multiplying them through frequency.

posts.summary: Stancil argues the modern data stack didn’t fail because the market turned; it failed because the product never converged on a shared, vivid vision of what the data experience should be. The early, clear goal of modernizing legacy tools drifted into vague promises of “data democratization,” leaving vendors building disjointed pieces without a Schelling point, so the ecosystem fragmented and stalled.
money_quotes: The problem wasn’t the market around the products we were building; the problem was the product itself.
Visionary products aren’t the manifestation of someone’s dream; they’re products that manifest the same dream in others.
There was no Schelling point to build towards; there were shills, pointing towards whatever thing they had built.
We weren’t just driving with no destination in mind; we also got stuck in our own traffic on the way there.
Modern data stack vendors chose speed, and never attempted to truly build something together.
posts.key_insight: Without a shared, tangible vision of the end-user experience, the modern data stack splintered into disconnected tools despite ample capital and momentum.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that natural language is a poor substitute for code when doing nuanced analysis, so LLMs shouldn’t directly write SQL for complex business questions. Instead, he sees AI as most valuable when it sits atop BI models or helps translate rough, mixed SQL/English sketches into precise queries, preserving guardrails and precision.
money_quotes: The obvious point here is that natural language isn’t always a particularly useful way to create something.
For certain types of output, English is crude, imprecise, and painfully inefficient.
There are thousands of computational devils in details like how to handle nulls.
I want to use a bad query to help me write a good query.
LLMs as an analyst, turning novel business questions into full queries? Seems unwise.
posts.key_insight: LLMs should augment analysts by tightening rough SQL or navigating BI models, not replace precise, code-based analysis with English prompts.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that secondary sales are not inherently unethical and that VCs’ moral posturing is inconsistent with how they actually reward founders who make money, even after failures. The real fairness issue isn’t whether founders sell, but whether employees—who bear the most concentrated risk—get transparency and a chance to judge or participate.
money_quotes: Because, for all of the hand-wringing on Sand Hill Road about this stuff, Silicon Valley is really just about making money.
Investors don't invest in nice people; they invest in monetizable people.
It is not enough for a founder to merely want their startup to succeed; they have to need it to succeed.
Secondary sales are fine, so long as everyone in the company is told about them.
Assume everyone will find out.
posts.key_insight: Secondary sales should be acceptable, but employees—not VCs—should be the ones to judge their fairness because they bear the most concentrated risk.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that while AI hype has pulled capital and leadership attention across the industry, most real winners so far are “shovel sellers” rather than companies getting rich by using AI, which makes the other side of the bet undervalued. He suggests that as the dust settles, some firms will have over-pivoted, creating opportunities for those who resist the rush. In a separate but related critique of startup finance, he argues liquidation preferences distort incentives more than secondary sales, misaligning founders and investors around exit outcomes.
key_points: AI investment has become a one-sided trade, with startups and incumbents alike piling in despite limited proven end-user riches.
The biggest AI gains so far accrue to infrastructure and platform sellers, not to downstream adopters.
Overcommitment to AI creates risk of strategic over-pivoting and leaves contrarian opportunities undervalued.
Liquidation preferences can be a rational market-clearing tool for mismatched valuations but often create perverse incentives.
The real damage from liquidation preferences is incentive misalignment across exit price ranges, not merely founder take-home losses.
money_quotes: Where is the gold? Where are the companies that have gotten rich using AI rather than selling AI?
But it’s still just a promise.
In other words, all the action is on one side of the line.
That’s the real issue with liquidation preferences.
For all of the histrionics about secondary sales distorting incentives, liquidation preferences are far worse.
posts.key_insight: AI’s hype has crowded the trade toward shovel sellers while liquidation preferences quietly create deeper incentive misalignments than most founders realize.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that Silicon Valley’s “meritocracy” resembles political primaries: elite insiders and influencers shape which companies get attention, capital, and talent long before markets can judge them. Using the Medium vs. Svbtle example and the Devin AI launch, he shows how hype from the right people creates momentum that becomes reality, even when few have direct experience with the product.
money_quotes: Silicon Valley sure seems to be driven by the exact same dynamic that people say drives presidential primaries: Elite insiders choosing who gets money and who doesn’t, influencing who can hire and who can’t, and tilting markets and (social) media narratives in favor of their preferred technologies, companies, and founders.
Medium’s momentum came from important people in Silicon Valley posting on it and posting about it on Twitter, and from the rest of us following them.
The point is that nobody has seen the emperor.
The reality is somewhere in the middle.
The party decides which companies win the early-stage primaries.
posts.key_insight: Silicon Valley outcomes are heavily shaped by influencer-led momentum, which creates early advantages that look like market selection but function more like insider-driven primaries.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that analytics teams often operate like a Ponzi scheme of promised future value: the discipline’s story of inevitable insight-driven impact rarely materializes for average teams. He suggests that since most decisions hinge on loose directional narratives rather than precise metrics, analytics may need to disband or rebrand—embedding in other functions and trading grand promises for practical influence.
money_quotes: As a practical point, however, I’m not sure how much these hypotheticals matter. The question is not whether such a world exists, but if we will ever get there?
If my life savings were on the line, my answer is that our hope is a mistake, and most analysts will never escape their current lot.
In short, my answer is that analytics—not as an industry or as a technology ecosystem, but as a discipline—might not work.
By talking primarily in the language of exact facts and figures, we make ourselves dismissible, because nobody really cares about the precise amount some metric went up or down.
Disband or rebrand—that may be our choice.
posts.key_insight: Analytics teams often survive on a narrative of future impact that rarely arrives, so their best path may be to reframe themselves around how decisions are actually made rather than doubling down on precision metrics.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that conferences rarely deliver real learning or authentic networking, often devolving into performative talks and repetitive social rituals. Still, he says they have a hidden value: they let you see many plausible versions of your future career all at once, helping you choose intentionally among finite paths.
money_quotes: The presenters aren’t there to teach, but to be recognized and celebrated; the audience isn’t there to become enlightened, but to feel enlightened.
Conferences aren’t just our professional connections and friends, all in one place; they’re also our parallel lives, all at one time.
That’s what conferences are for: For showing us how wide-ranging a profession can be, while reminding us it is not infinite.
If necessity is the mother of invention, boundaries are the mother of intention.
So as you leave, open the plane’s window. Look back; pay attention. Who are you becoming? Who do you want to become?
posts.key_insight: Conferences are less about learning or networking than about revealing the limited set of career trajectories you’re likely to follow, so you can choose one deliberately.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that organizations, like individuals, rely mostly on fast, intuitive “System 1” thinking, so analytics teams are misguided when they try to out-reason it with pure analysis. Instead of chasing isolated actionable insights, analysts should work with System 1 by surfacing and updating the organization’s underlying mental models. The goal is to reshape heuristics and theories that guide many decisions, not just provide one-off recommendations.
money_quotes: No matter how fast we are or how good our tools get, most decisions will be made using System 1; we can’t whack-a-mole them away, by putting an analyst in every room or a BI tool on every laptop.
If we are the company’s second system, we’ll always be second chair.
The much more powerful thing that we could do is teach a company a theory—or as Ahbi put it, our job shouldn’t be to look for insights, but to mutate mental models.
Don’t veto biased thinking; update the bias.
Theories, by contrast, ask people to see the world differently.
posts.key_insight: Analytics teams create more leverage by reshaping an organization’s underlying heuristics than by delivering isolated actionable insights.

posts.summary: Stancil uses a mock investor update for a fictional AI startup to satirize how startups contort narratives around growth, churn, and fundraising readiness. The piece argues that raising rounds to “pass the next grade” is a trap; money doesn’t fix a broken business, and founders should fundraise only when the current company can win the next stage.
money_quotes: Money gives them resources, but it doesn’t give them a new market, a new product, or a new idea.
A failing chatbot is still a failing chatbot, with or without the backing of some guy named David or Scott.
Whatever business gets a startup to their A needs to get them to their B.
Don’t raise money because you won the last race; raise money because you’re confident you can win the next race.
If foundercanclose an A round, butcan’t escape the feelingthat something will have to change to close the eventual B, don’t do the A.
posts.key_insight: Fundraising should validate that the current business can scale through the next stage, not mask fundamental weaknesses with fresh capital.

posts.summary: The post argues that the classic Silicon Valley story—software has near-zero marginal costs and therefore huge profits—breaks down when you account for the ongoing development needed to keep subscription software viable. High gross margins in SaaS mask the reality that continuous product updates function like cost of goods, keeping operating margins negative for many companies. Stancil calls for a new business theory that treats perpetual redesign as a core cost rather than a temporary growth investment.
money_quotes: But what if the theory is wrong? What if the most important cornerstone in Silicon Valley is actually a teetering liability?

And yet! According to Jamin Ball’s figures, the average public software-as-a-service company has gross margins of 74 percent—it only costs them a dollar to manufacture something they can sell for four dollars.
Despite their high gross margins, SaaS companies don’t just make less than other public companies; most of them actually lose money.
In other words, software development costs are COGS.

We should build a new theory that acknowledges that software can’t be sold unless it’s continually being redesigned and updated, that software margins aren’t actually that high, and that step three is mostly a theoretical fantasy.
posts.key_insight: SaaS profitability is overstated because continual product development is effectively a cost of goods, making the promised high margins and eventual windfall largely illusory.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that BI’s pendulum swing between generic platforms and domain-specific apps misses the real problem: companies struggle more with interpreting metrics than defining them. He suggests a third form of BI that is opinionated about interpretation, offering frameworks and guidance on what the numbers mean and what to do next.
money_quotes: Amazon isn’t Amazon because it’s great at defining metrics. It’s Amazon because it’s great at interpreting them.
We’ve spent thirty years building tools to help us answer our questions. The much harder thing is knowing what to do with those answers once we have them.
Most BI tools are the inverse. They govern inputs, and provide no guidance on how to make sense of the outputs.
If I had to guess, all of this is the real reason why BI tools are a tar pit: They solve the wrong problem.
Maybe it’s time to try another approach. Stop giving us more exploratory interfaces to get lost in. Stop waiting for us to become “data literate.”
posts.key_insight: The next useful wave of BI should be opinionated about interpreting results, not just flexible about defining metrics.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the SaaS delivery model incentivizes shipping incomplete products, with customers effectively doing QA, and that this pattern has bled into hardware via cloud-dependent AI devices. He suggests this creates customer fatigue and financial strain for SaaS firms, opening space for a countercultural model: software sold as finished, one-time-purchase products. He then pivots to a related puzzle—why software hasn’t become a luxury status symbol despite its craftsmanship and ubiquity.
money_quotes: Vendors ship incomplete software, the threshold for what makes a bad release slips a bit further, and the whole thing spirals in a slow devolutionary loop, until people are blaming reviewers for not liking useless garbage, because of some open-ended promise that it might one day not be useless.
Build something that you don’t have to keep building forever; sell the product based on what it is today and not based on what you can promise it’ll be in the future.
Ultimately, that’s the interesting question here: Will someone pull this off? Will someone build a big software company that isn’t a SaaS product?
Put differently, on this last point, why isn’t there status software?
Because hope isn’t optimism. Hope is a thing with features.
posts.key_insight: The SaaS model’s incentives to ship unfinished software may be unsustainable, creating an opening for non‑SaaS products and even new forms of software as status goods.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that traditional data analysis and user interviews miss a powerful third approach: attentive observation that captures patterns we didn’t think to ask about. He suggests transformer-style models applied directly to behavioral event sequences could scale this “watching and remembering,” potentially delivering the core value of analysis without the brittle machinery of metrics and dashboards.
money_quotes: You don't need to know how to define dancing to know when something is a banger.
It’s analysis by anecdote, math by memory, and inference by vibe.
The insights hidden in it like the Isla de Muerta: They can’t be found except by those who already know where they are.
It’s feeding patterns into a pattern-generation machine.
The numbers may have been the problem all along.
posts.key_insight: Transformer models trained on behavioral event streams could bypass brittle metrics and deliver the true goal of analysis: uncovering patterns we didn’t know to look for.

posts.summary: The post argues that most data products are designed around clean, demo-friendly narratives, but real businesses face messy data, noisy dashboards, and contradictory signals. Because products are built for the “happy path,” they fail users in the “bad place” where interpretation and deduction—not discovery—are the real challenges.
money_quotes: The dashboard is a horoscope, capable of telling whatever story its reader is looking for.
In a sense, every product is built for the happy path of Playful Pines, full of clean data that makes beautiful charts.
Most of us are Red Eye Entertainment, plagued by messy data, erratic metrics, and graphs with too many series.
More clues don’t help us know what to do; they just create more confusion.
We need help with deduction, not discovery.
posts.key_insight: Data products often optimize for clean demo narratives, but real-world data is messy and ambiguous, so tools should prioritize helping teams interpret and reconcile conflicting signals rather than just surface more insights.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that data products marketed as novel or specialized almost inevitably evolve into BI tools as customer demands, market incentives, and growth pressures pull them toward dashboards, self-serve access, and visualization. The real challenge isn’t clever differentiation but the stubbornness required to resist the gravitational pull of BI, even when it looks like the rational business choice.
money_quotes: In other words, no matter when a data product was created or what its first features were, it will inevitably collapse into the same singularity: Database connectors; a code-based query editor; a code-free query builder; lots of charts; dashboards; self-serve.
The biggest lesson, though, is that it's actually very easy to think different, but it’s very hard to stay different.
It is not having a vision that gets you labeled as a visionary; it’s refusing to let it go.
Similarly, you could build something that’s not BI, but you won’t do it by being clever. You’ll do it by being stubborn.
Even though you told yourself you weren’t going to build a BI tool, that’s where all the money was.
posts.key_insight: Differentiation in data tools collapses into BI unless founders stubbornly resist market pressure rather than merely crafting a clever pitch.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that dbt made data modeling easy but not simple, creating a generation of “analytics pretendgineers” who can write models but lack the architectural discipline to build durable data programs. He contends that the fix isn’t more features or education, but a framework with strong, opinionated guardrails—“dbt on Rails”—that enforces structure and prevents chaotic model sprawl.
money_quotes: dbt changed all this—mostly for the better, but a bit for the worse.
A hard thing became an easy thing.
In other words, dbt’s explosive popularity turned a generation of data analysts into analytics pretendgineers.
Behaviors are shaped by our environment and what that environment lets us do, not the other way around.
That’s what I want for dbt—either built into dbt Labs, or on top of it, like Django is on Python.
posts.key_insight: dbt lowered the barrier to building data models but, without enforced structure, it also lowered the quality of data-program design, so the real fix is opinionated frameworks rather than more tooling or training.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that Silicon Valley used to run on familiar boom-bust cycles, but the AI era is breaking the old “physics,” replacing predictable rhythms with frantic rebranding, uncertain value, and career anxiety. The result is a tech industry that outwardly projects confidence yet privately feels unmoored, unsure whether this is another wave or a structural shift.
money_quotes: In Silicon Valley, there are two sets of physics.
But in private, the vibe is different.
The puck is teleporting.
OpenAI events are the reaping.
There are no reliable physics anymore.
posts.key_insight: AI has turned Silicon Valley’s once-cyclical, legible boom-bust dynamics into a confusing, destabilizing moment where even veterans doubt the rules of the game.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that modern security and compliance practices are less about preventing breaches and more about allocating blame when they happen. Using Snowflake’s incident as a case, he says the cloud’s convenience shifts liability to users while vendors lean on audits and PR to claim innocence. He extends the “not my fault” logic to corporate behavior broadly, then to AI-era startup valuations driven by hype and talent rather than fundamentals.
money_quotes: That’s the thing about data breaches, and about the entire software security industrial complex: It’s not about security; it’s about blame.
Compliance standards aren’t about security; compliance standards are about liability.
In essence, Snowflake and other cloud warehouse vendors moved data out of the firewalled vaults, and into ATMs all over the internet.

Every SaaS company has carefully-worded security policies that are written by lawyers. And then they have lived security policies, which are usually “most people don’t know how to write SQL, so it’s probably fine.”
If you show revenue, people will ask how much, and it will never be enough. But if you have no revenue, you can be a pure AI play.
posts.key_insight: In tech, systems are often designed less to prevent failure than to ensure that when failure happens, no one powerful can be blamed.

posts.summary: The post argues that in software and data tooling, the industry overvalues optionality and underestimates the cost of constant evaluation, suggesting teams should embrace deliberate lock-in to force focus on people and process. It extends the theme of too many options to advertising, warning that AI-driven personalization could make even the content itself variable and manipulable for each viewer.
money_quotes: The magic we’re looking for is in the work we’re avoiding.
Chooselock-in. Buy the BI tool;burn the boats behind you; set your tools in stone. Force yourself to worry about people and process problems by taking away your other temptations.
More optionality means more suppliers, more wandering eyes, and less stability.
But maybe not for much longer? After all, why show people a real tile, when a modified one might get them to buy an extra cup of coffee?
posts.key_insight: Optionality in tools and media feels empowering but often erodes stability and focus, and AI personalization risks turning that tradeoff into a pervasive, profit-driven distortion.

posts.summary: Using the Away CEO controversy and his own career arc, Stancil argues that Silicon Valley’s obsession with growth turns ambition into a default, socially enforced scoreboard. He contends that our ambitions are largely circumstantial—shaped by the environments we immerse ourselves in—and warns that we can spend years climbing ladders we didn’t consciously choose.
money_quotes: In Silicon Valley, we’ve drowned ourselves so deeply in this ethos that we no longer realize that we’re in the water.
We created them to manufacture accomplishment for ourselves.

Standing on the shore, your old pond—the pond where you did your life's work, in which nothing seemed more important than being a big fish—suddenly looks like all the other ponds you used to care so little about.
You can manufacture ambition.
But at a minimum, we shouldn’t find our ambitions by accident.
posts.key_insight: Ambition in tech is often an environmental artifact, so without deliberate choice we end up chasing success metrics that the ecosystem hands us rather than the ones we truly want.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that LinkedIn has become a peculiar, self-contained social network where professional performance replaces genuine social life, creating an artificial feedback loop of engagement and influence. He likens it to a Truman Show-style simulation: a place that feels socially rewarding but is insulated from real-world culture, celebrity, and spontaneity.
money_quotes: It’s not socializing; it’s editorial calendar crosstalk.
It’s a vault, locked on both sides.

LinkedIn offers us a deal: Don’t really be yourself; don’t talk about taboo things; put up a professional headshot; feign excitement about your new role managing enterprise IoT applications on the edge.
The conversation is real, even if the motivations are more suspect.
Offer up your personality and a few self-promotional posts, and LinkedIn will make you feel like a celebrity.
posts.key_insight: LinkedIn succeeds by rewarding sanitized, professional self-performance with the feelings of popularity and connection, even as it remains cut off from real cultural life.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that typical pitch deck market-sizing slides often signal confusion about who the product is for and substitute big, vague TAM numbers for real planning. The better approach is to size and focus on the painfully small initial market that will actually buy an MVP, then use that as a deliberate path to expanding the market. The goal is not impressing VCs with huge numbers but demonstrating a credible, specific plan to reach scale.
money_quotes: The slide said that we don't know who our product was for, or who we wanted to sell to.
Step two is a delusion and a distraction.
Most startups die because the first thing they build... appeals to some tiny idiosyncratic subset of the billion-dollar Gartner blob.
That’s the only two-step plan that works: Start with some painfully small set of buyers... and expand into the same market but with fewer qualifiers.
Aim for a big market, and miss big by building something for nobody. But aim for a small one, and you might crack it open.
posts.key_insight: A winning pitch deck should anchor on the real, narrow initial market that will buy the MVP and show a credible expansion plan, rather than inflating TAM numbers to signal ambition.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that when a powerful new capability appears, the winning move is not to shoehorn it into existing workflows but to reshape the business problem to fit the capability. Applied to AI, that means abandoning attempts to make LLMs better baristas for today’s analytics tasks and instead finding problems that match what LLMs are uniquely good at, even if that means abandoning traditional “data-driven” rigor.
money_quotes: Rather than trying to fit the skill to your current problems, find some new problems that match the skill.

Perhaps the way AI breaks through—or maybe more concretely, the way that the companies that figure out how to make lots of money with AI end up using it—isn’t by solving some existing problem with an LLM; it’s by changing the problem to make it easy for AI to solve.
If you have friends who are mathematicians, don’t ask them to be accountants; come up with a way to make solving math problems very lucrative.
Better, it seems, to do what Jim Simons did, and try to imagine how we can contort our problem—understanding the world, so we can make better decisions about it—to fit what the technology is good at.

If this current generation of AI technologies is to produce its Renaissance, that seems like the sort of company it’ll be—one that all but abandons quantification entirely, so that it can take full advantage of the million researchers and writers who are offering it their services for $5.92 an hour.
posts.key_insight: AI’s real business value will come from redefining problems to suit LLM strengths, not from forcing LLMs to improve existing analytics workflows.

posts.summary: Stancil frames startups as a “money machine” whose controls are opaque, interconnected, and delayed, making the core challenge less about sheer effort and more about radical uncertainty. The stress comes from not knowing what to do, and neither cautious analysis nor frantic action reliably works; the only workable posture is a confident, even delusional, commitment to a chosen configuration.
money_quotes: The thing that makes the magical mystery money machine a magical mystery money machine is its mystery: You don’t know how it works.
It isn’t hard to operate because the switches are hard to flip—though they are—it is hard to operate because you don’t know what the switches do.
It’s not the amount of work that surprises us, but the nature of that work: Sitting in front of a mixing console worth of controls and not knowing what to do.
Button mashing in response to problems—and there will always be problems—rarely fixes the machine, and tells you nothing about it actually works.
They are people who are convinced that they know how the machine works.
posts.key_insight: The defining hardship of startups is not the workload but the uncertainty of which actions actually matter, forcing founders to operate on conviction amid opaque feedback.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that persuasive, data-backed arguments can still be wrong, and only real-world testing separates good ideas from bad ones. The Nike “data-driven” controversy illustrates how numbers can make debatable strategies feel like facts, creating a culture that prizes quantified rhetoric over experimentation.
money_quotes: Bad ideas sometimes sound like good ideas, and second, until you try it, it’s really hard to tell the difference.
A compelling rhetorical argument isn’t the same thing as the truth. The only real truth is what happens.

Doing quantitative analysis feels like figuring something out. It feels like a shortcut to the end; it reveals “insights;” it is a way to know something, or at least get reasonably close to knowing something, without doing something.
Data is useful for finding out by being useful in measuring interventions—the experiments, the building of the metrics layer, the new organizational structure at Nike.
The opposite of a debate-driven culture is not a data-driven culture. They are actually the same thing, just with debaters wielding different weapons.
posts.key_insight: Data and logic can make weak ideas sound true, so the only reliable way to learn is to test in the real world and measure what actually happens.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that “founder mode” is mostly a new set of rules masquerading as contrarian wisdom, and that copying Jobs-style leadership is just another cargo cult. The deeper point is that there aren’t real rules—effective leadership is doing something bold and making it work, i.e., “getting away with it.”
money_quotes: Computers don’t tie us down; we do.
Our rules are insurgent, but they are rules, and they are also hard to break.
Founder mode is not advice; it’s something to yell when you bulldoze into a meeting and make the decision yourself.
Founder mode is not the answer to that question; it is simply permission to look for a solution.
You can do it, if you can get away with it.
posts.key_insight: The real lesson of “founder mode” isn’t a new playbook but the permission to choose bold, unconventional approaches—and only success makes them legitimate.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that foundational LLM vendors face a brutal cost structure where model improvement is a perpetual, escalating expense, not a one-time investment. Because compute is leased and model quality decays quickly against fast-moving and open-source competitors, their moats are thin and short-lived, making survival depend on constant fundraising or massive revenues. The ultimate winners, he suggests, will be whoever is ahead when market hype and capital dry up—not necessarily the best or fastest builders.
money_quotes: Every LLM vendor is eighteen months from dead.
The billions that OpenAI spent on building prior versions of GPT is not, because better versions of it are already available for free on Github.
An LLM vendor that doesn’t spend tens of millions of dollars a year—and maybe billions, for the leaders—improving their models is a year or two from being out of business.
The market needs to be irrational for you to stay solvent.
And the winners won’t be who ran the fastest or reached some finish line, but whoever was leading when the market decided the race is over.
posts.key_insight: LLM vendors lack durable moats because model advances are costly, quickly commoditized, and easily matched, so survival hinges on relentless funding or massive revenues rather than technological lead alone.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that workplace chat tools like Slack optimize for casual chatter, not thoughtful, triage-able work communication, and he contrasts them with Yammer’s threaded, inbox-oriented design. He then satirizes the AI-hype venture landscape with the fictional benn.ventures, using it to critique trend-chasing and status games in VC. He closes by reflecting on the stalled career arc of data leaders and the uncertainty of what comes next for a maturing cohort.
money_quotes: The bones are horrible.
The issue, it seems, is that slack is for chatting.
Nothing is complete without AI.
Silicon Valley is a status game, and status is zero sum.
So what happens to all of us now? I don’t know.
posts.key_insight: The post uses a critique of chat-first workplace tools and AI-hype venture culture to highlight how fad-driven incentives and status dynamics distort products, funding, and career paths in tech.

posts.summary: The post argues that VCs contain two competing personas: a sincere helper who loves collaborating with founders and a harsher, status‑driven operator who wants to be feared and seen as decisive. While both modes can be useful, the author stresses that performative toughness and insecurity are easy to spot, and authenticity matters more than cosplay.
money_quotes: Inside every venture capitalist there are two wolves.
The second wolf will shiv you for a dollar.
Inside every venture capitalist, there are both wolves.
Nobody is as good of an actor as they think they are.
You can tell when someone is desperate to prove that they’re useful and not just rich.
posts.key_insight: VCs oscillate between helpful collaboration and performative toughness, but the insecurity behind the latter is transparent and damaging.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that Excel persists not despite its “anti-patterns,” but because those quirks are precisely what make it indispensable: local files, direct manipulation, and the ability to create private, versioned sandboxes. He suggests that any true successor must embrace these traits while adding scale, polyglot workflows, and optional cloud execution rather than trying to eliminate Excel’s messy flexibility.
money_quotes: The answer—obviously, probably—is that these aren’t bugs of Excel, but features.
Live, mysteriously-changing data is bad. Collaboration is bad. Restrictions on how to manipulate data are bad.
Excel doesn’t put anything between you and the raw data.

So far, most attempts to make a polyglot data tool stick notebooks in BI tools or stick spreadsheets in notebooks. But if you want to beat Excel, putting a notebook in a spreadsheet might be a better way to go.

If Excel could offload some of its work to the cloud—not by being a SaaS product, but by transparently pushing data out of the desktop app to some remote execution engine—its caps would be much higher.
posts.key_insight: Excel’s endurance comes from how well it supports messy, local, and opaque workflows that businesses actually need, so any replacement must preserve that flexibility while quietly extending its power and scale.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that dbt Labs’ Coalesce has shifted from a quirky, community-first gathering into a more conventional, revenue-minded corporate conference, reflecting the realities of monetizing open source. While that change brings a sense of loss, it also creates stability and clarity about what the event is for. The lasting value, he suggests, is in the small human moments that survive despite commercialization.
money_quotes: If the product is free, you are the product, the saying goes, and dbt had long been free.

This Coalesce made comfortably clear what future Coalesces will be: Mostly a means for the first two ends; a financially-motivated event, hosted by a financially-motivated entity, supported by financially-motivated sponsors; a corporate conference, not so different than the rest of them.
But that’s ok, because even if the talks are sponsored, if the fun is HR-approved, and if the happy hours happen on a budget, the magic was always in the little moments.
Because it’s not the marquees that make the mood, but the background music.
posts.key_insight: Coalesce’s commercialization feels like a loss, but it also makes the event sustainable, and the real value was always the human connections that persist beneath the branding.

posts.summary: The post argues that markets and companies may be drifting away from classical, data-driven valuation toward “vibes” and social consensus, using Trump Media as a case where fundamentals are irrelevant. It connects this to a broader shift in analytics culture: the data-driven movement delivered mixed results, and newer fashions like taste, founder instinct, and narrative are gaining ground. The core question is whether data-driven decision-making was a temporary vogue rather than an inevitable trajectory.
money_quotes: Do not value our business based on models and math and numbers, because we don’t use the numbers, we don’t know the numbers, we don’t track the numbers, and we definitely don’t audit the numbers.
It’s a meme that trades on the Nasdaq; it’s a presidential election prediction market; it’s a commemorative collectable coin, sold as a soft con.
The answer to what TMTG should be worth isn’t found in a math problem, but in—and only in—our animal spirits.
A company’s hype factor may not be a red flag; it might be its only ground truth.
Increasingly, “we are data-driven” feels less like a competitive advantage, and more like an empty sales pitch.
posts.key_insight: Both stock valuation and corporate decision-making may be less anchored in data than we assume, with social consensus and taste increasingly substituting for quantitative “fundamentals.”

posts.summary: The post argues that companies (and people) often break ideals not because they are uniquely bad, but because staying true to them requires exceptional success and leverage. Google’s early “don’t be evil” era is framed as a byproduct of a wildly profitable machine, and the author connects this to Mode’s sale and broader startup pressures. Ambition can force compromises: the bigger the goal, the stronger the pull to recalibrate values and move closer to the line.
money_quotes: Deep down, Google probably had the same two values that every other company has: First, make money, and second, some other nice sounding stuff, in that order.
But idealism has a price, and most companies can’t afford it.
The point of all of this is that wanting to do something and being able to do it are not the same thing.

If every venture-backed company has two ordered values—make money, and some nice sounding stuff—they also have two unstated product values—build something that you can sell, and build the thing you want to sell.
Because if you shoot for the stars and land on the moon, that’s great—but the thing that lands on the moon probably doesn’t look like the thing that was shooting for the stars.
posts.key_insight: Ideals persist only when performance is elite enough to protect them; otherwise ambition and market pressure steadily force compromises.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that individual votes in U.S. presidential elections are mathematically insignificant, yet people still vote because they want to participate in a shared democratic ritual. That belief is a fragile “noble lie,” and preserving it—especially during a contentious election—is essential to keeping democracy functioning.
money_quotes: No ordinary citizen has ever cast a meaningful vote in a presidential election.
We vote, I think, because we want to be part of something. We want to participate.
The moral sense that our vote is important is not an inherent truth about democracy, but a noble lie we tell ourselves because, at least for now, we believe in its possibility.
It is a week for appreciating the arresting magic of a democracy that is believed in.
It is that spell, and that spell alone, that makes us participate—and it us participating that makes it work.
posts.key_insight: Democracy depends less on the literal impact of any one vote than on the shared faith that participation matters.

posts.summary: Stancil recounts election night from the Harris campaign data team, where extensive technical preparation became irrelevant once the loss was obvious. He argues that the unseen work and contingency plans still mattered as evidence of people fighting on, and that being in the room—win or lose—changes you. The piece ends by urging readers to act, not only to affect outcomes but because participating is less hollow than spectating.
money_quotes: We had plans for every contingency.
The minutes and decimal places didn’t matter.
They were flawlessly delivered disastrous news all night.
Never before have I been involved in trying to answer a more pressing question for a more important audience—and never before has the answer been worse.
It is easier to lose on the field than in the stands.
posts.key_insight: Even when the data work can’t change the outcome, doing it alongside others in the fight gives the effort meaning and changes the people involved.

posts.summary: The post argues that while data quality is crucial for traditional analytics, it matters less than commonly claimed for many AI/LLM use cases because these models are robust to noisy inputs. The real reason data quality may gain attention now is not technical necessity but heightened executive interest in AI, which makes organizations suddenly care about the data feeding these systems.
money_quotes: In the age of AI, the story goes, the companies with the best models will win; the best models will be built on the best data; the best data is, at least in part, the highest quality data.
In other words, the approximate qualitative averages that get built by these models bulldoze bad data in ways careful mathematical computations do not.

If data quality matters more now than it did before, it seems like that will be the reason why: Not because AI models care more about higher quality data than traditional reporting, but because, at least during the current AI circus, businesses care more about AI models than they ever did about reporting.
Most of the time, we don’t think about this much, because the motivations are so transparent.

Which is not a very good answer? And it’s certainly a much worse answer than saying “we need to invest in robust data quality solutions, so that you can correctly report how much money we make and don’t get sued by the SEC.”
posts.key_insight: AI’s hype may elevate data quality investments, not because models demand pristine data, but because leaders finally care about the systems using it.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that much of knowledge work—think tanks, sports media, and analytics—functions as theatrical set dressing that sustains the appearance of expertise rather than producing meaningful insight. He suggests AI will displace many roles not because it is brilliant, but because the work often didn’t matter to the business in the first place, then challenges readers to prove that true, discovery-driven analytics is real.
money_quotes: They are the bricks in a false front: Collectively convincing from a distance, but bearing little meaningful weight of their own.
In other words, if AI replaces all our jobs, it might not because because what we do is easy, or because AI is so good that it can do as well as we do.
It may be because it never mattered if we were particularly good at it in the first place.
We sell this promise all the time.
And I’m not convinced it’s real.
posts.key_insight: The post contends that much of analytics is performative legitimacy work rather than genuine discovery, which is why AI could replace it even without matching real analytical skill.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the database world has swung from boxed software to cloud bundles to a fragmented “separate storage/compute/engine” stack that’s powerful but complicated, costly, and hard to use—creating demand for a bundled, simplified “Postgres in a box” again. He extends the idea to unstructured data and LLMs, suggesting that once we can query text and files like tables, content platforms become part of the same database story. The post ends by contrasting flashy AI insights with the enduring value of straightforward analytics that simply tell people what’s happening.
money_quotes: So that’s what databases did. They split themselves in half; they “separated storage from compute,” as the catchphrase goes.
You can’t put your data in one place, and bring your favorite calculator to it.
Your one database becomes a modern data stack of databases, in the worst way: A knot of intertwined and opaque costs.
Data is still downstream of emotions and biases; a quantitative survey of 10,000 people is no less corruptible than a qualitative interview panel of 10 people.
But that really is all we want.
posts.key_insight: The march toward modular, interoperable data stacks is real, but the resulting complexity and cost make the market ripe for a re-bundled, simpler “database-in-a-box” that even extends to unstructured data via LLMs.

posts.summary: The post argues that “working in data” often lacks a clear, climbable expertise cliff, so many analysts drift as broad generalists without developing deep, domain-specific mastery. Stancil suggests this creates a low-ceiling career path, because domain experts who are merely good at analytics usually outperform analysts who lack deep domain identity. The remedy is to choose a specific domain to specialize in and build true expertise there, using analytics as a supporting strength.
money_quotes: Pursuing a career in analytics feels like pursuing a career in “science”—it’s not specific enough to go anywhere.
We are fact checkers, not authors; we are logical mechanics, not drivers.
You cannot see the wind without something for it to disturb; you cannot be an analyst without something to analyze.
If we want to do something more important, we have to do something more important.
Otherwise, we risk being like the wind—everywhere, but nowhere in particular.
posts.key_insight: A sustainable, high-ceiling career in data requires committing to a specific domain and becoming a true expert there, rather than remaining a generic “data person.”

posts.summary: The post argues that as LLM performance converges and moats erode, AI companies increasingly resemble SaaS businesses whose advantage is UX, distribution, and brand rather than proprietary models or infrastructure. OpenAI’s recent product releases look like wrappers and workflow features, suggesting the real competition is in software ergonomics and brand positioning, not model supremacy.
money_quotes: OpenAI can no longer just entice us with foundational infrastructure and an accidentally successful chat app; they’re now trying to sell us commoditized tech and an increasingly complicated SaaS app.
In other words, people want the better software product, not the better model.
Silicon Valley has spent the last two years trying to figure out which AI companies are real, and which are just wrappers around ChatGPT.
Now, the brand leads, and the product—which is getting awfully expensive to build—follows.
So how do you keep the brand without burning billions to build an undifferentiated product?
posts.key_insight: As model advantages compress, AI companies’ durable edge shifts toward SaaS-style product integration, distribution, and brand rather than proprietary LLMs.

posts.summary: Stancil strings together survey results and a mailbag to show how people’s stated preferences about data, contrarianism, and tools often clash with how they actually behave or decide. Beneath the humor, he argues that roles, data access, and governance are less about pure ideals and more about incentives, signaling, and pragmatic constraints.
money_quotes: The data company wins every head-to-head matchup.
It gets unwieldy in a hurry, and it’s useful for somebody to be responsible for keeping the underlying architecture sane.
In other words, the nature of data engineering has changed (or expanded), in a way that’s generally unfavorable to data engineers.
We want it more than we’d actually use it.

Boundaries may limit us, but the infinite paralyzes us—and we are often better off accepting and making the most of what we have than we are spending our time trying to get more of what we don’t have.
posts.key_insight: People say they want maximal data access and contrarian identity, but in practice they gravitate toward familiar signals and the guardrails that make work possible.

posts.summary: The post argues that enterprise software buying has shifted from buyer–user mismatch to a new dynamic where popularity, taste, and social proof drive adoption more than product quality. As consumerization gave way to memeification, software now competes in a fashion-like market where attention and celebrity can outweigh marginal technical superiority.
money_quotes: If you have to use Jira, you hate it. If you don’t have to use Jira, you buy it.
Products being bought and sold by backroom negotiation is a form of market failure. Products being chosen on their own merits is pure and efficient.
We do not like things from first principles. We like them because other people like them.
Our desires are memetic; our social proof is gossip; our interests are compelled.
Because, for as much as Silicon Valley celebrates the builders, our economy is content and our opinions come from others' upvotes.
posts.key_insight: Enterprise software is increasingly bought like consumer fashion: attention and status can beat product quality, making meme-driven popularity a core competitive advantage.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that digital convenience and the illusion of total control have made us forget the stubborn physics of the real world. Events like wildfires and the Waymo standoff reveal how fragile our curated, software-shaped lives are when confronted with distance, matter, and uncontrollable forces.
money_quotes: It is easy to forget all this.
The only laws are the sharp edge of physics—the speed of light, the size of atoms, the amount of palladium in the earth.

When we are so accustomed to the tailored impermanence of internet life—feeds can be curated, text messages can be edited, broken phones can be replaced, bugs fixed, refunds issued—reality becomes unsettling.
The fire cannot be programmed. The Santa Anas cannot be bought. There is no app for that either.
The more we bend our world to our will, the more loaded its bow. The more we curate our lives, the weaker our immunity to its intruders.
posts.key_insight: Our software-shaped expectations collide with the inescapable constraints of the physical world, and that collision is only getting sharper.

posts.summary: Benn argues that the AI boom is compressing adoption and revenue timelines so much that traditional signals of traction no longer imply durability or moat. When growth can be manufactured quickly by fear-driven enterprise spending and a flood of new startups, fast revenue may indicate a shallow, contested vein rather than a defensible business.
money_quotes: There is no adoption curve anymore; there are only users, and the walking dead.
The best vendor is the one that walks in with a futuristic demo, a pile of cash from premier venture capitalists, and the wild eyes of an unhinged visionary.
FOBO compresses the time it takes to make money, but it doesn’t compress the time it takes to build something durable.
The former is a business; the second is a war.
The truth may somehow be worse: Rather than turning us into batteries, the machines will make us Jira admins.
posts.key_insight: In the AI rush, explosive growth is no longer a reliable moat signal because spending and adoption are faster than the time it takes to build durable advantage.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that Silicon Valley’s celebrated contrarianism is less about fearless truth-seeking and more about indulging ideas that feel fun or flattering, while avoiding uncomfortable realities like the 1619 Project. As tech’s power surges again, he warns that the industry is choosing self-serving narratives and political convenience over moral courage, especially in the face of obvious abuses.
money_quotes: My suspicion is that Silicon Valley contrarianism isn’t about a relentless pursuit of every truth; it’s about indulgence.
The talks at Hereticon aren’t hard realities that make the people giving and watching them uncomfortable; they’re forbidden fruits that make other people uncomfortable.
We can do anything we want.
We are self-governed now, and our decency—and our mercy—must come from within.
And if, despite all that, we cannot stand up to anything that is happening now—not even everything, but anything—perhaps we are not such brave heretics after all.
posts.key_insight: Silicon Valley’s self-image as fearless heretics collapses when truth threatens its comfort or power, revealing a lack of moral courage.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that DeepSeek shattered the assumed economics and trajectory of frontier LLMs, proving that powerful models can appear cheaply and unexpectedly, undermining the moat of big labs. This shift changes AI from a predictable, centralized race into a chaotic landscape of many specialized, uneven models with unpredictable societal impacts.
money_quotes: Every LLM vendor is eighteen months from dead.
Last week, the band snapped.
We are no longer living in a world where a few superpowers might build Skynet, but one in which Skynet could be anywhere.
This is not a new world; it is a streamlined one.
AI cracks several more—that expertise takes time to accumulate; that reasoning has some foundational legitimacy; that creativity does not scale—and now, anyone can do anything, almost immediately.
posts.key_insight: DeepSeek marks a discontinuity that democratizes frontier-level AI, making model proliferation and downstream societal disruption far more likely than the industry’s neat, centralized roadmap suggested.
