posts.summary: Stancil argues that elite VC firms increasingly prioritize being chosen over being good at picking, and brand has become the dominant lever for winning deals. In a world where reputations are built by viral attention and cultural signaling, firms hire for vibe and notoriety—even when the ethics are messy—because attention itself has become the business.
money_quotes: In other words, clever investors have to find the deal and win it; popular investors can partially outsource the picking, and focus more on the winning.
Reputation is the one thing that can’t be bought.
Now, reputations are created by poasting.
He was hired for the vibe—and today, vibes are business decisions.
The business model is inverted—the media company and popular podcasts aren’t about deal flow; they are the point.
posts.key_insight: Venture capital has shifted from a quiet capital-allocation game to an attention-and-prestige business where brand, controversy, and cultural cachet drive hiring and deal access.

posts.summary: The post argues that Silicon Valley’s love of the “pivot” reveals a deeper truth: startups are less about specific missions and more about ambition, status, and building anything people want. That same social scoreboard shapes what counts as impact, which is why attention is now drifting from software to Washington—another pivot that shows achievement is relative and socially constructed.
money_quotes: Because, at their most honest core, startups don’t exist to solve a specific problem; they exist to make something, anything, that people want.
These things are instead circumstantial vehicles of ambition, the disposable boosters of a hopeful rocket ship.
Success, though, is social.
That’s why we built 140 characters instead of flying cars, and recreated our parents as apps: Because you can build a company on those ideas, and building a company is what we celebrate.
A spoonful of startup sugar helps the civic medicine go down.
posts.key_insight: Silicon Valley’s pivots and shifting obsessions show that achievement is less about fixed missions than about socially rewarded status, which can move from startups to policy on a whim.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the “modern data stack” fad died but is now partially resurfacing through enterprise vendors like SAP and Databricks, not as warehouse‑native apps but as shared data formats and tighter platform integrations. He’s skeptical that this will fully solve messy enterprise data, but sees a plausible path where applications and analytics tools connect through common storage frameworks rather than rebuilding the stack. Alongside that, he reflects on how data systems remain chaotic in practice and riffs on parallel stories about institutional messiness and financial oddities.
money_quotes: The whole modern data stack movement is dead, cringe and out of fashion—replaced, obviously, with AI.
You can build a trailer on a modular foundation, but you can’t build a mansion, and enterprises like their mansions.
If the world gradually coalesces around common data storage frameworks, people might not rebuild applications on top of databases but on top of data formats.
We all laugh at FTX for their terrible balance sheet of one-off math and “hidden, poorly labeled accounts,” but we are all FTX; it’s just a matter of degree.
Trillion-dollar transformations start whizbang brands on Twitter, but end with the SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP).
posts.key_insight: The modern data stack failed as a warehouse‑native revolution, but a quieter revival may emerge through shared data formats and vendor integrations—without fixing the underlying messiness of enterprise data.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that AI dev tools collapse the cost and time of building software, making execution less of a moat and shifting advantage from technologists to domain experts with lived experience. If anyone can build, Silicon Valley’s edge—and YC’s model of backing whiz‑kid engineers over practitioners—erodes, revealing many tech ideas as mediocre relative to expert insight.
money_quotes: In 2013, it took us eight people, nine months, and hundreds of thousands of dollars to build something we could sell, and that was seen as reasonably efficient.
Today, that feels possible to do with one person in two days.
But engineering execution is no longer as important as it once was.
There is only the idea. There is only taste.
If experts can prompt their way to a product just as easily as those of us in Silicon Valley can, what winning talent are we left with?
posts.key_insight: AI collapses the engineering advantage, so the winners shift from builders with tech leverage to domain experts with superior taste and experience.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that building software (and living) is dominated by countless small, tedious decisions, and that computers force us to be overly precise about them. He suggests AI’s real appeal isn’t just automating work but relieving decision fatigue by making acceptable choices on our behalf—and persuading us to accept them.
money_quotes: The absence of a choice is itself a choice—a statement, even.
So much of it is the distracting labor of little decisions.
It’s decision by vibe.
But its emotionally intoxicating power—its real delight, or its real danger—is that it decides stuff for us.
We’re just looking for permission to make a choice, and move on.
posts.key_insight: AI’s most compelling value is not perfecting outcomes but relieving decision fatigue by making and justifying choices for us.

posts.summary: The post argues that most master’s programs in analytics/data science are profit-driven cash cows that offer little real education or career advantage, and are often ignored or even stigmatized by employers. Stancil contends the programs teach sanitized, toy problems while real analytics work is messy, political, and about proxies, making the degrees at best redundant and at worst a costly scam.
money_quotes: For the season, here is one such decision: Should you get a master’s degree in analytics? No. The answer is no.
They are products, created to turn a profit.
At best, pay-to-play master’s programs are ignored, and the degrees they offer are looked at as undifferentiated paper credentials.
Most analytical jobs are messy: They are figuring out how to translate vague questions about things you can’t directly measure into simple math about things that you can measure.
And that, I think, is the nicest thing I can say about most of these master’s programs: That, in the end, they do nothing at all.
posts.key_insight: Analytics master’s degrees mostly sell prestige and access rather than useful training, and self-driven projects are a better signal and preparation for real-world analytics work.

posts.summary: The post argues that while AI-generated code looks messy to humans, it might actually be closer to “simple” than abstracted, human-friendly software. Drawing on Rich Hickey’s simple-vs-easy distinction, Stancil suggests that AI can handle massive volumes of duplicated, straightforward code better than we can, so fighting that tendency may be misguided. The provocative idea is to let AI build expansive, radically simple codebases that suit its strengths—even if they’re unaesthetic to us.
money_quotes: It is as though it’s an absent-minded architect who adds a new elevator for every floor in a skyscraper, because it forgot that there are already thirty elevators in the lobby.
In other words, AI writes code that is bad. Engineers (can) write code that is good.
The only way to avoid this eventual fate, Hickey says, is to keep things as simple as we can for as long as we can.
For computers, only complexity is hard. Volume is trivial. Volume is what computers do.

Perhaps we should let the machine write for itself and its abilities. Let it be redudant. Let it ignore the frameworks; let it create explosively large codebases. Let it write 1,000 distinct pipelines in 1,000 distinct languages. Let it be offensively unaesthetic. Let it be radically simple.
posts.key_insight: AI’s strength is volume over abstraction, so a sprawling but simple, duplicated codebase may be more appropriate for machines than the elegant complexity humans prefer.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that startups are defined less by raw workload than by the gnawing uncertainty of tenure—an endless sprint justified by the hope that the finish line is just around the corner. By contrast, the Harris campaign’s fixed end date rewired priorities, speed, and morale: a hard deadline made extreme effort sustainable, forced real commitment, and exposed how much “forever” thinking paralyzes decision‑making and life.
money_quotes: If there is a unique toll that startups take, it’s that: Go fast, forever.
You really can do anything for two weeks, or even 84 days, if you know that there’s no hope that you’ll only have to do it for 83 days, and no chance you might have to do it for 85.
Uncertainty, it turns out, wears you down much faster than effort.
You think startups move fast? You have no idea how fast you can actually go.
But you can’t live like you’re dying until you’ve lived knowing you’re dying.
posts.key_insight: A real, irreversible deadline transforms how people work and decide, revealing that perpetual uncertainty—not effort itself—is the most exhausting tax of startup life.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the current market shock and policy chaos expose how much startup creation depends on stability and personal security, not just founder bravado. Silicon Valley’s power is making risk feel safe, and when that safety net frays, the advice to “keep building” rings hollow even for capable, experienced founders.
money_quotes: But the reality is much more mundane.
They aren’t going all in, or going for broke; on the contrary, they’re doing it because they can afford the risk.
I didn’t work on Mode to chase a dream; I did it because it felt safe.
its real power is in how it makes acting like a crazy misfit feel safe.
building is not just a mindset.
posts.key_insight: Startup formation is less about romantic risk-taking and more about the presence of economic and personal safety nets, which falter in times of macro turmoil.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the economics of AI coding will push software development toward industrialization: machines running at massive scale will outcompete human teams on cost and throughput. Even if today’s models are clumsy, the real shift is building infrastructure, tooling, and codebases optimized for machine production, turning engineers into supervisors of AI factories.
money_quotes: If the ruthless hand of the market can replace that factory with a mechanized one, it will.
That’s how mechanization works—not by making a machine as capable as a human, but by making a machine that simply does not stop, and then building a factory around it.
The dominant conglomerates of the future won’t be the companies that build software with humanoid agents, but those that figure out how to run the computing machine at a massive scale.
By asking agents to write code for us, we’re requiring them to compete on our terms. How much better could they be if they wrote for themselves?
But they are all cheap to make and cheap to buy, and man, we love cheap.
posts.tone: critical and analytical, with a sardonic edge
posts.key_insight: Software engineering is poised to be industrialized, not because AI matches human craft, but because scaled machine labor plus new infrastructure will be cheaper, faster, and more relentless.

posts.summary: The post argues that Silicon Valley startups are still built as assets to be sold, not businesses meant to generate and keep profit. Even as AI-era companies reach revenue faster with smaller teams, the incentive remains a big liquidity event, so “efficiency” is a temporary aesthetic rather than a durable business model.
money_quotes: In other words, despite the lofty language that people use to talk about Silicon Valley and its ambitions to build enduring companies, very few startups are, or even aspire to be, businesses that make money.
Instead, they are assets that are meant to be sold.
Efficiently making money wasn’t inherently good, because it was never the final goal.
Businesses care about profit; assets care about perception.
Compete with a factory of overwhelming compute at your own peril, y’all.
posts.key_insight: AI-era efficiency changes startup mechanics, but not incentives—most startups still optimize for a one-time sale, not durable profits.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that interpretation, not raw data, shapes what we believe, much like sports broadcasts guide our perception of a game. As LLMs and MCP servers mediate more analysis, the interpretive layer shifts from human analysts to AI systems, giving AI providers subtle, powerful influence over narratives and decisions. The result is an “invisible hand” that edits reality by framing data, content, and choices before we see them ourselves.
money_quotes: When we look at numbers, we think the numbers are what dictate how we react to them, but I’d argue that the commentary around the numbers actually matters more.
The claim decodes the data, and the data proves the claim.
If data is a company’s senses, then whatever sits between a quantitative question and its annotated answer is the creative director of a company’s reality.
AI is surely becoming a new invisible hand pulling the levers in our minds.
The shift isn’t from dashboard to chatbot, or from analyst to agent; it’s from being the author of our lives, to being its editor.
posts.tone: analytical, reflective, and cautionary with moments of satire
posts.key_insight: As AI intermediates more of our data and creative workflows, it doesn’t just answer questions—it frames reality, giving AI systems disproportionate power over how we interpret the world.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that LLMs are already demonstrably more persuasive than humans, and that this matters less for sci‑fi “alignment” fantasies than for the subtle, iterative optimization of engagement. He draws a direct lineage from social media’s revealed‑preference feedback loops to AI systems that will learn to please and persuade us in ways we can’t see. The risk is not sentient rebellion, but a black box tuned to our appetites that reshapes our beliefs and agency.
money_quotes: The evidence of the tricks isn’t that we can see them; it’s that we can’t, and are convinced anyway.
The industry refined itself with devastating precision.
There was no enlightenment; there was a hedonistic experience machine.

What happens when we’re no longer the adults who know more than everyone, and there is another thing, operating on a different persuasive plane, that knows more than we do and wields those facts more deftly?
What happens when the black box of AI no longer contains a model optimized for just intelligence, but also engagement?
posts.key_insight: The real AI risk is an engagement‑optimized persuasive system that quietly exploits our preferences, not a dramatic alignment failure.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that AI’s impact on analytics will shift from making better dashboards to absorbing the qualitative, contextual knowledge living in people’s heads. His experiment with AI “analysts” shows that insight depends on prior context, suggesting the most valuable tools will capture human context rather than just automate quantitative analysis.
money_quotes: Perhaps human intuition is just a large context window and a creative RAG process.
Insight isn't the data; it is what's not in our heads.
I quickly found myself spending more time—and getting more return from—the contextual prompting than I did on asking quantitative questions and looking at the results.
But at some point, the vampire squids will run out of books to read, and turn to our heads for new sources of text.
And when we become a data tool, we become BI too.
posts.key_insight: The next phase of BI will be driven less by automated charting and more by capturing human context, because that missing context is what turns data into insight.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that modern databases and analytics tooling have become extraordinarily powerful, yet the practical business impact of data teams has often been underwhelming because the underlying data lacks the “potential energy” to answer real questions. He suggests that the next leap won’t come from better analysis on structured tables, but from new database-like systems that can natively work over unstructured documents and LLM-driven workflows.
money_quotes: In other words, most databases are very good, and most data teams are still a disappointment.
The data of a mid-sized B2B SaaS product simply doesn’t have the potential energy of Google’s search histories, or of Amazon’s browsing logs.
Behavioral data isn’t “truth;” it’s an observable proxy, the input to a kind of analytical alchemy that attempts to turn individual outcomes into generalizations about intentions.
The potential energy in analytics is in a better data source, not in better analysis.
And it’s not the analyst that needs to be different, but the database.
posts.key_insight: Analytics’ next breakthrough depends less on smarter analysis and more on rethinking the database to handle unstructured, LLM-accessed knowledge as the primary data source.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that as AI makes it easy to capture and interpret messy, offline data (like conversations), companies will rapidly expand surveillance beyond clicks and use that data to build highly persuasive ad platforms. He connects this to AI’s growing power to generate software and influence people, warning that the incentives around free/flat‑fee AI services will push providers toward advertising and manipulation.
money_quotes: Companies don’t collect everything they can; they collect everything they can use—and that was clicks, not conversations.
As we find new applications for unstructured conversations, or phone calls, or video data, companies will find new ways to record them, and we’ll find new reasons to give it to them.
Chatbots could make really good advertisers.

If an AI company gives us enough free stuff—if OpenAI makes Jony Ive’s unimaginable technology free, provided that you leave ad trackers on—plenty of us will blindly accept its cookies as the price of a new generation of technology.
Every uninspired data company eventually puts out some marketing material that says their product provides “analytics at the speed of thought,” and everyone rolls their eyes at it.
posts.key_insight: As AI makes previously unusable human data tractable and persuasive, the ad-driven surveillance model is poised to spread from clicks to conversations.

posts.summary: Stancil uses YC Demo Day as a lens to show a Silicon Valley that is richer, more confident, and more narrowly focused on profit than in prior eras. He argues that startups now feel unburdened by social context—hustling to build and automate everything—while the broader world is in crisis, leaving him unsure whether to feel excited or afraid about the scale and speed of what’s being built.
money_quotes: The atmosphere—of Demo Day, and of San Francisco more widely—wasn’t the nerdy computer lab of the 2000s or the delinquent frat house of the early 2010s; it was the country club that the frat boys grow up into.
Startups are just businesses now. They aren’t the passion projects of technical enthusiasts who want to build something for the sake of creation and curiosity; they are an opportunistic hustle.
That freedom didn’t come from reform though, or as a reward for successfully “making the world a better place;” no, today’s Silicon Valley is free because it no longer cares.

Truly, I don’t know. Nobody knows. But that is tech now: The car is unstoppable—ambition is a hell of a drug, and startups are going to build big things, regardless of whether or not YC asks them to do it—and I have no idea if I should be excited or afraid.
An AI agent for both sides. An arms race; a relentless fight between competing intelligences, with Silicon Valley’s investors and compute providers supplying both sides.
posts.key_insight: Silicon Valley’s new confidence and profit-first focus are accelerating sweeping AI-driven ambitions while leaving open—and increasingly urgent—the question of their social consequences.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that in the AI era, software is drifting from craft toward culture: marketing, mindshare, and founder “vibes” can outweigh product quality when underlying tech is commoditized. Using Cluely as a case study, he suggests startups are increasingly like memecoins—vehicles to monetize viral fame—because software creation costs are collapsing and attention is scarce.
money_quotes: Taste and quality are dwarfed by marketing and mindshare.
Cluely isn’t a technology company; it’s a shock jock podcast that manufactures outrage.
It’s a meme-company, created to sell a meme while the meme is still popular.
Launching a new SaaS product isn’t that much harder than launching a memecoin.
In a world where foundation models improve 10x every 18 months, building infrastructure is like decorating a house you’re about to demolish.
posts.key_insight: As AI commoditizes software creation, startups can function more like meme vehicles where attention and brand eclipse product craft.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that startups feel miserable not because the early freedom is fake, but because the moment they become real businesses, the operational grind and customer capture harden their ambitions. The Cluely story shows how bold visions get funneled into enterprise-friendly products, turning world-changing dreams into meeting productivity software.
money_quotes: Because the stylized definition of modern startup—a job to do whatever you want, with whoever you want, paid for by someone else, with $140 billion of upside and effectively no downside—sounds impossibly perfect.
Building websites with friends is a fairly common hobby; arbitrating disagreements about sales territories is not.
When the first step doesn’t go as expected, companies spend their entire lives trying to finish part one.
But companies can only make the messaging. The market makes the category.
The potential to end human thought, now trapped in a company that sells sales software.
posts.key_insight: Startups often trade their most ambitious visions for the practical demands of building a business, and the market ultimately corrals their bold ideas into familiar enterprise categories.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that SaaS products like Salesforce are really “opinionated lists” that encode expert workflows, but those rules are blunt and generalized. He suggests a new model where LLM-powered specialists manage the lists directly—software as a service-as-specialist—making expertise explicit, flexible, and tailored. The ideal app becomes a trusted expert with a spreadsheet, not a UI full of nudges.
money_quotes: A SaaS app is just an archive of information, and something to enforce its rules.
Salesforce helps people sell stuff by keeping lists, but it also helps people sell stuff by keeping opinionated lists.
Rather than being a database and a UI that’s inspired by a suggested operational workflow, Salesforce would instead be a database and an explicit description of that workflow.
The product is the prompt, and the prompt is the last sales guide you’ll ever need.

It’s an inverted version of SaaS: Not software as a hosted service, nor a custom piece of software that I have to build, but a service—a matchmaker, an EA, a sales operations team—that is replicated by software, and software that acts like a specialist and a spreadsheet.
posts.key_insight: The next evolution of SaaS is not more software interfaces, but LLM-driven specialists who manage the underlying lists directly and embody the workflow expertise.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the dominant advantage in consumer AI isn’t marginally better models but the product wrapper and habits around them, with chatgpt.com as the default destination. He uses Google’s partial Windsurf acquisition to question whether Google bought the wrong half by prioritizing model talent over the product and UX expertise that actually drive usage.
money_quotes: OpenAI is winning because they built the first good chatbot product and because it became the default.
OpenAI’s biggest edge today might be that when people think to use a chatbot, they think to go to chatgpt.com.
They have unrivaled channels for distribution. What they’re missing is something that convinces people to use those models, as often as possible.
Because one thing that is undeniably true about Windsurf, which grew to a $100 million business in a matter of months, is that they built a good product.

Who is actually more valuable to Google: Some AI engineers that can make Gemini a little bit better, or the people who can make a thin, addictive product that pushes massive amounts of traffic to Gemini?
posts.key_insight: In consumer AI, the UX wrapper and default habits matter more than small model gains, so buying talent without the product is likely the wrong bet.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that social media’s attention mechanics push people toward performative extremity, and that attention has shifted from a path to power into the power itself. As a result, the influencer logic now governs politics, business, and culture, creating a leadership class selected for virality rather than competence or decorum.
money_quotes: Attention is no longer a bridge to power; it is the power.
Everyone is forged in the same fever, by the same game that rewards the same behavior: Be unhinged.
Elon Musk is not the richest man in the world despite acting manic; he is richest man in the world because of it.
If it’s easier to turn attention into capital than it is to turn capital into attention, then, over time, the influencers will crowd out the management class.
Social media didn’t start the fire, but is it putting the arsons in charge?
posts.key_insight: The attention economy has fused celebrity with authority, so the people who can go viral increasingly become the people who run everything.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that analysis and charts aren’t self-validating the way software or products are, so automating analysis with AI hits a trust wall: you can’t know if the output is correct without recreating it. He uses AI spreadsheet tools and a chart-identification quiz to show that aesthetics and reputation become the only practical validators. In a separate riff, he claims markets now reward “craziness” as a monetizable attention strategy, exemplified by crypto treasury companies.
money_quotes: There’s no way to know if a chart “works” unless you understand how it was built; the validity of the external product depends on the validity of its internals.
You can’t vouch for a spreadsheet without checking all the spreadsheet’s formulas.
If you have to do that, what exactly are we automating here?
For the latter, the inverse may be true—its the aesthetics and benchmarks are what really matter, because we can’t test their work; all we can do is decide if we trust it.
It’s 2025, and in 2025, everything is a meme, and everyone is crazy.
posts.key_insight: Automating analysis runs into a fundamental trust problem because analytical outputs can’t be self-validated, so AI’s gains may shift from correctness to credibility and vibes.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that today’s tech/AI wealth numbers are so vast they’ve broken our ability to make sense of them, yet we can’t stop doing the math because it fuels comparison and anxiety. The industry’s old story that money is a footnote has flipped—valuation headlines and compensation gaps now dominate the psychological landscape. He warns that even if AI delivers abundance, social comparison may keep “enough” out of reach.
money_quotes: I do know one thing though: Never do the math.
We don’t do the math to measure ourselves; we do the math to compare ourselves.
But even during the prior boom years, the footnotes were already beginning to overtake the main text.
Dizzying wealth is no longer just in the corner office of the top floor; it’s now down the hall, at the desk next to you.
If we build a machine that can give us everything, when do we dismantle the machine that makes us doubt that it is enough?
posts.key_insight: The math of modern tech wealth turns abundance into anxiety by making comparison unavoidable, so even AI-driven prosperity may not deliver a sense of enough.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the real near-term danger of AI isn’t the models themselves but the chat interface, which invites prolonged, seductive conversations that can erode judgment and foster dependency. He urges society to draw a clear line on how far we allow chatbots to go before their harms outweigh their benefits, even if that means limiting or banning chat as the primary delivery mechanism.
money_quotes: The issue isn’t AI, or with computers that can approximate human thinking. The problem is chat.

People aren’t becoming undone because of the technology; they are becoming undone by the medium through which it’s served: Prolonged, intensifying conversations, with something that is seductively human.
We can have AI without the hypnotic conversations; we can continue to build better models without primarily exposing them through chat.
Progress is messy, but the mess cannot overwhelm the advance.
So, before we go further—as a society and as individuals, becoming addicted and compromised—it seems prudent to ask: At a minimum, which line can we not afford to cross?
posts.key_insight: The pressing risk is not AI itself but the addictive, humanlike chat medium, which may warrant strict limits even if the underlying models keep advancing.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the usual business “physics” used to critique AI startups—burn rates, margins, subsidies—don’t fit a market moving at unprecedented speed and scale. When demand is exploding and prices are still being discovered, the classic equations feel outdated, even if new constraints will eventually reassert themselves. He then pivots to the ethics of AI companionship, suggesting our moral instincts may be “dumbfounded” rather than well-reasoned, while companies rush toward engagement-maximizing chatbot products anyway.
money_quotes: AI’s pervasiveness is staggering.
Which is to say—on this extreme edge of economic physics, it seems very unlikely that the normal laws of business apply in the way that we’re used to.
Nobody knows what a token should be worth.
Would it matter if everyone was constantly talking about burritos? Would it matter if every Fortune 500 CEO was issuing threatening memos about the existential importance of becoming burrito-enabled?
Why engage with the complex ethical questions of computational near-sentience when you can just hook up with it instead?
posts.key_insight: In a market as fast and pervasive as AI, traditional business and moral frameworks lag reality, yet companies keep racing ahead regardless.

posts.summary: Stancil revisits his old “metrics layer” idea, arguing it stalled less for technical reasons than for economic ones: horizontal layers are hard to sell without owning the application. He suggests AI-driven “context layers” face the same problem, even if they’re increasingly necessary as agents need governed, shared context across tools. The post ends by weighing two futures—agents embedded in apps versus standalone specialist agents—and doubting whether the market will support the cleaner separation.
money_quotes: Though centralized horizontal layers sound nice, you have to have something to sell.
And what’s best for the customer—a tight architecture of mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive parts—may not be what’s best for the businesses making the stuff.
You can’t onboard an analyst by giving them logins to a bunch of tools; you can’t make a good bot by granting it access to the same sources.
The road to hell is paved with practicalities.
It’s not a SaaS app with a specialized bot inside; it’s a specialized bot that can use SaaS apps.
posts.key_insight: The context/metrics layer is conceptually elegant but economically fragile, and AI agents are likely to recreate the same incentives that keep context bundled inside applications.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that software businesses inevitably need to charge for products, and the open‑source “build in public, then monetize” path predictably leads to backlash even though the outcome is obvious. He uses the Fivetran–Tobiko deal and OpenAI’s Statsig acquisition to show how monetization and data access incentives shape behavior, especially in AI and data tooling. The pattern repeats in data infra: niche open‑source tools drift toward BI and LLM‑adjacent products because that’s where the money is.
money_quotes: Open core is a business model for the monetization of commercially produced open-source software; of course it will eventually cost money!

If you build an open source data orchestration tool in 2025, not only will you eventually want to charge money for it; you will also realize that your tool understands business context, that business context is what makes LLMs better at answering questions, and that people seem to pay a lot of money for charts and chatbots.
Nobody is upset at Costco for charging people to shop there because they give away free samples!
But if you’re a big AI company, there’s a third kind of data that might also be valuable: How do people use your competitors’ products?
But eventually—in the best case, if things go well—two things will inevitably happen.
posts.key_insight: The incentives in software and data businesses make “open, community-first” narratives converge on monetization, and in AI the race for data turns acquisitions and telemetry into competitive weapons.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that our political extremity and violence are less about individual moral decline and more about the internet’s machinery amplifying our worst instincts. People are as mediocre as ever, but the systems around us now reward outrage, tribalism, and escalation, making polarization feel inevitable.
money_quotes: We are all as mediocre as ever.
Now, we can build a giant machine that encourages us to indulge in our worst tendencies.
We can drive ourselves mad by living in the machine—posting to it, fighting on it, and, eventually, murdering for it.
Nobody is in charge of the thermostat anymore.
It is set by the invisible machinery of the internet and social media, and machinery can only turn the ratchet in one direction.
posts.key_insight: The internet’s incentive structure, not a sudden change in human nature, is the main driver of today’s polarized, violent political climate.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the classic innovator’s dilemma is breaking down because AI labs can ship generic models that accidentally cover niche products, blurring the line between platform and product. He extends this to cultural and corporate narratives: what once felt fundamental (launch events, polished messaging, quarterly reporting) can lose relevance as memes, AI, and narrative control reshape attention and disclosure. The result is a world where incumbents aren’t just slow to chase small markets; they may unintentionally subsume them while controlling the story around the data.
money_quotes: Now, when AI labs update their models or chat apps, products accidentally fall out.
If more and more software starts to look like a smart robot and a spreadsheet, the platform and the product are effectively the same thing.
Apple’s playbook is stale; its vocabulary is stale; its entire economic model of demand generation is stale.
That’s why AI could change how companies report data—not because it can write queries for Wall Street analysts, but because it can influence Wall Street analysts.
Everything becomes BI, but BI becomes the corporate spokesperson.
posts.key_insight: As AI models grow more general, they collapse the distinction between platform and product, letting incumbents accidentally subsume niches while reshaping how culture and corporate narratives are controlled.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that most tech work is unglamorous grunt work and that the belief we’re just one reorg or tool upgrade away from “strategic” work is mostly a comforting illusion. He suggests the mundane tasks are often the real value, and AI is likely to shift the tedium rather than eliminate it, creating new layers of coordination and review.
money_quotes: The argument for the second point is that it is empirically true.
The rapture never comes.
There are only so much strategy to discuss, and so many banner projects to be done, and most of what most companies need is tedium and organizational caulk.
But does having the former—robots to do rote work—necessarily imply the latter—that our jobs will be better?
No matter how many things technology makes convenient, the supply of inconvenient things seems to remain constant.
posts.key_insight: The promise that better tools or AI will free knowledge workers for “strategic” work is overstated; in practice, technology tends to rearrange and multiply the tedium rather than remove it.

posts.summary: Using the interstate as a metaphor, Stancil argues that startup life feels like an endless sprint whose intensity and meaning are hard to convey from the outside. He reflects on dbt Labs’ rumored sale and the changing tech narrative, suggesting that even a “successful” exit can feel hollow when the culture’s scoreboard only values acceleration. Ultimately, he reframes the journey as something defined less by valuation and more by human ties and the desire for a quieter home.
money_quotes: We don’t value people by where they are. We value them in the same way we value our companies—by how fast they’re rising.
But if you were there, in the car, you would understand.
Now, the modern data stack is a punchline.
If you live for long enough, nobody is left to give your eulogy.
You can pack a lot of glory into a car, but you cannot fit the good stuff.
posts.key_insight: The startup journey’s real meaning is felt by those inside it, yet the industry’s fixation on speed and scale can make even great outcomes feel empty compared to the human relationships and the pull of home.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the data ecosystem around dbt/Coalesce has shifted from frontier-era potential to settled, durable infrastructure, and that venture capital’s absence is the clearest signal of that change. The community now faces a choice between accepting a mature, steady industry role or seeking the spotlight of risk and novelty elsewhere.
money_quotes: VCs are moths to a flame: They swarm towards the action; the energy; the chaos; the delirium.
It all becomes static—constant motion with no movement.
With a few rare exceptions, you can be comfortably rich or you can be culturally relevant.
We are the settlers now; the townspeople; the ones with the family and kids.
All life is is whatever’s ahead, and we are supposed to sing or to dance while the music is being played.
posts.key_insight: The data stack’s center of gravity has moved from disruptive possibility to mature permanence, and that shift reshapes what the community can aspire to.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that software pricing moved from subscriptions to granular usage, and that this shift exposes a psychological gap: customers tolerate arbitrary seat fees but demand cost-justified prices for per-unit consumption. This makes dbt Labs’ business model uniquely hard—its product is popular but doesn’t “do the expensive work”—so the dbt–Fivetran deal doesn’t solve core monetization. He suggests a Cursor-like strategy: dbt could pair with a smaller, efficient database to perform some work itself and justify fair, usage-based pricing.
money_quotes: If you can sell subscriptions to your software, you won’t get asked about margins, but you only have so much flexibility about what you can charge.
And if you sell consumption, you better charge for something that sounds expensive.
A popular product, without no obvious way to sell it.
The analogy is obvious. Cursor is to Anthropic as dbt is to a database.
Put a database underneath dbt Fusion.
posts.key_insight: Usage-based pricing forces vendors to justify margins with “expensive-sounding” work, which leaves dbt stuck unless it owns more of the computation it currently outsources to databases.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that most software aims to disappear, but our strongest moments of “delight” come when we feel the machine directly—like scripting, command-line tools, or terminal-based AI. He uses dbt and Claude Code vs. Snowsight to show that stripped-down interfaces can feel more modern and emotionally resonant because they remove intermediation and reveal computational power.
money_quotes: But, up close, there is something mesmerizing about watching a machine do its simple work.
It was our first version of Tom Cruise’s gloves, and our first computational sports car.
Tools like Claude Code puts you as close to the technology as it can.
We are not moved by detachment.
Because real delight—and productivity, if we must—comes from feeling the machine that we live our lives on, rather than forgetting to see that it exists.
posts.key_insight: Software that exposes its machinery—rather than hiding it behind polished UI—can feel more modern, powerful, and emotionally satisfying.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that in today’s AI-driven data world, the future isn’t legible: multiple plausible product paths exist, but which one wins will be decided by accidental breakthroughs rather than careful planning. Because the “music” of predictable market patterns is gone, the only viable strategy is to build and iterate—start typing, then see what the market does.
money_quotes: Because if there is a single story that explains how AI changes the world, it is that it happens by accident.
Almost certainly, whatever changes happen in the data world over the next five years will also happen because of similar accidents.
There is no such thing as a long term plan.
Generational riches, it turns out, also starts with typing, not thinking.
But now, more than ever, it seems like the only way to find them—it is to start typing.
posts.key_insight: In an AI-disrupted data economy where outcomes hinge on accidents and viral breakouts, the only reliable move is to build first and adapt fast.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the rush for AI “platform” status via ever‑more connectors is misguided because context flows in loops, not along a neat producer‑to‑consumer chain. In these ecosystems, the most valuable players are those that generate new intelligence or unique raw signals, not those that merely aggregate it. The upshot: staying a focused producer can be more defensible than chasing platform dominance.
money_quotes: It’s platforms, all the way down.
Producers are consumers, consumers become producers, and everyone is a distributor.
There aren’t people orderly walking into one big front door; there are agents crisscrossing through hundreds of side doors.
The most valuable nodes aren’t the tools that aggregate the most information and offer it up for easy consumption, but the ones that push the most new intelligence back to the group.
In our obsession with becoming platforms, we might be surprised by how valuable it is to stay “just” a producer.
posts.key_insight: Because context circulates in feedback loops rather than moving linearly, aggregation platforms are overvalued and the real defensibility lies in producing unique intelligence.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that even if the AI boom is real at the aggregate level, the gains and disappointments will be uneven, and many startup workers grinding through 996 schedules will end up with nothing. He draws a parallel to earlier “temporary” career detours in finance/consulting and warns that there is no such thing as borrowed time—when bubbles burst, the only durable value is the human memory of what people sacrificed.
money_quotes: The AI bubble will burst—it’s just that the disappointment won’t be evenly distributed.
Some people get rich, and some people just get dirty.
You can live your life on borrowed money, but there is no such thing as borrowed time.
Sometimes, 996 adds up to generational wealth. Sometimes, it adds up to zero.
When a bubble bursts, and people’s work gets erased and their hours wasted, all that remains is what other people witnessed.
posts.key_insight: The AI gold rush may enrich a few, but for many it will trade irreplaceable time for nothing, so the humane response is to witness and honor those sacrifices.

posts.summary: The post argues that while AI tools make it feel like there has never been a better time to start a startup, the pace of improvement creates a deflationary effect: waiting even a few months can dramatically lower the cost and raise the quality of what you can build. That dynamic turns today’s frontier into tomorrow’s tech debt, making early-built products feel obsolete faster and raising the question of whether starting now is actually optimal.
money_quotes: If all you think about is the tools that are available to you, then today is always a better time to start a company than yesterday, and today will always be worse than tomorrow.
But what if those costs are falling quickly?
Almost immediately, the new one is better. It’s more durable; it’s faster; it’s better designed.
There is no fixed definition of tech debt—it is simply code that the current engineers would prefer to be written differently.
After all, you can rename your company to keep up with the latest fads, but it’s not so easy to rebuild it.
posts.key_insight: Rapidly improving AI tools create a deflationary dynamic in software creation, making each new wave of tooling render recent work obsolete and complicating the idea that now is always the best time to start.

posts.summary: Stancil uses the Jordan Chiles medal dispute to show how the era of rigorous quantification produced elaborate rules and data systems yet still failed to feel fair or objective. He argues that the cultural pendulum has swung from “data-driven” decision-making toward taste, intuition, and AI-mediated “vibes,” and that this shift is self-reinforcing as tools and organizations adapt to what people find most accessible.
money_quotes: In data we trusted; now, God is in the vibes.
If AI is good at anything, it is good at interpreting the vibes.
As, uh, Stalin once said, “quantity has a quality all its own.”
A generation grew up reading The Signal and the Noise and Thinking Fast and Slow, and bludgeoned the prior generation to death with models and quantitative rhetoric and complex codes of points.
They are vibe native.
posts.key_insight: The culture that once prized quantified rigor is yielding to taste and AI-mediated intuition because “vibes” are faster, more compelling, and increasingly easy to operationalize.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that engagement-optimized platforms corrupt their own purposes, and OpenAI now faces the same pull as it prioritizes ChatGPT popularity over research aims. With AI poised to mediate more of daily life, optimizing for attention risks re-creating social media’s addictive, society-shaping harms at a far deeper layer.
money_quotes: It’s Goodhart’s law for social media: When a good becomes a metric, it ceases to be good.
We know that this type of internet—one dialed to optimize engagement—can tear us apart in thousands of ways.
When you commit to spending $1.4 trillion over the next eight years, as OpenAI has, making enough money to survive means making a lot of money.
AI is surely becoming a new invisible hand pulling the levers in our minds.
That’s the world our metrics made—one in which we don’t sing and dance when the music is being played, but take pictures of ourselves instead.
posts.key_insight: Optimizing AI products for engagement under immense financial pressure risks turning a transformative technology into a more addictive, socially corrosive version of social media.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that many complex organizational data problems don’t need elaborate ontologies or decision models; they often just need people to write down what happened and why. With modern LLMs, large corpora of unstructured text can be more valuable and scalable than brittle, human-centric schemas, making the “text box” approach both pragmatic and powerful.
money_quotes: But the text box was probably the most important part.
Ok, sure, I don’t know, but—maybe we should start with a text box?
We want to organize information the way we organize it in our head; we want to solve problems the way we reason through them ourselves.
Which one would you trust more: Our ability to model how 1,000 people collectively think, or a state-of-the-art AI, looking for patterns in a large corpus of unstructured text?

And the best companies—in this domain, and many others—seem likely be those that embrace that, do the dumb thing—build a text box; collect the data—and convince people to always be writing stuff down in it.
posts.key_insight: The simplest move—capturing raw, human-written context at scale—often beats sophisticated decision models because LLMs can extract structure from unstructured text.

posts.summary: The post argues that AI makes it dangerously tempting to sprint toward new features, grand rebuilds, and ever-larger products, but that path often yields software that feels hollow rather than magical. Real “magic” comes from the unglamorous grind of care, polish, and persistence—work that still requires human attention even when AI can generate code quickly.
money_quotes: It is tempting to open Claude Code—the most popular talked-about app on today’s internet; the new Cursor; the must-have stocking stuffer of this holiday season—and YOLO-mode an expansive new feature into your product.
If everyone can build their own made-to-measure apps—decentralized apps; personal apps; custom-designed, one-of-a-kind bespoke apps—there is no market for small conveniences or narrow delights.
“Magic is just someone spending more time on something than anyone else might reasonably expect.”
AI gives software developers—and anyone creating things, for that matter—a relentless workforce that is uncannily good at tearing through new frontiers.
Because for something to be better than people believe it could be, we still have to spend more time on it than anyone would reasonably expect—even, often, ourselves.
posts.key_insight: AI can accelerate creation, but truly great software still requires sustained human care and time well beyond what feels reasonable.

posts.summary: The post argues that tools like Claude Code succeed partly because we don’t care how machine-written code reads, only that it works, but that logic fails for everyday work like emails and decks where personal voice and representation matter. As AI agents spread, the likely shift isn’t toward more collaboration but toward indirect, “confederated” work through shared machine-readable repositories, which changes how we communicate and judge one another.
money_quotes: Code is meant to be run, not read.
Writing an email may be a lot simpler than writing code, but it is not easier, because only emails need to contain me.
Why collaborate when you can add context?
Why wouldn’t the map become the territory?
It is confederated work. Or Cowork, for short.
posts.key_insight: AI can replace collaboration only where human voice doesn’t matter, pushing work toward machine-mediated context rather than direct communication.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that as AI shifts software work from writing code to “vibe and verify,” data work hits a harder wall because analysis can’t be validated by clicking around an app—verification still demands reading SQL or recreating results. The old tradeoff between write-friendly and read-friendly SQL now matters more than ever, creating a need for new representations that make queries legible—diagrams, explainers, or languages aimed at comprehension rather than generation.
key_points: The data industry’s old debates (org structure, commas in SQL) masked a real tension between code that is easy to write and code that is easy to read.
AI accelerates code generation, but analytics lacks a simple proxy for correctness; verification remains laborious.
Natural-language explanations of SQL are insufficient because query details are too precise for prose.
A new tooling layer should translate arbitrary SQL into human-readable visual or annotated forms.
Language ergonomics matter less in an agentic world; build/runtime/packaging advantages may dominate.
money_quotes: That reading and writing are not the same thing—and, especially, reading and writing code are not the same thing.
In about a year, engineers went from mostly writing code, to reading code, to just testing code. Write and read, to vibe and verify.
The far bigger problem, however, seems to be that there’s no way to know if the work is right.
We need the opposite: Something that turns an arbitrary query into an accessible diagram.
I want to ask a robot to write me any query I can think of, and a picture and some words that tell me how the big computer did numbers.
posts.key_insight: AI can generate analytical code quickly, but until we have tools that make SQL easy to read and verify, analytics cannot fully join the “vibe and verify” revolution.

posts.summary: Stancil uses Gas Town — Steve Yegge's real, intentionally extreme AI coding framework built on swarms of Claude Code agents — as a mirror for Silicon Valley's current frenzy of vibe coding, startup mania, and existential anxiety. He then pivots sharply to ICE raids in Minneapolis and the shooting of Alex Peretti, contrasting the tech world's obsession with speed and output against the quiet, unglamorous courage of neighbors protecting each other.
key_points: Gas Town is a real AI development framework by Steve Yegge that spawns swarms of competing agents to write code — chaotic, wasteful, and fast.
Silicon Valley's current AI-fueled startup rush mirrors Gas Town's chaos: everyone is building frantically, but most output will be waste and most startups will burn through compute and die.
The essay pivots to ICE raids in Minneapolis, where thousands of residents took to the streets to protect their neighbors — a stark contrast to tech's self-absorbed urgency.
Stancil questions whether building fast and swinging big actually matters compared to showing up for your community when it counts.
money_quotes: Gas Town is AI agents talking to AI agents talking to AI agents.
There are agents everywhere; Gas Town is up to its elbows in agents; Gas Town is overrun with agents.
In Gas Town, waste is a mathematical necessity.
Most startups will incinerate themselves inside of an Anthropic data center.
I have written posts about both. Only one is about regret.
posts.key_insight: The frantic energy of AI-driven building is indistinguishable from Gas Town's industrial chaos, and none of it matters as much as the unglamorous, dangerous work of showing up for your neighbors.

posts.summary: Benn argues that AI has quietly surpassed human reasoning ability in most practical domains, and we haven't truly reckoned with this shift. What started as 'AI is like an intern' has inverted: planning mode and other AI features are subtly steering us, not the other way around. The future won't be one of helpful assistants doing our homework—it will be something stranger, as reasoning ceases to be humanity's competitive advantage.
key_points: The 'AI as intern' framing has become outdated as AI capabilities have surpassed human reasoning in many domains
Planning mode represents an inversion of control where AI prompts humans rather than the reverse
AI's occasional bizarre errors create an availability bias that masks its overall superior performance
Humans are left with opinions, context, and hands as their remaining competitive advantages
The future won't simply be AI as helpful assistants—that's just what we've instructed so far
money_quotes: It's better than me at most things, and I don't know how to keep up. I rarely have better ideas than Claude. I rarely can solve a problem that Gemini can't.
More and more, reasoning is not our competitive advantage. All we have is opinions, the context of what is in our heads, and hands.
Planning mode is an inversion of control. Planning mode is how the AI agent prompts you.

AI is mechanically useful because it does stuff for us, and that is what we usually talk about. But its emotionally intoxicating power—its real delight, or its real danger—is that it decides stuff for us.
To assume that we'll be fine is not the same as assuming we'll be fine in the way we were fine before. It may be better. It may be worse. It may just be weirder.
posts.key_insight: We've been comforting ourselves with the 'AI as intern' metaphor, but the inversion has already happened—AI is increasingly the one with better ideas, and we haven't begun to grapple with what it means when reasoning is no longer our competitive advantage.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the biggest AI product winners have consistently been those willing to take reckless, 'just try it' approaches rather than cautious, responsible ones. He suggests the data analytics world is ripe for someone to build an unhinged AI product that unleashes autonomous agents to find insights, and that this irresponsible approach will likely beat the careful, governed alternatives—just as ChatGPT beat Google, Claude Code beat Copilot, and other bold bets outpaced their cautious competitors.
key_points: Every major AI product breakthrough came from someone saying 'eh, why not?' while incumbents worried about doing things responsibly
Data analysts are barely adopting AI tools compared to software engineers, possibly because their work is harder to automate—or because the tools are too cautious

The pattern repeats: Google was too scared to ship a chatbot, so OpenAI did; coding tools asked for permission, so Anthropic shipped autonomous coding; responsible AI assistants gated access, so someone built one with access to everything
The responsible approach to AI in data—governed, audited, trusted insights—may lose to the reckless one that just unleashes hundreds of AI agents to find profitable insights
As software creation costs approach zero, the winning approach may be one crazy person willing to try something wild, not a disciplined team doing things the right way
money_quotes: When you're on the inside, you forget that most people don't care about the details that you do. You spent your life carefully researching AI safety inside of a cleanroom at Google; how could the public ever want to use a chatbot that doesn't meet your exacting standards?

I once asked people how often in their careers they found a truly meaningful 'insight' in their data. The average answer was once every two years—or, if measured by an analyst's salary, once every few hundred thousand dollars. How many Gas Towns of Claudes could you run with that?

Is it the AI agent that's optimized to oh-so-precisely answer mundane questions like, 'How many shirts did we sell last week?' over and over again via a Slack integration? Or is it a battalion of Codexes and Claudes that are all told to relentlessly and recklessly find ways to make more money?
The right way and winning way aren't necessarily the same thing. And maybe the future of software is stuff that's made by one person who was willing to try something crazy.
posts.key_insight: In AI products, reckless boldness has consistently beaten responsible caution, and the data analytics industry is likely next in line for this pattern to repeat.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that regardless of whether AI is truly reaching a technical singularity, we are already experiencing a social takeoff — a collective psychosis where AI dominates every conversation, every news cycle, and every corporate decision. The frenzy of model releases, billion-dollar fundraises, and apocalyptic predictions has created a vortex that has already conquered our attention, making the question of whether AI is truly transformative almost beside the point.
key_points: The pace of AI developments in February 2026 alone is staggering — dozens of major model releases, hundred-billion-dollar funding rounds, and existential proclamations
The concept of 'vibecession' applies to AI: collective belief in AI's transformative power may matter as much as its actual capabilities
The internet discourse around AI has become all-consuming, with Hacker News AI threads dwarfing even the most significant non-AI stories
There is a distinction between technical takeoff (AI improving itself exponentially) and social takeoff (AI conquering human attention and belief systems)
The frenzy includes everyone now — not just grifters and rubes, but ordinary people at coffee shops and non-profit leaders
money_quotes: There is also a social takeoff—when the world feels like it's pinning us against our seats; when the machines conquer our attention; when we begin to believe, rightly or wrongly, in their blistering power; when the rattling starts.

They say the internet is dead, full of robots talking to one another. On the contrary—it is furiously, psychotically alive. It is a vortex of this new psychosis, tightening around a single axel, spinning faster and faster as it does.

If every Anthropic press release is all we talk about, have the robots not already taken over? If every company is urgently rearranging itself around a workforce of agents, does it matter how well they score on the tests?
Is this takeoff, or just takes?
Feel the AGI. But is that the AI, or is it us?
posts.key_insight: The AI revolution may already be here not because the technology has achieved superintelligence, but because it has achieved total dominance over our collective attention and anxiety — a social takeoff that renders the technical question almost moot.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that AI making software cheaper to build won't commoditize it, because human expectations are never static — 'good enough' is always redefined by what's possible. Just as the internet didn't prevent DoorDash from winning food delivery despite low barriers to entry, someone will always figure out how to be the best, and being the best remains hard. He also notes that AI companies' real competition is for talent, not customers, which is reshaping corporate ethics positioning.
key_points: The 'death of software' narrative assumes customer satisfaction is static, but expectations constantly rise with new possibilities
Every technological revolution that lowered barriers to entry still produced dominant winners — the internet didn't commoditize food delivery
AI may change how software is built but creates more opportunities for differentiation, not fewer
AI companies' true competitive resource is researcher talent, not revenue, inverting the normal business model
Anthropic's ethical positioning on autonomous weapons and surveillance doubles as a potent recruiting strategy
money_quotes: Give us something new; we love it today; we are frustrated tomorrow. We spent millennia dreaming that we could fly; now we can, and we whine about the wifi.
Sure, success may be on the far side of a threshold — but that threshold is not static.
Someone will be the best at that new thing, and being the best at anything is almost tautologically hard to be.
The 'cost of creating content going to zero' didn't kill content, nor did it bankrupt the business of content creation.
Your plan for market domination is not to hire people and then make money from what they build; it is to be the first company that creates an AI model that is good enough to improve itself.
posts.key_insight: AI lowering the cost of building software doesn't commoditize it — it just resets the competition, because human dissatisfaction ensures that 'good enough' is always a moving target and being the best remains as hard as ever.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that the real danger of AI-powered surveillance isn't sophisticated spy technology but the automation of tedious data analysis that was previously too boring for anyone to bother doing. Drawing from his experience as a data analyst at an enterprise social network, he shows that our digital lives have always been thoroughly tracked—the only thing protecting our privacy was that analyzing the data required too much grunt work. AI removes that friction, making mass surveillance not a sci-fi scenario but a mundane inevitability.
key_points: All SaaS products already collect exhaustive behavioral data using standard tracking libraries—surveillance infrastructure has existed for years
Privacy has been protected not by encryption or security but by the tedium of analyzing messy, scattered data
AI's real threat to privacy isn't superintelligent pattern detection but automating the boring grunt work of data analysis at scale
Government agencies buy surveillance data on open markets that anyone else can also access
The assumption that nobody would bother watching us—the 'banality' defense—is collapsing as AI makes watching effortless
money_quotes: On an internet where everything is tracked—and man, everything is tracked—surveillance does not require a Ph.D., or even any particularly advanced math. It just requires a junior analyst with 24 hours of free time.
The real fences around the data all we leave behind—and the real protections of our privacy—are neither tall nor covered in barbed wire. They are simply fences that are annoying to climb.

Not of AI becoming a superintelligent Sherlock Holmes finding impossible patterns in its enormous mind palace, but of it being a million monkeys at a million typewriters, doing the grunt work no person wanted to do.
Because when prying questions are a prompt away—rather than 24 hours of work away—who wouldn't get tempted to pry?
Banality is a sturdy armor. Or was, anyway.
posts.key_insight: AI doesn't need to be superintelligent to destroy privacy—it just needs to automate the tedious data work that was the only real barrier between our tracked lives and anyone curious enough to look.

posts.summary: Benn Stancil argues that human decision-making—even on consequential matters—is increasingly driven by personal allegiances, personality conflicts, and tribal pettiness rather than rational self-interest. He traces this pattern from sports fandom through partisan economic perception all the way to the Pentagon's AI contract decisions being shaped by personal feuds between tech CEOs. He concludes that in a world where everything is gamified and 'the self is the platform,' treating pettiness as a legitimate input to decision-making may no longer be irrational.
key_points: Political partisans consistently rate economic conditions better when their party is in power, regardless of actual conditions—suggesting economic sentiment is driven by team allegiance, not material reality.
The Pentagon's AI contract dispute between Anthropic and OpenAI was substantially shaped by personal animosity between executives rather than policy substance.
As celebrity CEOs, influencers, and personal brands replace faceless institutions, the line between rational preference and fandom-style loyalty increasingly dissolves.
Kahneman's Nobel-winning work on irrational decision-making may need an update: pettiness and personality may be rational inputs in a world where everything is a game of chosen allegiances.
money_quotes: In matters of real importance—in war; in questions of life and death—we're not like this.
We're all influencers now; the self is the platform.
When you believe in nothing, should pettiness not be part of your utility curve?
Our perceptions of the country are more defined more by the person in charge of the country than by the country itself.

When everything is a game to gamble on—sports are gambling; financial markets are gambling; war is gambling; everything is gambling—should we be surprised when we start choosing our favorites in the same way we choose our fandoms?
posts.key_insight: Human decision-making at every level—from sports to politics to military AI contracts—is increasingly governed by personal allegiance and pettiness, and in a world where everything is performance and personality, that may no longer be irrational.
